experiments.title: How adding value prop to the donate button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop to the donate button on Care Net’s main donation page will achieve more donations because it will encourage users to click the donate button. It will remind them why they are giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2025 - 12/08/2025
Care Net’s donation pages typically have a donate button that says “Give Now” under that it says “Amount: $XX.” We were interested in seeing what would happen if we replaced the “Amount: $XX” with additional value prop. We replaced it with “I want to protect life.” We ran this experiment on their main donation page because that’s where they see the majority of their traffic and we knew this test would have a better chance of validating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated the powerful impact of mission-driven language on donor behavior. By replacing the default “Amount: $XX” label on Care Net’s highest-traffic donation page with a value-based statement — “I want to protect life” — the test reframed the giving decision from a financial transaction to a personal expression of identity and belief. The treatment produced a 44% lift in conversion rate, increasing from 18.7% to 27.0%, with 99.3% statistical confidence, confirming that the effect was both meaningful and reliable.
This result suggests that donors are more likely to complete a gift when the call to action connects directly to their values and sense of purpose, rather than focusing on dollar amounts or mechanics. In essence, emphasizing the “why” of giving at the point of decision appears to activate emotional motivation and a sense of alignment with the organization’s mission. For Care Net, this insight reinforces the importance of using identity-based and mission-oriented framing not just in broader messaging but in microcopy across key conversion points. Future tests could explore how different variations of purpose-driven language—tailored to distinct donor motivations—further influence giving behavior.

experiments.title: Will longer copy help donors convert on a lower third slide up?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more copy value proposition for the lower third slide up will achieve more transactions because the additional value prop will provide social proof and clarity into what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2025
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that is a well recognized brand. They drive tons of traffic to their site daily.
Right now, they are in the middle of a sustainer drive. To support this campaign we added what we call a ‘lower third’ slideup element to their content pages. We wanted to see if long or short copy worked best on the these pages to convert donors. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Long copy, which includes a value proposition for the lower third slideup, was more effective in converting donors compared to short copy.

2. The additional value proposition provided social proof and clarity, which likely contributed to the higher donation rate.

3. The treatment resulted in a significant increase in donations, with a 98.3% level of confidence that the results were not due to random chance.

In future experimentation, we should:

1. Continue testing different copy lengths and value propositions to further optimize donation conversions.

2. Consider incorporating social proof and clear explanations of how donations will be used in our messaging.

3. Regularly analyze and track the performance of different elements to identify what resonates best with our audience and drives the most meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How including how many people downloaded impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a subheadline of how many have already downloaded the offer for Avila’s landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because people are influenced to download because they see others have done it too.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 02/05/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding the number of people who have downloaded this offer to the landing page headline would impact email acquisition and donations.
We wanted to see if including “Downloaded by 18,377 others!” in the subheadline would engage others. This is known as social proof marketing or the bandwagon effect, where people are influenced to take action because they see others doing it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the number of people who have downloaded the offer to the landing page headline had a negative impact on email acquisition for Avila Foundation. The treatment, which included the subheadline "Downloaded by 18,377 others!" resulted in a 24.5% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the effects of social proof marketing strategies such as the bandwagon effect. While showing the number of people who have engaged with a particular offer can sometimes increase engagement, in this case, it had the opposite effect. It is crucial to test different variations of social proof messaging and other marketing strategies to understand their impact fully.
People may not have signed up for the offer if the "Downloaded by 18,377 others" didn’t feel relevant to them or it wasn’t clear why they should care. Additionally, if something appears too common or widely available, it can sometimes lose its appeal.
It's important to note that the average gift size in the treatment was $52.50" whereas the control was $13.75. It would be interesting to dig in to see if this was based upon one large gift or if it was a trend.
It is essential to continue using A/B testing and data analysis to make informed decisions about website optimizations and marketing strategies. This experiment demonstrates the importance of testing hypotheses and not assuming that a particular approach will always yield positive results.

experiments.title: How a tooltip on Ways to Help impacts clicks and Gift Card setups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tooltip describing what step one should take for authors will achieve an increase in Gift Card setups because adding that tooltip will give clarity on what the user should do and help reduce confusion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2025 - 01/31/2025
CaringBridge has the option for authors to set up a gift card so that people visiting their page can send the author a gift card as a way to help. For this test, we tried adding a tooltip that focused on what the user should do and specifying what the option is used for.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a tooltip describing what step the user should take for setting up a gift card for authors led to a significant increase in the Save button clicked for gift card setups on the website. The treatment group experienced a 363.9% increase in Save button clicked for gift cards on the What They Heard page for all traffic, with a statistical significance level of 100.0%.
Furthermore, the Tooltip CTA button clicked also saw a significant increase of 1,383.8% with 100% statistical confidence. This indicates that the tooltip providing clarity on what the user should do helped reduce confusion and encourage more users to set up gift cards.
In future experiments, it is important to continue emphasizing the importance of providing clear instructions and guidance for users to navigate the website effectively. This includes using tooltips or other visual cues to assist users in understanding the steps they need to take. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the value of testing different strategies for improving user experience and conversion rates. By continuing to experiment and iterate on website design and functionalities, we can further optimize the user experience and achieve better results.

experiments.title: How an aligned, value-driven image of an ebook on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using imagery that shows the content of an ebook for users visiting an ebook landing page will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because the imagery will showcase the value and ebook content more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/31/2024
During the Christmas season, Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) was running a devotional ebook acquisition offer. When running an offer like this, the landing page will typically feature a 3D mockup image of the ebook cover to help users on the page understand what they are receiving.
For this devotional offer, the ads on Facebook used a “scattered pages” design to showcase some of the unique offerings inside the ebook (devotions, Bible verses, crossword puzzles, etc.). We wondered if using this imagery on the devotional ebook landing page would help to enhance the value proposition and increase conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test for 29 days, with 50% of landing page traffic seeing the image of the 3D ebook, and the other 50% of traffic seeing the “scattered pages” design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing imagery that shows the content of an ebook on a landing page can significantly increase email sign-ups. In this case, the treatment version, which featured an image showcasing some of the unique offerings inside the ebook, led to a 35.5% increase in emails acquired compared to the control version that only showed the 3D ebook cover. This lift was at a 99.2% LOC, which means that the result is considered statistically valid.

In future experiments, it is important to continue testing imagery on the landing page that effectively showcases the value and content of the content offer. This can help capture the attention of users and communicate the benefits of the ebook more clearly, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates.

Further testing should also be done to determine the impact of aligning the imagery on the landing page with any imagery that users are seeing at the front of the funnel through ads, emails, MMS, etc.

experiments.title: How removing specificity from a direct ask donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate because donors may feel less emotionally connected or personally invested in a generalized ask, reducing their sense of urgency and clarity about the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2024 - 01/04/2025
The IMB is running a donation direct ask offer through paid Facebook advertising asking prospects to give to the IMB to fund Bibles being provided to different people groups around the world.
Due to issues of the gift designation, the IMB removed language about gift specificity to allow all donations to be designated where needed. Because of this, the “after” donation page did not have:
Gift handles specifying the donation amount
Specific language about what donating to this fund does
A goal thermometer
The results below show the conversion rates of donors during the “before the changes” time period compared to the “after the changes” time period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rate. The "after the changes" group experienced a 92.6% decrease in donations compared to the "before the changes" group. This difference was statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a clear impact of the changes made.

It is important to note, that our results from this direct ask offer went from the cheapest cost per donor ($206 GCPD) in the most expensive fundraising period ($17.33 CPM) to the most costly cost per donor ($384.17) in the least expensive fundraising period ($2.08). Meaning in the "before the changes" time period, it was costing the IMB $17.33 to show their ads to 1,000 people, receiving a 1.3% instant conversion rate, resulting in a $206 cost per donor. Whereas in the "after the changes" time period, it has cost the IMB only $2.08 to show their ads to 1,000 people. This should mean that our results are dramatically better! But with the new page, the conversion rate has tanked to 0.10%, resulting in an average cost per donor of $384.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the emotional connection and sense of urgency that donors feel when making a donation. Providing clarity about the impact of their gift and giving them a sense of ownership over where their donation is designated can lead to higher conversion rates. Therefore, it is important to communicate the specific goals and impact of the donation fund in order to motivate donors to contribute. Additionally, setting clear expectations and goals for fundraising campaigns, such as using a goal thermometer, can help create a sense of urgency and encourage donors to take action.

experiments.title: How a donor goal and exclusivity language in a high-urgency email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a donor goal and exclusivity language for Reason to Believe’s housefile appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it leverages that the donor is part of a special group, and breaks the goal into something they can visualize their impact upon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2024 - 12/03/2024
This year, rather than celebrating Giving Tuesday, Reasons to Believe decided to launch the organization’s very first RTB Giving Day. This was a high-urgency appeal that would last only one-day, with the goal of raising $15,000. We hypothesized that translating this to a 180 donor goal rather than a revenue goal, would increase donor conversion rate for two reasons. First, we believed that it would help underscore these donors as being part of a special group — donors to the first RTB Giving Day; second, we believed that this would help break the more intimidating $15,000 goal into something manageable that the donor could more easily see their own impact in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment illustrate that the use of a donor goal and exclusivity language in the appeal significantly increased the donor conversion rate for Reasons to Believe's RTB Giving Day campaign. This approach helped emphasize the donors' participation in a special group and made the donation goal more manageable and tangible for the donors. The treatment group, which received the donor goal and exclusivity language, saw a 54.0% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence (95.3%).
In future experimentation, we should consider the context around a high-urgency goal and the donor's place in them. The amount of time communicated to reach the goal and perceived difficulty of reaching the goal can all be things that potentially discourage the donor from understanding their impact rather than communicating it. Within the context of a 24-hour appeal and the efforts of RTB to start something unique, it appears that speaking directly to the donors needed to accomplish this was most effective.

experiments.title: Will expanding value proposition on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the main episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion because it will be able to better prime the user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/01/2024
The main episodic donation page has always converted donors really well with little to no value proposition copy. Over the last several years we have proposed and executed sever tests on the page and still, nothing will perform better than the little to no copy that they have on the page natively.
For this test, we wanted to see if somebody who is not involved in the Focus on the Family account could come in with fresh eyes and see where this page could see improvements.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition copy to the main episodic donation page for Focus on the Family actually led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which received the new value proposition copy, saw an 80.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, Transactions - One-Time experienced a 796% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that sometimes less is more when it comes to donation pages. In this case, the original page with minimal to no value proposition copy performed better in converting donors. It's crucial to continually test and iterate on different elements of the page to find the most effective combination for donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on refining other aspects of the page, such as design, layout, and call-to-action buttons, while keeping in mind the impact of adding additional copy.

experiments.title: How does amping Value Prop (clarity, credibility, appeal and exclusivity) impact email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that amping up the elements of a value prop for email subscribers will achieve a higher engagement with the email because the merits of Dr. Jeffress outweight the merits of the book alone.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month Pathway to Victory promote a different book. For October 2024, PTV is promoting its popular book Final Conquest.
For this month, the offer is not performing as well as the past so we decided to ramp up some test to unlock more engagement and therefore donors.
Typically PTV emails focus on the book, its value, the unique design, the topic that is covered etc. So with this experiment we decided to test into a different approach of highly Dr. Jeffress education and credibility as a pastor for many years.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that emphasizing Dr. Jeffress' education and credibility as a pastor in the email content led to a significantly higher engagement rate from subscribers. By highlighting Dr. Jeffress' background and expertise, subscribers were more inclined to click on the email and potentially engage with the content.

In addition to the high engagement we saw

experiments.title: How a pop-up inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion on donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting one-time donors to convert their one-time donation to a recurring gift will have a more significant impact and will increase recurring giving by making the value proposition of a recurring gift more appealing and transparent to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 07/31/2024
This organization uses a tabbed donation form to enable donors to select either a one-time or a recurring donation. Last year, they tested adding a callout to the recurring tab to highlight the greater impact of becoming a recurring donor. This validated an increase in recurring giving and became a part of all their donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could further increase recurring giving on the page by firing a popup when someone clicks the donate button on the form. This popup would invite donors to give a smaller recurring gift to have a bigger impact than the higher one-time donation they had selected. The form would automatically ask for a gift that was 75% of the value of the one-time donation originally chosen.
We ran this experiment on instant donation pages and the donation pages of their one-time donor-focused July Campaign. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The popup inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to a recurring gift significantly increased recurring donations by 129.4% for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.6%). This indicates that emphasizing the value proposition of recurring donations can effectively incentivize donors to choose ongoing giving.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to highlight the benefits of recurring donations and nudge donors toward choosing this option. Additionally, leveraging popups strategically to interrupt the donation process and present compelling offers can be a powerful tool in increasing donor engagement and conversion rates.

Most importantly, we found that the popup had no statistically significant impact on overall donor conversion, so we were not sacrificing one-time donations by running the popup. Because of the significantly higher retention rate and lifetime value of recurring donors, this increase in recurring giving could have significant impacts on donor revenue for this organization.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to enhance authority impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal, clarity, and authority through a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
In a previous experiment, we had seen that adding a personal quote from the professor on the course registration page increased sign-ups. We wanted to test this same hypothesis on additional courses to see if this is something that was specific to the previous course or specific quote or if this was a global concept that could be rolled out on all courses taught by the seminary’s president. For this course, we added a brief testimonial and a photo of the professor so potential course registrants can get a glimpse of additional clarity about who is teaching the course and how having social proof from a figure of authority would motivate visitors to give away their email address on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered after running the experiment for one month was that the treatment lifted the conversion rate on the acquisition page by 7.4% with 97.2% confidence. Drilling down into specific segments, we did notice a directional decrease in conversion from paid social, which will need to be addressed. However, on its face we believe this treatment layout makes the course more appealing by humanizing the offer. Since we have now seen similar treatments provide lifts on various courses regardless of the course-specific testimonial content, we will add this feature to the existing layout of the course registration pages for those that are taught by the seminary president.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a daily devotional subscription impacts email conversion on the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe strengthening the appeal and exclusivity of a daily devotional subscription will increase the email conversion on its landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2023 - 08/07/2023
For Bible League Canada’s Words from the Heart daily devotional, we felt the Value Proposition was weaker than it should be. Particularly in the exclusivity and appeal it conveyed. The control page seemed to assume that visitors would already believe that they should be reading their Bibles each day, instead of reminding them why daily Bible reading may be important.
We hypothesized that the landing page’s value proposition could be strengthened by first articulating why a daily devotional is important and then explaining how this short, exclusive devotional would enable subscribers to experience the peace and presence of Christ. To do this, we added a scripture reference that encouraged believers to daily meditate on God’s Word. Then, we emphasized the negative things that can happen when believers don’t take time to follow this verse’s instruction and meditate on Scripture. Finally, we emphasized three separate times in the copy that this was an “exclusive 3-minute daily devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After just one full week, we were able to validate the impact of this strengthened value proposition. This test was a reminder that we shouldn't assume that our audience already understands the full value of an offer. Instead, we should take time to reinforce the need the offer meets as well as the exclusive ways it will benefit users. We will continue to test the value proposition on our other landing pages to ensure that we are providing greater appeal and exclusivity to visitors.

experiments.title: How providing additional value prop on a content offer impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more value prop in the ad copy for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is additional appeal and clarity on the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV utilizes Facebook as a platform to acquire new names and donors for their organization.
One offer that has been a consistently well-performing content offer is their Jesus Map digital download.
The current version of the ad has been running for multiple years and had not been optimized.
We decided to test out expanding the copy so that there is more information regarding the offer and the reasons to request it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Including more value proposition in the ad copy is effective**: The treatment with expanded ad copy led to a significant increase in Complete Registrations, Clicks, and improvements in Cost per Click and Cost per Registration. This indicates that providing more information and clarity on the offer can attract more viewers and increase conversions.

2. **Audience segmentation is crucial**: The experiment revealed significant increases in Complete Registrations across different visitor segments, such as 65+ visitors, Female visitors, Mobile visitors, and Tablet visitors. This highlights the importance of tailoring messaging and targeting specific audience segments to maximize engagement and conversions.

3. **Statistical significance and confidence levels**: The high confidence levels, especially at 100% for Clicks, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration, indicate that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This reinforces the validity and impact of the experiment's findings.

To apply these learnings in future experimentation, we should:

1. **Continue to test and optimize ad copy**: Based on the success of the expanded ad copy in this experiment, we should continue exploring ways to enhance value proposition and clarity in our messaging. This could involve testing different variations of ad copy to identify the most effective messaging strategies.

2. **Segment and target specific audience groups**: Building on the positive outcomes seen across different visitor segments, we should further analyze audience data to understand preferences and behaviors. By tailoring content and targeting specific groups, we can drive higher engagement and conversions.

3. **Maintain statistical rigor in experimentation**: To ensure the reliability of results, we should continue to prioritize statistical significance and confidence levels in future experiments. This will help us make data-driven decisions and confidently implement successful strategies.

Overall, by leveraging the learnings from this experiment, we can optimize our digital marketing efforts, enhance audience engagement, and ultimately drive greater success in acquiring new names and donors for Pathway to Victory.

We decided to test out expanding the copy so that there is more information regarding the offer and the reasons to request it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Including more value proposition in the ad copy is effective**: The treatment with expanded ad copy led to a significant increase in Complete Registrations, Clicks, and improvements in Cost per Click and Cost per Registration. This indicates that providing more information and clarity on the offer can attract more viewers and increase conversions.

2. **Audience segmentation is crucial**: The experiment revealed significant increases in Complete Registrations across different visitor segments, such as 65+ visitors, Female visitors, Mobile visitors, and Tablet visitors. This highlights the importance of tailoring messaging and targeting specific audience segments to maximize engagement and conversions.

3. **Statistical significance and confidence levels**: The high confidence levels, especially at 100% for Clicks, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration, indicate that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This reinforces the validity and impact of the experiment's findings.

To apply these learnings in future experimentation, we should:

1. **Continue to test and optimize ad copy**: Based on the success of the expanded ad copy in this experiment, we should continue exploring ways to enhance value proposition and clarity in our messaging. This could involve testing different variations of ad copy to identify the most effective messaging strategies.

2. **Segment and target specific audience groups**: Building on the positive outcomes seen across different visitor segments, we should further analyze audience data to understand preferences and behaviors. By tailoring content and targeting specific groups, we can drive higher engagement and conversions.

3. **Maintain statistical rigor in experimentation**: To ensure the reliability of results, we should continue to prioritize statistical significance and confidence levels in future experiments. This will help us make data-driven decisions and confidently implement successful strategies.

Overall, by leveraging the learnings from this experiment, we can optimize our digital marketing efforts, enhance audience engagement, and ultimately drive greater success in acquiring new names and donors for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How adding exclusivity and personalization in a subject line impacted email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a highly personalized and exclusive subject line for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because there is a higher perceived value insinuated for the content offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/24/2024
When sending a content offer to the housefile, we wondered if we could increase response and engagement of the message by increasing the perceived value of the offer through adding exclusivity and personalization in the subject line vs. a subject line that merely invoked curiosity around the contents and its potential usefulness or benefit to the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a highly personalized and exclusive subject line for housefile recipients can significantly increase email clicks. By creating a sense of exclusivity and personalization in the subject line, the perceived value of the content offer is increased, prompting more recipients to engage with the email. We saw a 58% increase in click rate at 99% level of confidence so the results of the experiment are valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue utilizing personalized and exclusive language in subject lines to increase engagement with email content and potentially improve overall campaign performance. This could involve tailoring subject lines based on recipient behavior or preferences, creating a sense of VIP access, or offering unique opportunities specifically for housefile recipients. By leveraging the power of personalization and exclusivity, nonprofits like Luther Seminary can effectively communicate with their audience and drive higher levels of engagement with their messaging.

experiments.title: How moving a headline with value proposition on a landing page impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the headline with value proposition up for the landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction. because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/19/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has multiple free online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list.
Evangelism has been a popular course that they have been advertising for years. When looking at all of the landing pages separately, we noticed that some landing pages had headlines “above the fold” (visible before someone has to scroll on the screen) while others did not. Evangelism had no headline above the fold.
In a follow up to this test, we decided to test again moving the headline with value proposition up higher on the landing page. One thing we did do differently this time is that the only thing we changed was the placement. We did not add any additional language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the headline with the value proposition up on the landing page led to a significant increase in email conversions for Dallas Theological Seminary's Evangelism course. This change increased clarity for visitors about what they would learn from the course, ultimately reducing friction and prompting more sign-ups.

In the future, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider the importance of positioning critical information, such as a value proposition, prominently on their landing pages to improve conversion rates. This experiment underscores the impact that small changes in layout can have on user behavior and reinforces the significance of clarity and reducing friction in driving conversions. Building on this learning, future experimentation should continue to focus on optimizing the placement of key elements on landing pages to maximize engagement and drive desired actions from visitors.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a landing page headline impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the value proposition up higher for the course landing page will achieve an increase in course sign ups because it increases the clarity of what the reader will get out of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/26/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has a number of online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list. Their latest course on 1 & 2 Peter was experiencing a low sign-up rate.
After looking at all of their most popular courses, we uncovered inconsistencies in the headlines. 1 & 2 Peter named the course at the top of the page, but after scrolling, there is a secondary headline featuring copy that appeared to have a much stronger value proposition, explaining what the reader will get if they sign up for the course.
So for this test, we decided to switch the headlines to see if it would impact overall course sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the value proposition higher on the course landing page resulted in a significant increase in course sign-ups. The treatment group, which had the value proposition moved up, saw a 32.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.6%.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the placement of the value proposition plays a crucial role in attracting potential learners and convincing them to sign up for the course. Moving forward, it is recommended to prioritize highlighting the benefits and value of the course at the top of the page to increase clarity and remove any potential friction that may prevent sign-ups.
Further experimentation can be done to optimize the messaging and design of the value proposition. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the value proposition to determine the most effective wording or format that resonates with the target audience. Continuous A/B testing can help refine and improve the course landing page to maximize email acquisition and overall conversions.

experiments.title: How using organization credibility language on a survey page affects email sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page will increase the percentage of survey takers because it will provide a sense of credibility and authority for visitors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2023
ACI is a conservative organization that is guided by the four cornerstones: life, liberty, faith and community. They were running a survey to address the state of religious liberty in America and were driving paid Facebook traffic toward this offer. Once the user completes the survey, they will be brought to a donation page asking them to donate to support the organization and their efforts.
In this experiment, we wanted to test how adding more language around who Dr. Ben Carson is and what ACI is doing to protect individual liberties would affect the survey completion rate. The additional language gives more context about how the survey relates to the goals of ACI and provides more context about Dr. Ben Carson’s beliefs. Rather than asking the user directly if they are concerned about religious liberty in this country, we added language that allows the user to align their beliefs with both ACI and Dr. Ben Carson.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page did not increase the percentage of survey takers. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease of 10.9% in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence compared to the control group.
This suggests that providing additional information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson may not necessarily enhance credibility and authority for visitors. It is possible that the additional information may have overwhelmed or distracted the visitors, leading to a lower completion rate.
In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to focus on streamlining the landing page and making the survey process more straightforward and engaging. Rather than relying on extensive background information, it may be more effective to highlight the value and relevance of the survey to the visitors, and clearly communicate the purpose and potential impact of their participation.
Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the qualitative feedback from the survey takers to identify any specific pain points or areas of improvement in the survey experience. This can help refine the survey process and increase completion rates in future experiments.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership for existing members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what existing members will lose by not renewing their membership can be a powerful motivator for donations. The treatment that mentioned the potential loss saw a 41.9% increase in donations compared to the control group with a 99.4% level of confidence.
This suggests that emphasizing the negative consequences of not renewing membership can be more effective than highlighting the positive benefits or incentives of renewing. It taps into the fear of missing out and the desire to avoid losing something valuable.
To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different variations of messaging that focus on the potential loss. This could include highlighting specific benefits or privileges that members would no longer have access to, such as exclusive content, events, or discounts. It may also be effective to use personalized messaging that reminds individuals of the specific contributions they have made and the impact they have had as a member.
Overall, the key takeaway is to leverage the fear of loss as a motivator in membership renewal campaigns. By highlighting what individuals will miss out on if they don't renew, nonprofits like The Heritage Foundation can increase the likelihood of conversions and maintain their supporter base.

experiments.title: How a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition impacts clicks to purchase
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition will increase clicks to purchase by increasing the perceived value of the product
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/05/2023
An organization was launching a brand new streaming series called RVL Discipleship: The Study. Unlike their traditional video series’ acquisition offers that are free with an instant donation opportunity, this would be a product sold through the Focus on the Family store.
The funnel at launch included a product landing page that pointed to the store page that pointed to the cart and checkout pages. The primary purpose of the product landing page was to increase interest in the streaming series and get visitors to click the button to purchase the series.
In reviewing the page, our team noticed that it led with a promo trailer and then offered a free episode. While there was some value proposition copy on the page, it was further down the page and overall, the user experience was very different than the experience of someone on one of their video series’ landing pages that we’ve refined through testing during the last three years. Instead of focusing on the product, there seemed to be almost as much time focused on explaining who the Ray Vander Laan was. We hypothesized that a treatment page that looked more like their video series’ landing pages, with a clear value-driven headline at the top and copy explaining the impact of the series would lead to more purchase clicks than the current design because it would provide greater clarity about the product and its impact on buyers.
We designed a new version of the page and used VWO to split traffic between both versions. We measured clicks to purchase on each and ran the experiment for about 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Simplifying the product landing page and leading with a clear value proposition can increase clicks to purchase.

2. The treatment page had a 47.2% increase in visits to the RVL Store Page for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 100.0%, indicating that the results are highly reliable.

4. By emphasizing the value and impact of the product, potential buyers are more likely to be interested and motivated to make a purchase.

Our next experiment will be removing the store page and cart pages from the funnel altogether so that visitors to the new landing page who click to purchase go straight to the store checkout page. We believe this will further remove friction and lead to more purchases of the product.

We did see 13 purchases on the treatment page while only 10 on the control, but our level of confidence for this increase in purchases was only 50%. More time and data would be needed to validate the impact on purchases.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because they understand what we are asking them to do..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2023
Ben Carson’s American Cornerstone Institute was running ads promoting their Little Patriots Curriculum. On review, the ads that had performed best seemed to have a mismatch of creative and text. “Liberty the Eagle” was featured in the image, while the text asked the reader to pledge to teach kids founding principles.
Liberty the Eagle is a character in the Little Patriots curriculum, but that’s only encountered after they sign up — which means at the point of seeing the ad, the reader has no idea who Liberty is. They decided to replace this with a picture of Dr. Carson, a well-known figure. Once they did that, they realized they could change the “sign the pledge” ask to a “statement of belief” ask that is followed by the “sign the pledge” ask.
They launched two copy variants of the new ads to their audiences to see what worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The importance of aligning the creative and text in an ad: The mismatch between the image of "Liberty the Eagle" and the text asking for a pledge to teach kids founding principles resulted in lower clickthrough rates. This suggests that the audience may not have understood the ask or the connection between the image and text.
2. Clarity in the call-to-action: Increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors led to a significant increase in clicks. This emphasizes the importance of clearly communicating the desired action or response from the audience.
3. The significance of statistical confidence: The treatment group showed a 218.6% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This high level of statistical confidence suggests that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
To utilize these learnings in future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the creative and text in ads are aligned and coherent. Additionally, clear and explicit calls-to-action should be employed to improve engagement and conversions. It is also important to continue using statistical analysis to determine the significance of the results and validate the effectiveness of the changes made.

experiments.title: How a more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that direct ask ads for Facebook users will achieve an increase in conversion rate because they already understand the story of the situation and the needs.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2023
IMB was raising money for missionaries in the Holy Land. It was a high-urgency event, and they didn’t know which way of presenting the value proposition would resonate best. They created four versions of the ads:
A story-based approach
A more aggressive version of #1
A needs-based approach
A direct ask
Though they hypothesized that the direct ask would work best, they launched these ads across all audiences to determine which approach was most impactful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads was the most impactful in terms of generating clicks and achieving a high conversion rate. Despite the hypothesis that the direct ask approach would work best, it was important to run the experiment and gather data to confirm this assumption.
Based on the results, it is clear that Facebook users already understand the story and needs, and they respond well to a direct ask for support. This suggests that in future experimentation, the nonprofit should continue using the direct ask approach in their ads, as it has shown to be effective in increasing conversion rates.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different approaches and not relying solely on assumptions or hypotheses. By running experiments and analyzing the results, the nonprofit can make data-informed decisions and optimize their ads for maximum impact.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads resonates well with the audience and should be used in future experimentation and campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a video on a slide-out impacts transactions

rows.name: Treatment - Security Box with Lock and Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-out featuring a video will drive more transactions than a photo. The video adds a personal and human touch, allowing prospects to see and hear directly how their donation will benefit WUNC. This engaging approach is likely to make them more inclined to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2023 - 08/18/2023
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to determine whether a slide-out with a video or a sticky bar with a photo would drive more transactions. Both the slide-out and the sticky bar featured WUNC’s Capitol Bureau Chief, delivering a value proposition on why prospective supporters should contribute to WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out featuring a video outperformed the sticky bar with a photo in terms of driving transactions for WUNC. The video likely added a personal and human touch, making prospects more inclined to contribute. The treatment group saw a significant 107.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 94.2%. Although this result is approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the intended effect.
Moreover, Revenue also experienced a 9% increase with a 98% level of confidence, indicating that the video slide-out had a positive impact on the financial outcome as well.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of different visual elements in driving conversions. The results suggest that incorporating videos with a personal touch can be a successful strategy for engaging potential supporters and ultimately increasing transactions and revenue for WUNC. It would be beneficial to conduct more experiments with varying types of multimedia content and to continue analyzing the impact on key metrics. Additionally, exploring the optimal timing, placement, and design of such visual elements could further enhance their effectiveness in driving desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a homepage donation CTA impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding increased clarity to the value proposition language around the homepage CTA will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page by providing users with a better understanding of why they should make a gift and what their gift will actually do.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2023
CBN is a Christian news, media, and distribution organization with a mission to preach the Gospel and demonstrate God’s love for the people of the world. The homepage of their website features an image with a Call-to-Action (CTA) that encourages readers to make a gift to CBN and support their work to share Christ’s love with the world.
For this test, we wanted to test into the impact of enhancing the value proposition language around this CTA by adding additional clarity, appeal, and credibility. We ran this test with the following variants:
Control: This variant featured a short and simple headline that said “Sharing the Love of Christ Around the World” above the “GIVE NOW” button
Treatment #1: This variant was designed to give readers a better understanding of how CBN shares Christ’s love by explaining that the reader’s gift to CBN will provide humanitarian aid and the Gospel to people. The headline in this variant said “Your gift right now will reach people with humanitarian aid and the Gospel of Jesus”
Treatment #2: Like Treatment #1, this variant added clarity to the headline, but it also added body copy that explained the problem more, starting with “Millions of people still lack access to the Gospel, hot food, and clean water but you can help change that.” It also reassured readers that 100% of their donation would support CBN’s mission. The final change made to this variant was that it would not display the hero image to mobile users. Instead, the headline and CTA would be displayed on a simple colored background to make it easier to read on mobile devices.
Our hypothesis is that the additional clarity provided in Treatment #1 and Treatment #2 would cause more users to click the “GIVE NOW” button and go to the donation page. We theorized that Treatment #2 would perform the best in this experiment because it added the most clarity to the value proposition language and was designed to make that language easier to read for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition language around the Call-to-Action (CTA) on the homepage had a significant impact on user engagement. The treatment variant, which added clarity, appeal, and credibility to the value proposition, resulted in a 111.3% increase in clicks to the donation page.

These findings suggest that users responded positively to the added information about how their gift will be used to provide humanitarian aid and share the Gospel. Additionally, the inclusion of body copy that highlighted the problem and reassured readers about the use of their donation likely increased trust and motivation to give.

In preparing for this experiment, one concern we had was that the length of variation two could create too much friction and that web visitors would instead prefer the shorter copy of version 1. The results however found the opposite. The longer copy provided even more details about the impact of a donor's gift, leading to higher donor conversion. While running this experiment, we began testing these value propositions on the donation page as well. The visitors in those experiments were excluded from this one to keep the tests clean, but we will use those tests to further refine the value proposition provided for donating to CBN.

We attempted to dig into donor conversion on the main donation page, but had an issue with the UTMs being stripped on the traffic coming to the page from this CTA.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 06/23/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Bringing Biblical Truth to a Broken Culture video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the new language almost validated an increase in emails acquired on the page, it led to a significant decrease in downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page. These results conflicted with the results of a similar test we conducted over the same time period on their Winning Your Inner Battles landing page.

It is possible that the new impact language, while making the offer more appealing in general failed to increase the perceived value for the visitors most likely to donate. So, for these subscribers, the original language was better as it left them with the perceived impact they were looking for in the video series that was removed by the new bullet points. More testing is needed to identify the best impact/value language for this offer to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How more user-friendly value proposition copy impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more user friendly value proposition copy for course prospects will achieve an increase in email sign up rate because prospects will be more likely to read the full value proposition and will better understand the value that the course will bring to their lives.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/22/2023
DTS is getting a low email conversion rate through their Galatians online course. In reviewing the existing course sign up page, we wonder if the formatting and org-centric messaging is deterring people from signing up for the course. The existing page includes lengthy paragraphs and unusual spacing, and focusing a lot on the organization rather than the prospect.
Therefore, we built a treatment variant that breaks up the lengthy paragraphs into smaller ones, implements typical spacing, and focuses more on what value the course offers to the prospect rather than why the organization created the course. Let’s see which performs better…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce a lift in course sign up rate like we anticipated. It led to a 57.9% decrease in course signups with a valid 99.9% level of confidence.
Prospects did not respond well to our simplified, more user-focused value proposition copy. With DTS, it seems the user-centric approach over the org-centric approach is not always best. It may be that brand recognition is strong for DTS and users appreciate how DTS discusses why they created a course and what their overall purpose is.
And maybe despite breaking the two lengthy paragraphs into shorter paragraphs, the fact that there were more paragraphs and overall, and lengthier copy turned away a greater number of users. It may be that users were already motivated to sign up for the course, but seeing multiple paragraphs ahead of the sign up form felt like a barrier to sign up.
Overall, there were multiple elements being tested in this experiment. To gain confident results, it's probably more effective to isolate the elements to determine what specifically is leading to a decrease or an increase.

experiments.title: How a clearer value proposition on an acquisition offer landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clearer value proposition on a video series landing page will increase email and donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/16/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
In reviewing their Inner Battles Series we felt that the headline and body copy lacked clarity about the battle the series would help subscribers win. “battle with yourself” was too vague. We proposed copy that would emphasize that this series was for anyone facing a “secret struggle with sin” and struggling with “habits, regrets, and sin” holding them back from the victory that “Jesus wants for them.”
We split the traffic and ran the test. Our hypothesis was this could increase landing page donor conversion and subsequent donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer value proposition did not increase email signups. However, in providing a stronger value for subscribers, it increased their likliehood of donating on the subsequent donation page. We believe that people were better able to identify with the promised benefits of the video series, especially those who were actively carrying "shame or regrets" regarding a "secret struggle with sin." This made them more likely to give as they felt seen and cared for.

Our final LOC was between 87%-95%. We had an error where another experiment was launched in the last 3 days of the campaign on the donation page for this offer that would have impacted donor conversion in this experiment. However, in looking at our results prior to that second test launching, we were seeing increased donations for the treatment experience.

We will next test bullet points outlining the impact of these videos rather than simply their content.

experiments.title: How increased appeal through value proposition and social proof impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increased appeal through value proposition and social proof for potential coursetakers will achieve an increase in course sign ups because potential coursetakers will be inspired by what the course offers them and by the overall popularity of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/08/2023
In an effort to increase course sign ups through their Revelations course registration page, DTS has developed a treatment to communicate enhanced appeal for the offer and leverage social proof.
For the treatment, we’re refocusing the value proposition on the potential course taker rather than the organization and the course professor. We want to test the angle of “what can this course do for me” or “what can I get out of this course”. And we also want to leverage social proof to see if joining “one of our most popular courses” or “30,000 people who have already enrolled” will motivate people to take the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our combination of increased appeal and social proof produced a valid 62.7% decrease in course sign ups during a time span of only 13 days.
It seems this approach was not well-received by our audience, as we saw a decrease in all segments and down-funnel at the donations and revenue levels as well.
Perhaps our audience does appreciate the background of the professor of the course and learning about the organization's purpose for their courses as they get ready to sign up – more so than learning what they may get out of this specific course. Additionally, social proof may not move the needle if someone is interested in the course from a personal education standpoint rather than a "popularity" stand point.
Ultimately, we may want to test different value proposition that better compliments the course instructor and organization's mission instead of taking an entirely different angle like we did in this experiment. We might also test different types of social proof such as testimonials instead of popularity call outs.
We can isolate these elements to get better direction for future tests.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Value proposition on the monthly donate tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2022 - 11/04/2022
An organization that uses online content and breaking-news stories to engage with visitors wanted to test ways of acquiring more monthly donors on their main donation page.
They decided to test the impact of adding a callout above the “monthly donation tab” on the form with an additional value proposition for making a monthly gift.
They tested this approach on the main donation page (this experiment) and also on a campaign donation page they used in the October recurring donor campaign. The results of that experiment are recorded separately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, they achieved an 11% increase in donor conversion with an 88% Level of Confidence. They were able to validate a 57% increase in donor conversion for returning visitors to the site. Other key segments with only the exception of direct traffic all showed directional lifts in conversion.

Our hypothesis is that for most visitors to the page, the extra value propositional copy increases the perceived value of becoming a donor and thus increases donor conversion. Interestingly enough, we were not able to validate an impact on monthly giving from the callout. It seems that saying that a monthly gift "saves lives 365 days a year" reminded visitors that a onetime gift saves lives as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
All Traffic visitors had an 11.0% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 48.6% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 1,025.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 21.1% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 100.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding value proposition  for Avila Army web visitors will achieve a higher conversion of monthly donors because there is added clarity to what it is and the benefits of joining. because  there is added clarity to what it is and the benefits of joining.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversions on the Avila Army donation page, a monthly donation ask, we decided to add additional value prop around what the Avila Army is and clarity around the benefits of various levels of gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by the Avila Foundation showed that adding a clear value proposition around the Avila Army monthly donation ask resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment version, which clarified the benefits of joining the Avila Army, saw an 81.3% increase in donations for all traffic with a 96.5% statistical level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity and value proposition is essential for increasing donor conversions. In future experimentation, the Avila Foundation should consider testing different value propositions and messaging to determine what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, they should continue to prioritize the reinforcement of the benefits of joining the Avila Army and emphasizing the impact of their mission.

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to a call-to-action impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here
For a Gift of Any Amount, Get Your Resource Here

experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by bringing clarity to the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2018 -
On Focus on the Family’s broadcast pages, they offer a daily resource for each broadcast. The button below the resource said, “Give Now”. In a previous experiment we tested that button copy against copy that communicated what people would get when they would click the button. We used button copy that read: “Get the DVD Here”. We were able to significantly increase traffic to the donation page with this tactic, but we actually decreased donor conversion. We hypothesized that with this approach people were expecting to just get their resource by clicking the resource button on that broadcast page and abandoned the donation page once they realized that in order to get the resource they had to make a gift to Focus. To build upon these learnings, we proposed bringing clarity to the buttons rather than the donation page so that we set the expectation prior to them getting to the donation page. To do this we ran a three way test: “Give Now” was the control, “Get Your Resource For a Gift of Any Amount Here” was the first treatment and “For a Gift of Any Amount, Get your Resource Here” was the second treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatment versions increased donor conversion! But the first treatment, "Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here" increased donor conversion the greatest with 98.9% validity by 18%! When we communicated not only what a person would receive, but also how they would receive it early on in the conversion process, we were able to decrease friction and bring clarity to the conversion process.

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call to action while adding language to increase the “fear of loss” increase donor conversion rate over the existing treatment emphasizing the more general ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
The Tribute widget drive the majority of Caringbridge’s organic site revenue. It is small advertisement on a patient’s journal page that asks the visitor to make a tribute gift. With the volume of Caringbridge’s site traffic, improving the conversion rate of this widget would have an exponential impact.
A treatment was developed with the goal of clarifying the purpose of the gift. The idea was that if we can have visitors better understand the purpose of the gift, they will be more likely to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The resulting treatment made multiple impacts along the path to conversion. By itself, the new widget language increased the number of clicks by 19.3%.

Not only that but this increased traffic was more qualified than the visitors that the control would drive since they had a better understanding of the purpose of their gift. As a result, the visitors to the subsequent page had a 52% increase in conversion and a 54% decrease in refunds.

The cumulative effect of all of these lifts resulted in an 85.9% increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because  specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2023 - 03/26/2023
Luther Seminary has run this experiment across multiple of their content offers running in paid acquisition through Facebook including study guides and an online course for Revelation. They recently launched a new online course about the Book of Psalms, so we wanted to test this concept on the instant donation page to see if it would produce the same lift in conversion as it did for Revelation (834.6% lift with a 99.1% LoC).
The control donation page focused on reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. We hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, the online courses that the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, we drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than three weeks, the single-value-proposition treatment produced a lift in conversion rate by 105.2% with a 98.2% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the online course it promotes. By providing a "micro-value-proposition," which is more targeted and relevant, it helps decrease cognitive friction and increase conversions.
Furthermore, the experiment showed that the treatment was particularly effective for email visitors, desktop visitors, and returning visitors. Email visitors saw a 1564% increase in donations, desktop visitors had a 1350% increase in donations, and returning visitors had a massive 4817% increase in donations.
In future experimentation, organizations should focus on implementing a more targeted and relevant value proposition to improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact donations. The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 1.3% increase in donations, with six coming from the treatment and none coming from the control. Although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.

experiments.title: How communal language vs. listed value proposition impacted a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the value offered for engaged website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 01/01/2023
During calendar year end, we were running a direct ask as a Dear Reader beneath articles, blogs, and podcast pages site-wide. There was a housefile cultivation email called “8 Ways TGC Will Continue Serving You” that was sent on Dec. 13 and we were curious if including these 8 ways as a reminder of the value added to the readers would propel them to make a gift. The control copy used communal language that was conversational in style and the treatment listed out the 8 ways readers are served by TGC. We split traffic between the two variants and let the experiment run for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that listing out the various ways the readers were ministered to by the organization resulted in a 48% decrease in conversion rate with 96.9% confidence compared to the control language so the results are statistically valid, however we were just shy of recording 50 total transactions that we would typically target as a minimum for a test. Because valid decreases were observed across multiple channels and visitor segments after only running for one week during such a crucial time of year for fundraising, we decided to pull down the treatment copy that was diminishing results and chose to roll out the control to 100% of traffic during the final days of the year to take advantage of the higher performing copy.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and tangibility to monthly giving premiums impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and tangibility to monthly giving premiums for page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2022 - 12/23/2022
A ministry client incentivizes recurring giving by offering two premiums on their recurring giving donation page. When a recurring gift is made, the donor receives a copy of two different eBooks. With the recurring giving page not producing donations at the rate we hoped for, we put together an experiment.
We wondered if the lack of conversions is in part a result of limited clarity regarding the premiums. The eBook premiums are called out on the page by title, but the title alone does not tell the page visitor what the eBook is about, nor does it show them what the eBook looks like. A description should give page visitors clarity, connection, and application – they should know how the premium can help improve their life and their faith. Plus, imagery is powerful, and showing an image of each eBook could be appealing and a draw to the page visitor.
Thus, we developed a treatment: When the eBook premium title is clicked, a popup appears displaying the eBook cover image, title, and CTA telling the visitor how the eBook can help improve their faith or be applied to their faith.
We’ll see if the added clarity and tangibility improve donation conversions, specifically recurring ones.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than a month, the treatment resulted in a valid 253.8% increase in donations. Oddly enough, only one of the donations the treatment produced was recurring. In fact, the control produce two recurring gifts to the treatment's one.
What might this mean? It's possible that page visitors thought they would receive the premiums when making a gift of any type, not just recurring. It does however clearly say in the "SPECIAL OFFER" section of the copy that the premiums would only be sent upon someone making a recurring donation.
It is also possible that overall giving increased to such a degree because visitors saw the type of offers TGC generates and wanted to do their part to support that work. Perhaps the popups gave credibility to the organization through proof of work, which many donation pages don't make clear.
Despite not seeing a lift in recurring donations, the number of donations overall produced by the treatment, and valid lift, led us to roll the treatment out to 100% of traffic.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 111.2% increase with a 89% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 320.9% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.New visitors had a 584.6% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 200.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
