experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted click through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact clicks (and donations). The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 20.5% decrease in click rate. The brief email with a cartoon proved to be more click-friendly than the longer email with no graphic. Some users may have even clicked the cartoon to see what it led to before even reading the email and noticing that its purpose was to ask for support. What is far more telling with this experiment, however, is that the treatment produced 6 donations compared to none from the control. So although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.

experiments.title: How making the organization the actor of a Giving Tuesday donation page value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making KCBI the hero of the value proposition on the Giving Tuesday donation page will decrease donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 11/29/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if donors were more motivated to give  TO KCBI because of what KCBI would do with their gift or if they would be motivated to give because of what their GIFT would do THROUGH KCBI. So, which “hero” motivated more visitors to the donation page on Giving Tuesday to become donors.
Historically, they’ve tried to always emphasize the donor as the hero in their donation pages.
For the control, they emphasized in the headline and body copy the impact the donor would have by giving. In the treatment, they tweaked it slightly to emphasize how the impact is through KCBI. Examples include….
“You can give practical help and biblical truth this Giving Tuesday” vs “Give Practical Help and Biblical Truth this Giving Tuesday Through KCBI“
“By making a gift right now, you will be broadcasting the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensuring that practical help and biblical truth is carried out through our community…”
vs
“By making a gift to KCBI right now, you will help us broadcast the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensure that we provide practical help and biblical truth to our community…”
“Will you give right now?” vs “Will you give to KCBI right now?”
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift using the secure form below. You will have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
vs
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift to KCBI using the secure form below. Your gift will enable us to have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
We split the traffic 50/50 and ran it during their Giving Tuesday campaign that began a week early online. We hypothesized that the control page with the donor as the hero of the value proposition would have the greater conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To our surprise, the control donation page that made the donor the hero (actor) in the value proposition had a decreased donor conversion rate and our treatment making KCBI the hero of the page increased conversion by 31%. When looking at the traffic by segment, all segments favored the treatment over the control.  In our analysis of the results, we looked to see if there were any large donations that may be skewing revenue and average gift results and found only a total of five gifts. Removing these gave us the following results:
Control (donor as hero):
98 donations, 24.7% conversion rate, $8,089 revenue, and $82.54 average gift
Treatment (KCBI as hero)
126 donations, $9,003.85 revenue, and $71.45 average gift.
This analysis did not change the level of confidence or the overall result. We still brought in approximately $1,000 in revenue on the treatment despite the lower average gift thanks to the higher donor conversion rate.
While our thinking has been that donors want to be the actor/hero, it is possible that the way KCBI emphasized this in their control body copy made it sound like the donor was doing "work." The donor could have anxiety about "saving lives" "sharing the Gospel" etc. The treatment page made it clear that KCBI did these things through the donor's gift.
More testing is needed to see the influence of a value proposition with KCBI as the hero outside of a unique campaign like Giving Tuesday. It is possible that the unique motivations of this day of giving influenced the results.

experiments.title: How a new content offer with strengthened value prop in a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a new content offer for website visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2022 - 12/02/2022
When running an in-site content offer, we were seeing decent response rates from web visitors but we were curious about the lifespan of having a single offer available. This free eBook had been available and marketed to the audience for several months, so at some point returning visitors to the site would no longer engage if they had already received the offer. We had a new content offer to launch, but rather than replacing the existing content offer we wanted to understand how the audience would respond comparatively. We split the traffic 50/50 between the two offers to determine how this change would impact both email acquisition and instant donor conversion before rolling out the new offer to 100% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for one week, was that the treatment—a new devotional eBook—achieved a 1,698% increase in email acquisition rate with 100% level of confidence. We were surprised to see this increase was not limited to returning visitors, but new visitors saw similar lifts as well. Traffic from email and social and both desktop and mobile all saw valid increases in email acquisition. Donations also experienced a 51% increase with 88% level of confidence. We believe this increase is partly due to our hypothesis that a new offer would be more appealing than an offer visitors could have already seen multiple times. But we also believe the new offer was presented with an increased sense of relevancy and urgency that strengthened the value proposition and made it more appealing for visitors to get. The copy encouraged visitors to get their copy now in the last remaining weeks of the year so they could begin the devotional in the new year which was quickly approaching. This specificity on the timeline helps to answer the question in the mind of the visitors - why should I get this offer TODAY, rather than any other day or not at all? This relevance/urgency is an element missing from the control that could explain why we saw such a significant lift in results.

experiments.title: How adding language that conveys the value of joining a monthly newsletter near the form affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conveying the value of joining a monthly newsletter will increase email acquisition by giving visitors a tangible reason to sign up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/17/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates of website visitors on the homepage, we wanted to know if conveying the value of joining, and what visitors can expect to receive when signing up, would increase the rate at which people were willing to sign up.
Previously there was very little supporting copy near the signup form, with only the phrase “Subscribe to receive updates and event invitations.” presented in small font beneath the form.
For this test, we split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for just over two weeks before validating our results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 17 days, we were able to validate a 71.2%  lift in email acquisitions from visitors who saw the treatment page.
For the treatment, we wanted to convey enough value, social proof, and exclusivity to give visitors an enticing reason to submit their email addresses and agree to receive email updates from this public policy organization.
To add social proof: we leveraged the considerable size of the organization's housefile (146,000+) to express to the visitor that many other people with the same concerns, values, and interests have already signed upTo add exclusivity: we made it clear that they would be receiving emails from Dr. Ben Carson, a well-known and admired public figure, when they signed up. We also used the verbal cue, "exclusive, subscriber-only content" to reinforce this exclusivityTo add value: we clarified what subscribers receive when they sign up ... updates, educational content that teaches America's founding principles and delivers expert explanations, key insights, and critical analysis on the toughest challenges facing our nation today.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that simply asking someone to give you their email address with a generic sign-up prompt is not enough to earn their trust, pique their interest, and motivate them to join. Instead, organizations should consider what value subscribers receive when they join (what's in it for them), and communicate exactly WHY someone should subscribe and WHAT sets your email updates apart from other organizations.
Email marketing remains one of the most cost-effective, powerful tools an organization has to grow their housefile, build advocacy, and convert them into generous donors ... but as people become more and more selective about what messages (and from whom) they want landing in their inbox, communicating "what's in it for them" is no longer an option.
By setting yourself apart and conveying clearly the value people can expect to receive when they sign up, your organization has a huge opportunity to significantly improve your email acquisition rates today!

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a Facebook ad impacts conversion and cost.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2018 - 02/02/2018
Care Net offers a curriculum for churches to start a pro-life ministry called Making Life Disciples. To get more people to sign up for the curriculum, they pushed the offer through paid Facebook ads. They developed a control that had a quote on it about God being able to make abortion unthinkable through the church. They hypothesized that they might be able to motivate more people to convert if they gave more context in the ad through more value proposition copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment significantly increased conversion by 859%. This tells us that when we provide more context, communicate the value of the offer, and have a call-to-action in the text copy of our ads, we can increase conversion. The expanded value proposition not only got more people to click through to the landing page, but these people were much more highly motivated to convert. This treatment increased conversion, but even more, it decreased cost per conversion substantially from $32/conversion to $5.30/conversion.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 1)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding project-specific value proposition language for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 08/27/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift. The control of this experiment had the generic donation page and the treatment added value proposition language centered around Lottie Moon.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in an increase like we had originally thought. We saw a valid 40% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. While this was unexpected, we noticed that the highest negative impact was seen on desktop. On desktop this experiment is laid out as a 2 column experience. It could be that prospective donors had a difficult user experience with having to read the copy on the left hand side and having to scroll back up to make their gift on the right hand side of the page. Our hypothesis is that if we made the treatment a 1 column experience, we would see a lift in desktop.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign goal to an acquisition landing page during year-end impacted results.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a campaign goal for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/28/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular eBook offers is their Lady of Lourdes Novena.
During their calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether including the year-end campaign goal amount, as well as some specific value proposition language, on the Lourdes instant donation page would help drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large drop in conversion rates on the treatment, campaign-goal oriented donation page, with a 41% decline in conversion rates and a 99.5% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to a couple of factors. First, the Lourdes offer was not actively pushed during the campaign; most of the digital messaging on donation forms and the homepage mentioned the year-end goal. Therefore, people that landed on the Lourdes page through a generic Facebook ad might not have been aware there was a campaign going on. Moreover, the campaign goal itself, and why people should donate to it, was not very clear.

For future experiments we will test whether making the value prop clearer and more urgent will have a more positive impact on conversion rates.

0% increase in traffic
				× 41.1% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a program-specific value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Daily Signal focused page increase donor conversion for visitors coming from a news-focused email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2017 - 08/16/2017
During The Heritage Foundation Board Match fundraising campaign, we wanted to incorporate one of their more popular programs (The Daily Signal) into a fundraising appeal email. The goal was to show the broad impact of the organization by talking about the various aspects of the organization.
Historically, we had utilized the same landing page throughout the campaign for each email. This is something that we had tested into, finding that switching landing pages didn’t often result in any significant lifts so we determined it was not worth the extra time and energy.
However, in this situation, we felt that the brand recognition and popularity of the program may be worth creating its own page. We decided to test that hypothesis by creating a new landing page that was branded as The Daily Signal and focused its value proposition on that one aspect of Heritage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that reinforced The Daily Signal brand and focused the value proposition solely on that aspect of the organization increased donations by 272%. This emphasizes the importance of creating a cohesive message all the way through the donation process. It also all verifies a trend that we had been noticing in the last several month, that The Daily Signal is a viable program for raising funds with.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the click rate on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad treatment will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/10/2016
At the launch of our value proposition research study, Why Should I Give to You?, we ran Facebook across our typical Facebook audiences. We wanted to determine what components of the value proposition resonated most with different audience groups early on.
The control focused on the depth of the study, treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition, and treatment 2 focused on the result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that with our NPO Professionals target audience, the control that focused on the depth of the study resonated the most and drove the most clicks. Future tests will look at different variations of smaller variables within the control ad to perpetually optimize the click rate.

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: How a value proposition built upon “what” instead of “why” impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase email acquisition by limiting friction and emphasizing the value to the visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2016 - 02/15/2016
The treatment ended up decreasing email acquisition by 6%. Our hypothesis is that this was not caused by reducing friction or emphasizing the value to the visitor but because we removed the “why” behind the course and focused solely on the “what.”
The paragraph removed talked about the mission of DTS and it allowed visitors to the page to align around this same mission and mentally put themselves in the same audience. Those that felt they belonged would be more likely to take the desired action of signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had recently launched their first online course, a study of the Gospel of John. The course was offered for free in exchange for a visitor’s name, email, and address. With significant traffic being sent to the site, we wanted to find the best way to communicate the value to the visitors.

Using the learnings from experiments run with similar organization, we tried applying two key principles to the copy on the page:
We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
 	We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.

Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and details of impact affect sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that adding more information about the personal and direct impact a sponsor has for that child will increase the likelihood for that donor to sponsor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2020 - 11/27/2020
PFI launched a new digital campaign to acquire sponsorships. Our first hypothesis was that the value proposition on the page was brief and generic to sponsorship rather than specific to the child. So we updated the headline to include more value proposition and then laid out for the reader exactly how they would change that specific child’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had to do a few iterations of this test and it was touch and go for a bit. In the end, the iterations did increase sponsorships by 68%. We posit that showing the direct impact and the value of a monthly sponsorship gift does increase conversion. But we think we can do better and will continue to test.

experiments.title: How does the impact of a CTA that is Value Driven vs Product Driven affect the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: My hypothesis was more visitors would convert on a CTA that was value driven rather than product driven. So, the value of a daily pro-life devotional that is emailed to you is that it reminds you to pray daily for the unborn.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
Every Lent, we offer a 40-day Lenten Devotional Prayers for Life. This is always a popular offer and this year we wanted to have a sticky bar on our blog pages to draw visitors to the offer. We often use the CTA Get Your Free 40 Day Lent Devotional. I wanted to test this against a challenger variant that I have used in the past on Facebook. Get a Daily Reminder to Pray for the Unborn!
experiments.keyLearnings: People were 242% MORE motivated by the promise of a daily reminder to pray than simply by a free Lenten devotional. We will continue to test into ways of getting at the WHY of an offer in the CTA vs just WHAT the offer is.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition and adding a call-to-action affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action and reinforcing the value proposition improve donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2016 - 06/13/2016
In an effort to optimize the main donation page for The Heritage Foundation, we took a look at the copy that was currently there to find ways to improve and increase the force of the value proposition. While the existing copy was strong and provided solid evidentials, we noticed it was missing an important element in the copy, an ask. Our hypothesis was that we would be able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue by adding copy, to what was already there, that included a call-to-action and reinforcement of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had arrived at the original copy through a series of prior experiments. As a result, we were relatively confident that it adequately communicated the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation, but by simply adding an additional paragraph that reinforced that message and asking them to make a gift, we were able to not only get more donors to give, but give even more generously. The treatment increased donor conversion by 49%.
Additionally, we saw an increase in average gift by 62.9%. While the average gift lift had not yet statistically validated by the time the test was shut off, the approaching validity seems to indicate that we would be experiencing a combined lift to revenue of 143.5%.
This experiment reinforced the importance of increasing the force of the value proposition by reminding people of what their gift will do and always having a call-to-action in the copy.

0% increase in traffic
				× 49.4% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How the strength of the value proposition affects pay-per-click donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion from search engine marketing?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/03/2016
The Heritage Foundation had run pay-per-click campaigns on brand terms (like “Heritage Foundation”, “Heritage Foundation membership”) for years. These campaigns ran at a positive ROI and gave users who expressed intent through search a clear path to make their gifts.
Because users had high motivation that was expressed through their search terms, a very transactional landing page had tested well. But Heritage wanted to see if a more emotional appeal might improve conversion — one that told a narrative story and utilized the four pillars of a successful value proposition: appeal, exclusivity, clarity, and credibility. The page also used photography, different background, and stacked member benefits right before it asked for the conversion.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page with the more thorough value proposition and member benefits produced a 44.1% lift in donor conversion. This tells us that we can actually affect donor motivation -- even though someone is looking to make a gift, they aren't always entirely convinced. The strength of the value proposition must capture them where they are and assure them even more in their decision.

We are never done "selling" the donation until they submit their gift. That's why landing pages must always leverage each of the four pillars of the value proposition to maximize the force with which it is delivered.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donations and revenue, in spite of a different design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/15/2016
The Heritage Foundation was sending lots of traffic to their “Conservative Declaration”, a document that asked conservatives to unite under shared principles. Immediately after signing, the visitors were presented with an offer to become a member of The Heritage Foundation by making a donation. The entire experience took place within a single design.
Heritage had another membership page that had been performing extremely well. However, design was a key part of this page, and it didn’t match the design of the Conservative Declaration. Knowing that congruency is a key part of conversion, they decided to test it anyways to see if increasing the force of the value proposition outweighed design consistency.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 460% increase in revenue, despite breaking from the design of the original offer. This is a powerful learning that shows that the message is more important than the container. This also points the way to a future test to see if revenue can be further increased by working the new value proposition into the existing design and increasing congruency.

experiments.title: How a DailySignal value proposition impacted conversion on the serial ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a DailySignal ask for Heritage donors will achieve an increase in overall serial ask conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2020 - 11/01/2020
In an effort to boost overall revenue for Heritage, regardless of the designation of the funds, we wanted to test out the serial ask language for donors to the main donation form, the /join form, and the /renew donation form. Given the crossover for DailySignal readers and Heritage donors, we decided to test out a value proposition that would utilize those efforts as the reason to give a second gift. We then tested the two value propositions against each other and validated the experiment based upon the overall number of serial ask donors compared to the number of people who saw the message (THF donors).
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full three months, we saw a 27% decrease in overall conversion to the serial ask. With this in mind, we decided to end this experiment and moved on to testing other value propositions related to HAFA to try to improve the serial ask conversion.

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition through cause and effect copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will clarifying the value proposition in the headline using cause and effect copy increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/26/2016
The treatment produced a 21.1% increase in donor conversion. This tells us that by introducing the effect of the gift before highlighting the cause (the gift from the donor), we can increase the likelihood that the visitor will make a donation.
Too often, we ask people to donate without reminding them of the impact of their gift. This test prompts further testing of ordering of the statements to see if we can increase clarity and further increase results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The majority of CaringBridge’s donations come through their tribute donation pages, where visitors can give support CaringBridge in honor of a friend or loved one. A previous test had shown the effect of organization-centric copy on donor conversion, and the team wanted to build on that learning. They proposed a test to the value proposition in the headline copy using “cause and effect” language.
The control headline had been tested many times — “You make [tribute name]’s site possible”. The team proposed moving this to the subheadline, and highlighting why the site exists in the main headline: “This website helps [Tribute name] stay connected to family and friends”.
Then, the original headline would assign responsibility (or credit) to the visitor below that copy.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the amount of value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/20/2017
The treatment produced a 26.7% increase in donor conversion from the control, which showed that the amount of copy in the email is not as important as what it says. Not all words are created equal.
CaringBridge had done extensive testing in the area of personal tone—that is, getting the emails to read like an actual person wrote them. However, this concept needs to be balanced with the principle of value proposition. If the copy maintains a personal tone but does not convey enough value, then it’s just a nice communication piece.
This experiment is a good check against the concept of “long copy vs. short copy”—the most important concept is to get the right copy, regardless of length.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge sent a fundraising email to their housefile as part of a monthly appeal. The email told a personal story from a mother who had experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand. Their control email told a compelling personal story using a convincing personal tone, but they thought it lacked the value proposition copy necessary to compel readers to give.
They created a treatment that increased the amount of copy significantly, but provided (what they perceived to be) a stronger value proposition. They believed that even though this increased the time it took to read the email, that the additions would produce a greater return.
They split their file in half and launched an experiment to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How different value propositions affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will convert the most donors on the JEN donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2018
One of the essential services that CaringBridge provides is sending free email notifications when a user updates their journal. These are internally called “JEN” emails—Journal Email Notifications. Each JEN email contains a donation ask, which leads straight to a donation page. The copy on the JEN donation page had never been brought in line with other copy, so the team decided to test a value proposition that had worked elsewhere in the site, as well as a new value proposition on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The "tribute" copy, taken from the tribute page produced a 23% decrease in conversion, though the result was not statistically valid. However, the "email copy" variant, which took copy from the email and put it on the donation page, produced a statistically valid 35% decrease. 

This shows two interesting lessons—what works best in one area of the site will not necessarily work best in another, especially when the user is driven from an email instead of another page within the site. However, with less than 10% of users completing a transaction on this page, there is still plenty of optimization to be done.

experiments.title: How advertising imagery affected the clarity of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will produce the highest motivated visitors resulting in the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 08/31/2016
The ads that focused on the images of children had nearly double the email acquisition rate as the ad that just focused on the guide. Our hypothesis is that the images of children served as a reminder of why the prayers were needed whereas the guide was just what would be given. By answering the “why” first, the visitors were more motivated to download the devotional.
The visual aspect of black and white versus color did not seem to make any significant difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of their Shoes for Orphans Souls campaign, Buckner International created a 7-day digital devotional. The e-book guided individuals in prayer as a means to lift up orphans and other vulnerable children.

As part of the advertising for this campaign, we created Facebook ads to drive visitors to a conversion-focused email acquisition page. For similar organizations, past experiments had proven that showing a 3D version of the guide would produce the best response but with the topic of orphans and vulnerable children, we had a hypothesis that images of kids would provoke a better response. We created multiple versions of this ad and tested it across multiple audiences

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in a Facebook ad affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity of the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2017 - 01/23/2017
The treatment with the dark text and the book on the left produced a 95.6% increase in clickthrough rate as compared to the control, beating the other three treatments (which also produced lifts).
This experiment shows the importance of clarity in the ad, even at the initial point of contact. The high contrast in the text makes it jump out, especially in Facebook where the majority of the platform is white. Also, leading with the book image draws the reader’s eye into the text, rather than the other way around.
This experiment will help future ad campaigns begin with more optimized design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International was offering a free eBook by their president, Albert Reyes, called “The Jesus Agenda”. They hypothesized that their control ad, with a photo of Jesus, might lack clarity in the prospect-level value proposition, since it didn’t show the viewer what they were getting.

They drafted three new treatments: one with the eBook and dark text, another with the eBook and lighter text, and one with the eBook right-aligned and the text on the left. They launched a four-way test to determine which one produced the highest clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an image of the 3D eBook in the ad produce more clicks than an image of children? How do people react to a soft ask vs. a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2017 - 05/03/2017
The Buckner team realized that users were 40% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was promoting a new free ebook to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through to the landing page from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected conversions on a Facebook ad

experiments.summary:

The Buckner team realized that users were 120% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate and a 120% increase in email acquisition.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International was promoting a new devotional to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

In their first test on this ad, treatment number 3 (a photo of the eBook with a soft ask) led to a 40% increase in clickthrough. Their next test was to find out if this increase in clickthrough ultimately led to an increase in conversions.

experiments.title: How adding specificity and exclusivity language to a subscription form in the footer impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity and exclusivity language to a newsletter sign up form will result in an increase an email acquisition among website visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2022 - 10/15/2022
RTB had run previous experiments on their homepage/website footer email subscription form that did not result in any valid lifts. There were some experiments that ran as a multivariate test that did not isolate a specific variable. In an effort to increase email acquisition rate through this newsletter sign up form, we decided to hone in on the value proposition in the copy. Our hypothesis was that by being clearer in what the recipient was receiving, we would see an increase in email acquisition rate. This is because the end user would understand what is valuable about RTB’s emails instead of just signing up for another email list.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 258% lift in email acquisition rate. Our hypothesis for this experiment was confirmed in that site visitors were not clear on what they were receiving when they signed up for emails with RTB. When we provided clarity on what they were receiving and why it's exclusive to RTB, more people were willing to subscribe to their email list.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image for ad visitors would increase click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/09/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition was experiencing a low click-through rate, so we tested a few different ad angles to see if they would increase clicks. We wondered if grabbing the reader’s attention right off the bat with a statement that they could “get behind” and clearly understand what was at stake would increase click-through rate. We also wondered if the existing ad image was experiencing user fatigue, and therefore tested two new images to see how they impact click-through rate. We ran an a/b/c test. The copy for the “b” and “c” variants differs from the “a” variant only in the opening sentence of the copy.
The “b” variant has a different image than “a”, using an image with symbols that represent Texas and that almost any Texan would recognize. The “c” variant has a different image as well, showing a person riding a horse in open Texas land – an image that we hypothesized Texans would relate to and distinguish themselves through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatment 1 and 2 led to an increase in ad clicks. Treatment 1 saw a 38.9% lift in clicks with a 96.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 83.4% lift in clicks with 99.9% level of confidence. This tells us that users did appreciate the clarity in the opening line of the ad copy, as they learned immediately what the ad was asking of them (to sign a petition) and what the petition was for (to keep Texas Texan). Also, by pointing at this this petition was "critical", users likely felt more of a sense of urgency to click the ad to reach the critical petition. As these variables were not isolated, we don't know for sure whether the clarity or urgency factored more into their decision to click through to the landing page.

Ultimately, treatment 2 experienced the biggest life of the three variants. This tells us that the ad image in treatment 2 resonated the most with users. They're asked to complete a petition to "keep Texas Texan", and the image of a man riding a horse through an open field on a dirt path rightly represents Texas. Users were more likely to click an ad image that offered authenticity than an image that was more "cartoony". That's because users see Texas and Texans as "authentic".

This ad approach is worth testing with other ads and for other offers. The results and learning would be clearer, however, if the ad variants are isolated. We don't know for sure if users were more motivated to click because of the copy change or the image change.

It tells us that this audience values clarity over persuasion — a quick, short ask trumps a longer, more personal ask that attempts to draw in their emotions.
It also suggests that this page is highly functional – since it is a “dashboard” of sorts, people use it to get from one place to another, which suggests that future copy should be concise and to the point.

We wanted to increase clickthroughs on this ask to generate more donations. We created a treatment that was highly donor-centric — it gave them a virtual “pat on the back” and reminded them of all the ways that they help those they care about.

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: Discovering the Daily Signal’s value proposition with an inline donation ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most donations for The Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2016 - 09/26/2016
The Daily Signal was originally launched several years back to serve as an unbiased news source. Since its launch, it has seen a significant boost in traffic with millions of people visiting each month. This site is an initiative that is run by The Heritage Foundation.
A decision was made recently to discover ways to start monetizing the traffic while keeping the actually content free for visitors. It was decided that we could position a donation ask at the end of each article. The question then became “what aspect of The Daily Signal would visitors be most willing to sponsor?” To determine this, we launched an experiment utilizing three different value proposition statements.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running this experiment, we determined that the "investigative journalism" and "conservative policy analysis" aspects of The Daily Signal were more appealing to the readers than the other options. This value proposition lifted donor conversion by 275%. Our hypothesis is that this statement was the one that best tapped into the unique aspects of the website. While the other value proposition statements may have been appealing to visitors, they could have been said about any number of other websites.

experiments.title: How a simpler, more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a shorter, more direct value proposition affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2017 - 04/10/2017
The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”

They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.

experiments.title: How the tactic used in presenting the value proposition impacted The Daily Signal’s donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which tactic in presenting the value proposition will lead to the greatest increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2016 - 12/21/2016
The experiment produced significant lifts in both click through rate and donation rate. The table above lists the individual conversion rates from reader to donor. The click through rates followed similar trends. This results in two key learnings:
By increasing the process-level value of donating (fully laying out the reasons to give), both treatments were able to outperformed the control.
The “Dear Reader” version had the highest impact on clickthrough rate (256% lift) and donor conversion rates (642% lift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through a series of previous experiments, we had discovered a value proposition for The Daily Signal that seemed to resonate with donor. This value proposition focused on the type of journalism provided by TDS on a daily basis. With this in mind, we wanted to see if we could find a better means to deliver this message.

We created two new treatment options:
The first treatment took the form of a “Wikipedia-style” delivery that appeared in the middle of every article. It devoted more space to cohesively lay out the reasons to support The Daily Signal.
 	The second treatment took the form of an inline-survey to readers. The idea was to get the readers to say whether they valued the content. If they did, they would then be shown the donation value proposition as the next step in the process. If they selected they did not like it, they would be shown a comment box to submit the reasons why.

experiments.title: How authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increased authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed four different treatments for the advertisements. Testing proved that the third and fourth treatment produced a significant lift to clickthrough rate, but they wanted to gather more data to validate if one of these resulted in more downstream conversions—in this case, more sign-ups for the course.
The primary difference in the two treatments was the image and the copy within. The first treatment had a patriotic background that described what the course was: “Conservatism 101: An Introduction to the American Conservative Tradition”. The second, however, featured a picture of Mark Levin, a popular radio host and lecturer in the course. This image contained call-to-action copy as well: “Join Mark Levin in this new free course, Conservatism 101.”
The team isolated these two ads to determine which ad was more effective at driving downstream conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The second treatment, with Mark Levin and the call-to-action copy in the image produced a 63.7% increase in downstream conversions. This showed that the increased authority and active call to action was able to shape motivation to affect downstream actions. Ultimately, Leadership Institute didn’t just want more clicks—they wanted more clicks with a higher motivation.

This test prompted a new round of testing that used the winner as the control to continue to increase results.

experiments.title: How emotional language, clarity and authority affect ad clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does emotional language, clarity and authority affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed several different treatments for the advertisements. The first treatment posed a question: “What are conservatives actually trying to conserve?”, which implied that the course would answer that question. The second treatment introduced emotional language that outlined the threat to conservatism and made a call for conservatives to rediscover their principles. This treatment also included a call to action (CTA) in the top copy: “Start today by enrolling in Conservatism 101 now.” The third treatment took a similar tack with emotional language, but sought to align with the mindset of the reader by declaring the threat to be invalid. This treatment heightened the clarity and authority around the course offer, introducing a list of featured speakers, and introduced line breaks to better isolate the CTA: “Activate your free course today and discover—or rediscover—the principles and beauty of conservatism.” The final treatment used the same copy as the third treatment, but introduced a picture of Mark Levin with the CTA “Join Mark Levin in this new free course: Conservatism 101.”
Since Facebook is able to serve multiple ads simultaneously and gather data on each, they launched this A/B/C/D test to determine which ad produced the most clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a few days, LI had enough data to determine a winner. Both the third and fourth treatment produced a clear, statistically significant increase in clickthrough rate over the other treatments. Treatment 3 produced a 56.7% increase over the baseline due to a combination of increased clarity and authority as well as visual clarity around the call-to-action. Treatment 4 produced the largest increase of all—a 108.7% increase over the baseline due to the copy adjustments from treatment three and the new image and embedded CTA, which reinforced the authority of the top copy.

In their next experiment, Leadership Institute isolated the top two performing ads to determine which one produced the most conversions on the following landing page.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/09/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below reflect are from the ad set targeting the Network for Good Facebook interest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this audience, the result was a resounding “No.” The Network for Good audience had a 75% decrease in course registrations when shown the bullet points ad.

Each post had a comparable amount of clicks to the landing page, implying that people who viewed the control were more primed to complete their registration. It’s possible that by adding these bullet points, it made it easier for someone to skim through the post and not experience the full value proposition of the offer.

This learning should not be taken out of context and applied to every audience. Other ad sets targeting different audience segments are approaching validity with the Bullet Point ad as the winning treatment.

experiments.title: How a program-specific value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Daily Signal focused page increase donor conversion for visitors coming from a news-focused email?

Timeframe: 08/02/2017 - 08/16/2017
During The Heritage Foundation Board Match fundraising campaign, we wanted to incorporate one of their more popular programs (The Daily Signal) into a fundraising appeal email. The goal was to show the broad impact of the organization by talking about the various aspects of the organization.
Historically, we had utilized the same landing page throughout the campaign for each email. This is something that we had tested into, finding that switching landing pages didn’t often result in any significant lifts so we determined it was not worth the extra time and energy.
However, in this situation, we felt that the brand recognition and popularity of the program may be worth creating its own page. We decided to test that hypothesis by creating a new landing page that was branded as The Daily Signal and focused its value proposition on that one aspect of Heritage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that reinforced The Daily Signal brand and focused the value proposition solely on that aspect of the organization increased donations by 272%. This emphasizes the importance of creating a cohesive message all the way through the donation process. It also all verifies a trend that we had been noticing in the last several month, that The Daily Signal is a viable program for raising funds with.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?

When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this ad set, we saw a 184% lift in conversion rate from the ads that used bullet points. Additionally, we say a 32% decrease in cost-per-lead.

Interestingly enough, we saw the opposite result with an ad set targeting people unfamiliar with NextAfter. I would hypothesize that since we have already established a sense of trust with our housefile, they do not require as deep of an understanding of the value proposition of our offers.

By bullet pointing the key aspects of the course, someone who already trusts us is able to see how the course is relevant to them and decide to register. The ads without bullet points have the key aspects of the course buried in paragraph form, making it harder for someone to quickly see whether or not this is a resource they’re interested in.

When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile lookalikes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The use of bullet points with the housefile lookalikes audience saw an 81% decrease in course registrations. This result, combined with the result of the Network for Good audience test, continues to confirm our hypothesis that the use of bullet points is less effective with an audience that is not familiar with NextAfter.

They need to see the full value proposition of the offer in order to understand its credibility and depth, whereas our housefile already trusts our work and just needs to quickly see whether the offer is relevant to them.

As part of the Shoes for Orphan Souls campaign, Buckner International created a pledge for visitors to sign. The primary goal of the page was to collect email addresses. This could have a variety of meanings to different visitors.  The treatment built on earlier learnings about the Christian motivations of typical Buckner donors and focused on the Biblical imperative to care for orphans: “Stand up for orphans and vulnerable children”.  This was a more direct and clarified value proposition and call to action.

experiments.summary:

experiments.title: How a different value proposition in a Facebook webinar ad affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition centered around the need for a new fundraising model lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/28/2018
In this experiment, we wanted to see which value proposition focus would get the most webinar registrations for the NextAfter.com traffic audience. The control focused on the idea that organizations get cut out of the process when relying on Facebook’s fundraising tools. The treatment focused on the need for a new fundraising model that doesn’t rely on the typical “best practice” ideas and strategies.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw an 83.2% increase in conversion rate, and a lower cost per registration. This is helpful to know as we continue to develop and market resources centered around using Facebook for fundraising – it will cause us to focus more on the specific contrast between our Facebook model and the typical approaches.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and details of impact affect sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that adding more information about the personal and direct impact a sponsor has for that child will increase the likelihood for that donor to sponsor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2020 - 11/27/2020
PFI launched a new digital campaign to acquire sponsorships. Our first hypothesis was that the value proposition on the page was brief and generic to sponsorship rather than specific to the child. So we updated the headline to include more value proposition and then laid out for the reader exactly how they would change that specific child’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had to do a few iterations of this test and it was touch and go for a bit. In the end, the iterations did increase sponsorships by 68%. We posit that showing the direct impact and the value of a monthly sponsorship gift does increase conversion. But we think we can do better and will continue to test.

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the amount of value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/20/2017
CaringBridge sent a fundraising email to their housefile as part of a monthly appeal. The email told a personal story from a mother who had experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand. Their control email told a compelling personal story using a convincing personal tone, but they thought it lacked the value proposition copy necessary to compel readers to give.
They created a treatment that increased the amount of copy significantly but provided (what they perceived to be) a stronger value proposition. They believed that even though this increased the time it took to read the email, that the additions would produce a greater return.
They split their file in half and launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 26.7% increase in donor conversion from the control, which showed that the amount of copy in the email is not as important as what it says. Not all words are created equal.

CaringBridge had done extensive testing in the area of personal tone—that is, getting the emails to read like an actual person wrote them. However, this concept needs to be balanced with the principle of value proposition. If the copy maintains a personal tone but does not convey enough value, then it’s just a nice communication piece.

This experiment is a good check against the concept of “long copy vs. short copy”—the most important concept is to get the right copy, regardless of length.

experiments.title: How a premium offer affects donor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a premium offer affect donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2016 - 11/14/2016
The treatment with no premium book offer produced a 55.1% increase in donations. Without the “anchor point” of the book, average gift dropped by 34.5%, but there was no discernible impact on revenue.
This indicated something deeper about the motivation of their donors: without the premium offer, the value proposition was entirely centered around supporting the free online courses—and more donors responded. This suggests that DTS prospects are more motivated by an aspirational offer rather than a tactile offer.
The fact that the average gift for the control is right at $75 suggests that people either give to get the book, or don’t give at all. This may imply that people are conflating the offer with the incentive, and the aspirational aspect of the value proposition is clouded by the offer.
Because DTS can offer the premium throughout the course (in a banner) and directly through an email, they will move forward with the treatment. As a result of this experiment, DTS was able to grow their donor file significantly faster without affecting revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had released a free online course on the book of Revelation. After visitors registered for the course, they were given the opportunity to give a gift to support the free courses. DTS had traditionally used commentary books as premiums for donations, but wanted to test the impact of removing the premium offer on donations and revenue.

They hypothesized that removing the book might result in more donations, though the average gift might be lower without the “anchor point” ($75, in this case) for the book offer.

They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects name acquisition conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a clarified value proposition and shorter copy increase name acquisition conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/08/2016
By clarifying the value proposition and removing friction in page length, we saw a 80.5% higher conversion rate. This confirmed out hypothesis that we could increase name acquisition conversions by clarifying the value proposition with a more direct call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of the Shoes for Orphan Souls campaign, Buckner International created a pledge for visitors to sign. The primary goal of the page was to collect email addresses. We hypothesized that the value proposition on the control landing page was too ambiguous so we set out to clarify the value proposition on the treatment, as well as shorten the copy.

On the control page the value proposition was captured in the headline “Protecting the Vulnerable and Lifting up the Fatherless”. This could have a variety of meanings to different visitors.  The treatment built on earlier learnings about the Christian motivations of typical Buckner donors and focused on the Biblical imperative to care for orphans: “Stand up for orphans and vulnerable children”.  This was a more direct and clarified value proposition and call to action.

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition through cause and effect copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will clarifying the value proposition in the headline using cause and effect copy increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/26/2016
The treatment produced a 21.1% increase in donor conversion. This tells us that by introducing the effect of the gift before highlighting the cause (the gift from the donor), we can increase the likelihood that the visitor will make a donation.
Too often, we ask people to donate without reminding them of the impact of their gift. This test prompts further testing of ordering of the statements to see if we can increase clarity and further increase results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The majority of CaringBridge’s donations come through their tribute donation pages, where visitors can give support CaringBridge in honor of a friend or loved one. A previous test had shown the effect of organization-centric copy on donor conversion, and the team wanted to build on that learning. They proposed a test to the value proposition in the headline copy using “cause and effect” language.

The control headline had been tested many times — “You make [tribute name]’s site possible”. The team proposed moving this to the subheadline, and highlighting why the site exists in the main headline: “This website helps [Tribute name] stay connected to family and friends”.

Then, the original headline would assign responsibility (or credit) to the visitor below that copy.

They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How additional ad copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing copy in the image affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2015 - 11/30/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their Christmas devotional series as a free offer on Facebook. Previous testing had shown that using copy in the image of the ad increased clickthrough rate and conversion rate. But as they reviewed their ad, they wondered if there was actually too much copy in the ad, and reading it bogged the reader down and kept them from clicking.
They made a treatment that removed the subheadline from their ad image, leaving only a single call-to-action with no supporting copy in order to simplify the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ad with reduced copy produced a 19.8% increase in clickthrough rate, which indicated that less copy simplified the value proposition enough to attract more interest from people who saw it.

experiments.title: How streamlining the tribute widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a streamlined widget with added social proof inspire more people to begin the donation process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2015 - 04/24/2015
Most gifts to CaringBridge start with the “tribute widget”, a small box that appears on all Site Journal pages and profile pages. We noticed one day that the tribute widget had a lot of information that could be extraneous and distracting from the intended action, which was to begin the gift process.
The CaringBridge team developed a streamlined version of this widget that removed a “most recent quote”, along with avatars for signed in users. To increase clarity, the team put pre-filled text into the box to instruct the user what to write. Also, we added social proof into the widget, dynamically pulling the number of previous donors to inspire the user to join them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The streamlined widget with added social proof resulted in nearly 52% more people beginning the donation process. We closely monitored donation page conversion rates as well, and there was no change. Clarifying the value proposition by removing extra graphics and copy and reducing anxiety through social proof resulted in a significant lift in dollars raised.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects click rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most clicks from a Facebook Ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/12/2016
We released a research study on nonprofit value proposition and advertised it through Facebook Ads to people affiliated with NextAfter including emails on file and website visitors.
There are many different aspects of the value proposition that we could focus on for the promotion of the study, so we wanted to test 3 primary value propositions to see what resonated the most with our audience.
The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.

This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/28/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Proverbs Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to an increase in conversion rate for email traffic: 67.7 increase in conversion rate for email traffic with a level of confidence of 96.9%. When it came to the all-traffic segment, we saw a 59.1 increase in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96.9%.
When it came to paid social traffic, the change of the language did not provide a valid or directional increase in conversion rate.
We believe the value prop-centric language had the most effect on users who know Luther Seminary well and are very aware of its mission and goals. When it came to new visitors (i.e. traffic from paid social), the language was not motivating enough to convince more people to donate to the Seminary.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

				× 59.1% increase in conversion rate
