experiments.title: The revenue impact of shorter and more efficient value proposition copy on a premium offer donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter and more efficient value proposition copy will reduce friction and increase donor conversion on the donation page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2022 - 08/14/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They use premium offers on Facebook to acquire new donors and have been very successful with this approach. For their most popular offer, Seeing Beautiful Again, they wanted to increase the donor conversion on the donation page. In reviewing the copy, we found that it appeared quite long on mobile devices, which were the primary devices visiting the page.
We hypothesized that shorter and more efficient value propositional copy would increase donor conversion by reducing friction on the page.
We developed a treatment version that reduced the word count from 307 words on the control to 154 on the treatment while retaining what we felt was the core value proposition of the premium and of donating to FamilyLife. We split traffic and ran the experiment almost two months through Optimize
experiments.keyLearnings:
There wasn't a significant source of traffic that experienced a decrease from the shorter treatment. We also verified that there were no larger gifts skewing the results. Donor conversion also increased, however we were not able to validate the increase on donor conversion. However, once again, the increase was being found on desktop, mobile, new, and returning visitor segments so we had no concerns with rolling out the shorter text on the page and ending the test.
We will be using this approach going forward.

				gift).

									204.1% increase in revenue

experiments.title: The impact of clearer and more efficient value proposition copy on email conversion for a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy length and increasing the clarity and strength of the value proposition will increase email conversion by reducing friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2022 - 08/14/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They use content offers and premium offers on Facebook to acquire new donors and have been very successful with this approach. For their content offer, Soul-Care Staycation, they wanted to increase email acquisition on the landing page. In reviewing the page, we found that the copy was long on mobile devices, the primary source of traffic to the page. It also lacked a clear headline and the value proposition could have been stronger. We hypothesized that a stronger, more effecient value proposition would increase email conversion.
For the treatment we changed the headline from simply the title of the free resource to one that conveyed more of the value of the offer, “your free guide to essential soul-care.” We also made the copy more conversational and included highlights of the eBook’s exclusive nature and content designed to help the subscriber “take a breath and focus on what’s truly important.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that this clearer, more efficient value propositional copy increased email acquisition for all traffic with a 92% Level of Confidence, but most importantly, increased it for NEW visitors which were the primary source of subscribers for this acquisition offer. In reviewing the data for other segments we didn't have concerns with rolling out the treatment for all traffic. We will use a similar approach when launching new content offer landing pages in the future.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 28.9% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How using value prop language in an email impacted click-through and donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop language for an email audience will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/24/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of the language used in email appeals has traditionally been focused on the premium offer itself, with mentions of the book, what it contains, and different ways to get the book. For this experiment we wanted to test whether making the email more value-prop focused, with a reference to the book, would impact click-through and donor conversion rates.
This test was run with multiple emails; the results in this experiment are for the 1st email send, including a resend. Both emails pointed to the exact same landing page, which focused on the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were extremely interesting. While there was a 106% increase in click-through rates total, with a 100% level of confidence, when looking at the experiment through a donation conversion lens, the results were different.
When looking at open-to-donation conversion rates, there was a 29% lift in donation conversion rates with the value prop version, with a 70% level of confidence. However, when looking at how many people clicked a link and actually completed the donation, there was a 37% DECREASE in donation conversions, with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this result is that while the value prop version was driving more clicks to the landing page, without having any value prop language on the page itself, those potential donors lost their interest in making a donation and receiving the premium.
This then led to the next round of testing, which incorporated value prop language onto the landing page itself.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
106.2% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler copy approach and stacked design increase name conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2015 - 05/12/2015
The Heritage Foundation launched a petition against Common Core Education Standards. Despite reaching an ideal audience and achieving a low cost-per-email, the page still had room for improvement in terms of name acquisition. They hypothesized that stopping “Common Core” might be too specific of an ask, if they weren’t familiar with the policy fight itself. They introduced new copy that focused on the underlying principle of the argument — that the Federal Government shouldn’t control public education. Additionally, they placed the petition itself under the copy to allow the reader to fully digest the value proposition before completing it.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were concerned that the target audience wasn't as familiar with the specific term "Common Core" and would respond more favorably to the principles underlying the relationship between the federal government and education. This tells us that Common Core might actually be the best "opposition keyword", at least with this highly-educated group.

0% increase in traffic
				× 37.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experiences from 3 steps to steps for prospective donors from an ebook offer will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 08/06/2022
Since transitioning to a new giving platform, The Navigators’ giving process has been split up into multiple steps and pages. When we run an ebook offer on Facebook, people now have the following experience: they fill out the form to get the ebook, get a confirmation page with the donation value prop language, click through to donate, go to a generic donation page to select their gift amount, and then a checkout process. Our hypothesis is that all these steps are causing friction for the potential donor. As much as possible, we wanted to consolidate the steps by running a redirect experiment on one of their ebook offers.
We had already run this experiment with a previous offer but we did not get a large enough sample size to validate the results. So, we re-ran this experiment with their most popular offer in order to achieve statistical validity in our results.
The treatment for this experiment reduced the process down to 2 steps so that the value proposition language was on the same page as the gift array. We also made this new page our ideal donation page by creating a tabbed form and moving the gift array beneath the copy. This is to mimic what the donation pages used to look like for this organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results for this experiment produced a valid 97% lift in donor conversion rate, with a level of confidence of 99%. These results indicate that the new giving experience is indeed causing friction for prospective donors. Reducing the steps down to 2 steps made it easier for the user to make their donation. In addition to that, the adjusted donation page format introduced other variables that may have also contributed to the significant lift in donations. Ideally, we would like to test adding the actual payment information and process on the same page so that we can further consolidate the steps and reduce the percentage of those who abandon the process.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 97.2% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding more appeal to the value proposition on a donation page affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more appeal to the value proposition for readers of an instant donation page will achieve a higher number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2022 - 05/27/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this instant donation page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “bottom of the marketing funnel”. The donor conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more appeal into the body copy would result in a higher number of donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment validated for the control with a 44% decrease in donor conversions with a 97/7% level of confidence.
This experiment used one of the 4 tenets of a well-executed value proposition - appeal. In hindsight, we probably should have applied more of the other elements, like exclusivity and clarity. While we did reduce the number of paragraphs, the overall body copy grew, and could have probably been more clear. For the future, we will attempt to add more of the other elements.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition on an instant donation page will achieve higher instant conversion rates for a paid acquisition campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 08/14/2022
We had been running a content offer for a free ebook on CRT produced by one of our public policy clients. At the time of this experiment, the offer had produced a healthy email acquisition rate of 46.7% but suffered from a low instant conversion rate (ICR) of just 1.8%.
After reviewing the instant donation page, we quickly identified an opportunity to inspire more people to give by improving the offer’s value proposition—why a potential donor should give to our client, instead of someone else, or not at all—by improving its appeal, clarity, exclusivity, and credibility.
Since other key metrics were trending positive, we felt that we were reaching the right people with the right offer and that improving the value proposition around an issue our audience cared about presented a significant opportunity to improve the performance of this campaign.
Improving the value proposition for this offer meant we would need to change a number of variables in the way the story was being presented through the copy on the instant donation page.
To improve appeal, we replaced passive, informative language with copy that focused first on the urgency of the problem, then the specific impact the prospect’s donation would have—and the specific things they would help the organization do to fight back. 
To improve clarity, we broke the copy into smaller, concisely written paragraphs that focused on continuing the narrative forward in a logical linear fashion, reducing potential friction caused by the meandering nature of the control. We also put the call to action front and center in a headline to make it clear to the prospective donor exactly why they were there.
To improve exclusivity, we replaced generalized impact language with persuasive bullet points that expressed not only what the organization was doing to solve the problem, but also how, and the impact these actions would have
To improve credibility, we used our last bullet point to present the model legislation that our client wrote and which was already being used by lawmakers in several states to ban CRT from schools—something we felt clearly set them apart from other organizations
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of our experiment were encouraging: our focus on improving the value proposition produced a valid 25% lift in donations with a 95.2% level of confidence for all traffic and a directional lift of 58.3% directional lift in donations (91.7% LoC) for paid social traffic.
The treatment also produced a 21.3% lift in revenue (87% LoC) for all traffic and a 113% directional lift in revenue for paid social traffic.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by personalizing the confirmation message at the top of the page with the prospective donor's first name. We will still want to verify these results by running a subsequent experiment to isolate the impact of first name personalization vs. strengthening of the value prop.

experiments.title: How Adding Value Proposition Language near the ‘Give’ Button on a Homepage Affects Conversion Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language near the give button on a homepage for web traffic will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2021 - 12/29/2021
Although Luther Seminary has a large, bright donation callout on their homepage, we observed that it was not driving the expected level of donations.
Since there were other bright design elements competing for attention around the donation callout, and because Luther Seminary’s homepage is somewhat ‘busy’, with many navigation choices for visitors, we hypothesized that perhaps visitors weren’t being given enough of a reason to choose to donate, with so many other options from which to choose.
Previous experiments have shown that clearly communicating a value proposition motivates donors to give. In light of these past learnings, we decided to test how adding value proposition language near the ‘GIVE’ button on Luther Seminary’s homepage might improve conversion rates without necessitating a redesign of their homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running our experiment for 90 days, we observed a 21% decrease in conversion rate with an 82.5% level of confidence.
While this result was surprising, it does provide context and points the direction for future optimization.
Most interestingly, while returning visitors showed a 65% decrease in conversion rates, the new treatment did result in a 127% increase in donations from new traffic (including an impressive 295% lift in paid traffic donations).
This provides some interesting insights:
first, we can hypothesize that for first time visitors, especially those being acquired via paid traffic, the value proposition was helpful in inspiring them to give
second, there could be a variety of factors leading to the decrease in donations from returning traffic, one of which may be the sheer number of things competing for a visitor's attention on the homepage. A quick heuristic analysis of the homepage indicates that it is quite busy and may cause friction for returning visitors. However, an analysis of website data (such as page path, exit, and bounce rate reports), combined with scroll tracking / heat mapping, should be conducted to see what people are doing when they are landing on the homepage and where they are going when they click through
It is doubtful that the inclusion of helpful value prop language was the key motivating factor in reducing donations from returning visitors, but additional analysis would be required to determine this with a high level of certainty.

experiments.title: How reiterating the value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional value proposition language reduce the refund rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2015 - 03/31/2015
With the rise of crowdfunding sites, CaringBridge has seen a rise in refunds as supporters misunderstand or are misinformed about who receives donations collected on the site. To attempt to reduce the refund rate, we restated the value proposition and clarified that donations go to CaringBridge — not the patient — immediately below the donate button, to be read at the moment of decision.
experiments.keyLearnings: The increase in conversion was an unexpected result — but the learning tells us that by restated the value proposition at the moment of final decision, we can increase the likelihood of a donation. This is a learning that can be tested site wide.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and creating a one-column experience on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a more robust value proposition on the main donation page and creating a more streamline experience for site visitors will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/25/2022
IMB had a main donate page that had no extensive value proposition language accompanying it. The only copy on the page was to “Advance the Kingdom” and that “100% of your gift goes to missionaries.” While these are aspects of their value proposition, we hypothesized that it did not fully encapsulate why someone should donate to the IMB. Using language from their value prop workshop, we pulled out several value statements to create a donation experience that outlines multiple reasons a person should give. We used 2 variants for this experiment: treatment 1 kept the same format but added more value proposition copy, and treatment 2 used the same value prop copy from treatment 1 but was a 1 column experience. While the page automatically becomes a 1 column experience in mobile, we wanted to see if there was a marked difference in donations on desktop through this treatment.
Traffic to this main donation page was driven by both organic and paid traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for an extended period of time (21 days), we did not see a difference in results from either treatment. Treatment 1 resulted in a 12.6% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 82% while treatment 2 resulted in a 1.5% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 12.3%– none of which validated. While it may seem like there was a decrease in revenue, this does not account for outlier gifts. When looking at the difference between desktop and mobile site visitors, we do not see a significant difference in results within those segments either.
There are follow up experiments that can be done on this page to further optimize the experience like: reducing the number of calls to action on the page, highlighting a "most popular" gift amount, or highlighting different value statements in the copy.
Upon further investigation, we noticed that most of the traffic going to this main donate page is actually from a "Lottie Moon" landing page--which can explain the disconnect in messaging that a site visitor may experience when going from a Lottie Moon page to this main donate page. Further testing around this funnel will be done in order to increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition impacted signups for a weekly newsletter – Part 2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing appeal, clarity and exclusivity for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in newsletter subscriptions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2022 - 04/15/2022
When sending visitors to a landing page with a form to sign up for a weekly newsletter, we thought that providing additional clarity about the contents of the newsletter would lead to more email subscriptions. Our initial treatment decreased conversion, so we developed a second treatment that condensed the landing page copy into just a few paragraphs highlighting the appeal and exclusivity of the newsletter contents. We split traffic between the control with a single headline and the treatment with the additional value proposition and ran the experiment for 7 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: After one week, we observed a valid 64.2% decrease in email conversion with 97.8% confidence so we ended the experiment. Mobile visitors specifically saw a 50% decrease in email acquisitions with a 95% level of confidence which leads us to believe the treatment copy was too long and hiding the form and call to action "below the fold" of the page and required too much scrolling which was adding friction to the page. Also, because these page visitors are coming from an email that gives a thorough description of the value and benefits of the newsletter, we know that traffic is already motivated to get the product and we believe we can use less copy once they indicate they are interested by clicking from the email. We also believe that using "for all of life" in the headline could be causing some friction and anxiety rather than offering clarity and making the user think they will be getting the email newsletter "for all of life" rather than once a week and cause confusion about their ability to opt-out of these messages in the future. We will relaunch the experiment with a simple headline and button copy change and see how this impacts email conversion going forward.

experiments.title: How concisely communicating the value proposition impacted conversions on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that concisely communicating the value proposition for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2022 - 04/11/2022
We previously tested the introduction of value proposition on TGC’s main donation page and saw that it was directionally decreasing conversion on the page so we ended the test and went back to the drawing board for new ideas on how to improve conversion and achieve the lift we were looking for. We still believed that some value proposition would be better than no value proposition so we wanted to condense the copy and see if a shorter presentation of the value prop would get greater results. We developed a treatment that acknowledged the visitor’s interest in making a gift in the headline, highlighted the need at hand, and quickly outlined the direct impact the donor’s gift would make. We also removed the header and footer with outbound or distracting links to keep the visitor focused on the donation page ask and make the desired call to action clear. Traffic was split 50/50 to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 3 weeks, we achieved a 116.4% increase in conversion rate with 99% level of confidence so the results are valid. We saw valid lifts for desktop visitors (130.8% increase), returning visitors (295.2% increase), and email visitors (866.7%) all with 100% level of confidence. We believe this happened because we acknowledged their reason for visiting the page and began the conversation to communicate the impact of making a gift clearly and succinctly to page visitors. One thing to keep in mind is that revenue experienced a 56.1% decrease with an 87% level of confidence due to a few large gifts on the control version that are outliers impacting these results but we should also consider if there is anything on the treatment page that could be causing donors to choose a lower average gift. To combat the indication that there is a directional decline in revenue, on a future iteration of a test for this page, we could highlight a "most popular" gift choice on the array to help improve average gift. We could also call more attention to recurring giving to increase the overall value of a donor upon conversion.

experiments.title: How a contextual value proposition and a reordered form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that contextualizing the value proposition and reordering the form for survey completers will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2021
The Boy Scouts were running a survey to their National Eagle Scout Association members to better understand what they valued about membership. Since many of these members were lifetime members, they decided to end the survey on a donation page. Historically, their donation pages had been broad messages about investing in future Scouts. The donation pages also had copy on the left side of the page under an image, and the donation form flowing down the right side of the page.
Based on a library of fundraising experiments, the team decided to make two radical shifts to the page. First, they crafted new copy that thanked the user for completing the survey and presented a value proposition asking for a monthly donation. Second, they moved the donation form below the copy, so that the user was forced to read through the copy and absorb the value proposition before engaging with the form. The hypothesis was that the user would be more motivated by the contextual ask and that the ordering of the form would place the giving opportunity when the donor was primed to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new form and copy produced a 258% lift in donations. This shows that the contextual copy, combined with the reordered form, produced a much more likely donation outcome.
This provides many future implications for Scouting Gives and the appeal creation process, as well as simple form adjustments that can reduce friction and increase conversion.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a main donation page impacted conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including value proposition for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2022 - 03/03/2022
Because we saw a lift in donor response at calendar year-end by adding value proposition to the main donation page, we wanted to introduce similar language on the main donation page outside of a high urgency campaign. Throughout the body copy, we emphasize the problem TGC is trying to address and revised the headline to indicate how the donor’s gift would be used. Beneath the donation button we added a sentence of supporting value proposition demonstrating the impact TGC donors have had throughout the year. Finally, we removed the navigation bar and alternate conflicting calls to action in the footer to keep the donor on the page and stay focused on moving forward with making their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found across all channels and segments was the treatment decreased both transactions and revenue coming in from the main donation page traffic. It's possible the amount of copy before the donation form was actually getting in the way of highly motivated visitors who were already prepared to make a gift upon arrival to the page. While this approach originally worked for a high urgency campaign, we will want to test some shorter language to introduce the donation form itself.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 71.1% decrease with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a more prominent callout combined with a more powerful value proposition affects new site signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the visibility of the CTA and the force of the value proposition for blog post visitors will achieve an increase in new site signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/17/2021
CaringBridge had engaged in a campaign to write more blog posts to help people in life situations who might be ideal prospects to start a CaringBridge site. However, on the blog posts themselves, the CTA was a small purple button that was inserted in different areas throughout the posts. The team thought that the button should be fully built out into a detached section that made a focused value proposition for starting a site and had a more powerful CTA.
They created a box that popped out visually from the main container, had a headline that addressed the audience segment directly, and listed a clear, concise value proposition for starting a new site. Finally, they centered the CTA around the user and highlighted that it was free, and used a lighter brand color to call more visual attention to the button.
They hypothesized that this would increase the number of new sites started, a critical metric for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 65.5% increase in new site signups, which showed the first step in converting more blog traffic to start new CaringBridge sites. Future tests will include more design differentiation, placement of the box, and augmenting it through sticky bars and popups.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to benefits impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that communicating the value proposition for main donation page visitors will achieve increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/07/2021 - 11/14/2021
The Avila Institute was looking to improve the donor conversion rates and revenue associated with their main donation pages across both their primary website and their spiritual direction websites.
In reviewing the main donation page, we noticed that most of the page is communicating the benefits of giving at a certain level, but there isn’t much reason communicated as to WHY you should give.
Therefore, we theorized that clearly communicating the value proposition within the page copy itself on the primary donation pages for the websites in question would be the greatest place to start with experimentation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When combining the data from both websites, we increased donor conversion rates by +811% (with a 99.8% level of confidence) and revenue by +857.4% (with a 99.9% level of confidence) within the treatment experience.
This is a critical reminder that the reason to give is NOT a benefit (or incentive), but rather a clear articulation as to the reason WHY a donor should give to your organization instead of some other organization (or not at all).
By communicating this value proposition in this way, the Availa and Spiritual Direction website visitors were more compelled to give a gift at all, but also appeared to be more generous when they decided to give, as well.

experiments.title: Whether the use of personalization in subject line (Your report) will increase open rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implying ownership in the subject line for recipients will achieve a greater open rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2021 - 09/20/2021
We knew from prior testing that using the word “You” in a subject line would increase open rates. We hypothesized that saying “your report” would increase open rates even further as it implied that the recipient was receiving something that belonged to them and that they were invested in. We decided to test this against “YOU made this possible.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we hypothesized, implying ownership of the email by using "your report" in the subject caused more recipients to be interested in opening the email AND even MORE to click once opened. This shows us that if we can imply ownership in the subject line, not only will more people want to read the email, more will be primed to take action.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Clicks experienced a 21.0% increase with a 92% level of confidence.Clicks to Opens experienced a 23.6% increase with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How clarifying and reinforcing the monthly ask affected revenue per visitor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and reinforcing the monthly ask for prospective donors will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/07/2021
Prison Fellowship International wanted to increase their monthly donor conversion rate for their Lamplighter prison ministry program. In reviewing the page, they found two key opportunities to optimize conversion. First, they hypothesized the design of the “one-time” or “monthly” selector might be making it unclear to the viewer which option was selected. To attempt to make this clearer, they changed the color and added a checkbox to increase visual certainty as to which option was selected.
Then, in an attempt to increase the percentage of donors who selected a monthly gift, they added an image and a text callout that reinforced the benefits of giving a monthly gift for the organization and for the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for several weeks, the treatment directionally increased recurring donor conversion, but the result was not significant or statistically valid.
However, looking at overall donations, there was a 21.7% increase in all giving, and a noticeable lift in average gift, even after large outlier gifts were removed. When revenue per visitor was calculated, it emerged that the combination of increased conversion and increased average gift led to a 134.6% increase in revenue.
The big question is why did this happen?
Our hypothesis is that clarifying whether "one-time" or "monthly" was selected increased conversion by giving more clarity to the visitor as to their gift type. This may also have had an impact on average gift as one-time donors (who typically give larger gifts) were more confident that their gift was, in fact, one-time.  Additionally we hypothesize that the "impact language" designed to increase recurring gift conversion increased overall average gift by inserting value proposition into transactional donation form, right at the moment before the donor selected how much they were going to give.
The next test will focus on providing separate gift arrays for "one-time" and "monthly" selections with the goal of increasing average gift for one-time gifts.

experiments.title: How reinforcing need on a child sponsorship page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the element of need for Facebook visitors will achieve an increase in completed sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2021 - 08/27/2021
Compassion International was testing their top performing offer funnels, among which was “Recently Lost Sponsor”. One thing they noticed as they went through the funnel was that the key value proposition from the advertisement—lost sponsor—was missing once you got to the child’s biography page. They hypothesized that reinforcing that fact with a callout on the biography page would increase conversion rate.
They launched a simple test to fire a red banner with this fact on all traffic that came through the “lost sponsor” campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a statistically valid 48% increase in "adds to cart", or children who had the "sponsor me and checkout" button clicked, which starts the checkout process.

However, there was only a 21% directional increase in children sponsored, with 51% Level of Confidence. This finding highlights that there may be some issue with the "checkout" process, as this and other tests have shown that similar accelerations in clickthrough rate and adds to cart do not result in more completed sponsorships.

experiments.title: How focusing the donation page copy on the offer and putting it in more peoples hands impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by focusing on helping us reach the next person with these daily devotions for those that have previously signed up to receive Godpause will achieve a higher ICR.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/10/2021
This experiment was run on the donation page of GodPause which is a daily devotional offered by Luther Sem.
This experiment started out as a radical redesign where we simplified the form and removed all of the hyperlinks in the header and footer as well as updated the copy on the form. We quickly learned that the form redesign didn’t have any effect on conversion rate so we focused specifically on the copy. The Control copy focused on Luther Seminary and its standard value prop of training future Christian leaders and pastors and filling over 1,000 empty pulpits. With the treatment copy, we took a different angle and focused on the daily devotional offer itself with the ask of “would you help us find the next subscriber to Godpause by making a donation today”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The biggest takeaway is these new subscribers don't know Luther Seminary or has a connection to Luther Seminary so when we focused on Luther Seminary and its main value prop of training future pastors and filling empty pulpits it didn't resonate.
However, when we flip it and focus on reaching the next subscriber to GodPause (Luthers daily devotional) it makes it easier for the new subscriber to connect to. These are people that have just signed up to receive these daily devotionals so can see the value in it, so we focused the copy on helping us continue to get these daily devotions out to more people by making a donation today.

experiments.title: How urgency impacted donor conversion on The Daily Signal donation ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including language and a countdown clock about the approaching year end increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/01/2017
The Heritage Foundation had recently discovered a tactic that was able to successfully increase the donor conversion rate of readers of The Daily Signal. This website is a Heritage-owned property that receives over 2 million visitors per month so even minor improvements can lead to significant gains.
In the days leading up to December 31st, we wanted to see if we could utilize the upcoming deadline to increase the urgency of the gift. For this test, we modified the existing value proposition statement to allude to the December 31st deadline for tax-deductibility and then added a countdown clock to visually reinforce this date.
We then launched the experiment with 10 days remaining in the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had two interesting observations. The first, and most important, is that we were able to lift donor conversion rate by 61% by reinforcing the urgency of the December 31st deadline. The second observation came from an analysis of the number of visitors that clicked on the initial ad.
What we found is the year end messaging resulted in a 45.6% decrease in click through rate (statistically significant). That means we were able to achieve a 61% lift in donations from roughly half the number of visitors. The key to this learning is that, while the urgency-based message may not be as appealing to a larger audience, it increases the motivation of those that do click on the link.

experiments.title: How an encouraging nudge affects journal comment rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a nudge to comment for journal page visitors will achieve an increase in journal comments.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2021 - 08/15/2021
CaringBridge had run an experiment that found that including a small, data-based nudge to comment would increase engagement on their journal pages. However, over multiple site iterations, the nudge language had changed and had been dropped from the experience in certain places.
They decided to re-test the original winning nudge copy against “no nudge” and the replacement copy that had been inserted at some point to see if they could lift overall engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The statistic-based nudge copy produced a valid 6% lift in journal comments—which seems like a small number, but at Compassion's scale, will lead to tens of thousands more comments each month.
This experiment is one of a series of tests to increase engagement and will be followed by experiments to increase followed sites.

experiments.title: How beginning with value proposition on a product page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Starting with value proposition will lead to more purchase conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/03/2021
FamilyLife resources asked us to optimize their acquisition for their small-group kit products. The pages were built to first show the reader the purchase option and a video. After the video it talked more about what was included. So the page led with the ultimate decision point and ended with why they should purchase the item.
We hypothesized that flipping this progression would lead to more acquisitions. So we altered the page to begin with why this kit was valuable, then tell the reader what it includes and then ask them to purchase it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page design led to an 46% increase in conversions, proving the hypothesis true that explaining value before the call to action is key in acquisition.
Future iterations of this test could include, reducing the navigation and the other product options below.

experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased donor conversion by 51%, which meant 23 more donations and $2,500 more dollars. We have a few hypotheses as to why this happened. First, it brought clarity to who the donation would help. It told the donor more of the story that they would be a part of. This also led to longer copy, that gave the donor more information, which could also have led to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion on website sticky bars
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Talking to constituents like friends and partners will increase their engagement and donations
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2020 - 12/28/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Missionary Oblates were running a sticky bar on their website promoting giving during year end. This sticky bar was placed across their website. Due to some other successes in talking to their constituents like partners in their mission, we wanted to test similar language on the year end sticky bar. So we tested the typical year-end language with elements of urgency against a more communal language with a more conversational tone that also pointed to the upcoming deadline to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased donor conversion from the sticky bar by 89%! There are a couple of factors behind this success:
The treatment spoke to website visitors as "friends". By recognizing them as such and even recognizing their prayers and support, we were able to draw people in and increase initial engagement. We see this by the fact that the click-through rate for the treatment increased by 53% with a 99.9% level of confidence. The treatment's call-to-action was softer and more conversational as well by saying, "If it's not too much to ask...". This approach aligned with the initial communal style and continued to reinforce this "friend" and "partnership" approach.
This experiment reinforces that communal language resonates well with this audience. Donors have even told the organization in the past that they see themselves as part of the mission. This presents more opportunities for testing this similar approach in other channels and deeper in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a more prominent callout combined with a more powerful value proposition affects new site signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the visibility of the CTA and the force of the value proposition for blog post visitors will achieve an increase in new site signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/17/2021
CaringBridge had engaged in a campaign to write more blog posts to help people in life situations who might be ideal prospects to start a CaringBridge site. However, on the blog posts themselves, the CTA was a small purple button that was inserted in different areas throughout the posts. The team thought that the button should be fully built out into a detached section that made a focused value proposition for starting a site and had a more powerful CTA.
They created a box that popped out visually from the main container, had a headline that addressed the audience segment directly, and listed a clear, concise value proposition for starting a new site. Finally, they centered the CTA around the user and highlighted that it was free, and used a lighter brand color to call more visual attention to the button.
They hypothesized that this would increase the number of new sites started, a critical metric for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 65.5% increase in new site signups, which showed the first step in converting more blog traffic to start new CaringBridge sites. Future tests will include more design differentiation, placement of the box, and augmenting it through sticky bars and popups.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying the value proposition for Facebook users will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2021
Compassion International was running ad to drive traffic to a campaign that offered users the ability to round up their purchases and donate the marginal “change”. The original campaign copy started with a high-level value proposition—”Poverty is complex. Generosity can be simple”. Then, it offered the user the chance to join millions of others who are donating change, and then explained the concept of RoundUp without naming the brand.
The team hypothesized that clarifying the value proposition by:
creating an ad that spoke more directly to the user on a 1:1 basis,
AND informed them of the impact they could personally make, 
AND connected the impact to a specific child, rather than a cause,
along with a “preview” of the brand name (RoundUp)
…would attract more clicks. They decided to test this with an ad in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ad produced a 134.2% increase in clickthrough rate—which showed that the clarified value proposition was more likely to attract a click than the control ad.
However—since all clicks are not created equal, downstream conversions (initial signups and "added credit cards") must also be validated to make sure that these clicks are equally motivated as the control group.

As the Christmas season was approaching, we knew people would be considering Adventures in Odyssey memberships as a gift and wanted to enhance the value proposition on the sign-up page to gift a membership. We produced a treatment that was seasonally relevant and reminded visitors of the value that would be included when they gave a membership. experiments.title: How communal language on a content offer donation page changes donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Using communal language, that includes the reader in the community of work, will increase donation conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
The Oblates were running a content offer that they give each year, we wanted to test communal language on the donation page. It was a communication style that we have tested with great success on sticky bars and have seen work well in emails to their donors, so the natural next step was to test it on the donation page. We took their typical copy, made it more conversational, welcomed the reader into their community of prayer, and care for their fellow men. The new copy showed how not only do the Oblates care most deeply and pray for the reader, but also that their work is an extension of the reader’s prayers and intentions. It also focused less on getting the premium for a gift, but rather allowed the premium to be a natural extension of their prayer as well and as a token of sincere appreciation for the work that they make possible through their giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language increased donor significantly, by 56%. We believe this is because readers feel like the integral part of the mission (that they are) and they can sense the community that they are joining really truly brings improves lives. And because of that, revenue also increased by 41% with a 97% level of confidence.
Both new and returning visitors appreciated the communal language, the returning more so with a 97% increase in giving. Which make sense because they have a closer relationship with the Oblates as the language in the copy suggests. However, it still welcomed new visitors in with a 37% increase in donations at an 88% directional level of confidence.
Next we would like to test the premium as a reminder of the good they have done with their prayers and their giving to the Oblates. As well as test this more fully in email and on content offer landing pages.

experiments.title: How adding seasonal relevance impacted sticky bar clicks

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial & Email Disclaimer - w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing relevancy of an offer for website visitors around the holidays will achieve increased free trial signups through the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2020 - 12/10/2020
Surrounding the Thanksgiving and Christmas holidays, we wanted to increase the appeal and relevancy of a free trial to families by indicating its usefulness for road trips and increased time at home. We pulled a testimonial to use as the treatment copy – “We have so enjoyed this free trial…long rides in the car have been great and we have included AIO in our bedtime routine. My boys love it.” and we ran it against the current testimonial with the strongest leading performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we discovered was the treatment—while seasonally relevant—decreased conversion rate by 17.3% with a 97.6% level of confidence. Our hypothesis for this decrease in results was due to the limiting factors that were introduced by the new copy. The control copy appeals to a wider audience as it mentions it is for the "whole family" and "kids 5 to 45" while the treatment copy only specifies that "My boys love it" and it may appear that the episodes are only valuable to listen to during travel or at bedtime instead of helpful on a daily basis regardless of the activity at hand. For future experiments, we will look to use testimonials that are seasonally relevant but that would still appeal to a wider audience rather than a limited segment.

Organic Search visitors had a 30.6% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 21.7% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 28.2% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects donor conversion from a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 58.3% increase in donor conversion rate. This is significant in that donor conversion increased at a higher rate than clickthrough rate (see that data here), which meant that it not only produced more clicks, it produced more motivated clicks. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.
First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".
This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.
This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

experiments.title: How ask amount affects donor conversion in an email fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a higher ask amount or a less specific “generous gift” ask increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2016 - 05/24/2016
Harvest Ministries sent their mid-month fundraising appeal and wanted to test the size of the gift ask in the copy. This appeal made the direct ask in the copy, and to mirror the direct mail, suggested a gift size of $35, $45, or more. They hypothesized that asking for a larger amount might increase average gift size.
As they were setting this test up, they came up with a new hypothesis — maybe the specific ask, though it worked in direct mail, was too specific. They developed a new hypothesis that asking for a “generous gift of any amount” would increase conversion and overall revenue.
They set up the A/B/C test to test the control against two treatments and closely monitored conversion to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email with the less-specific ask produced a 43.1% increase in donor conversion, showing that allowing donors to choose their own gift size, in this case, meant that they were more likely to become donors. This ask amount had proven to generate a higher clickthrough rate as well, but clickthrough is not always an indication of intent to give.

It is worth noting, as well, that the less-specific ask amount generated a higher average gift than the control.

experiments.title: How simplifying your ad impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the ad experience for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/17/2021
As a part of an open letter (or petition) promotion campaign, Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying an otherwise verbose and complicated open letter promotion.
They thought that by presenting a simple “Yes” or “No” question to the person the ad was being served to, that it could improve clickthrough and email signup rates, subsequently positively impacting donor conversion and revenue for the same dollar spent to advertise the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 96.2% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion by +64.4% by simplifying the ad experience in such a way that people would be presented with a simple "Yes" or "No" answer to a question, and would be called to take action today if they aligned with the organization (in this case "No").
In fact, we saw increases at every step of the funnel.
Here is the lift that we achieved across the treatment version of the campaign:
Ad CTR: +268.8% (LoC: 100%)Email Signup: +142.4% (LoC: 100%)Donor Conversion Rate: +64.4% (LoC: 96.2%)Revenue: +52.7% (LoC: 98%)
The results achieved with the simplified approach to the ad experience really started by dramatically decreasing the cost per click — showing a -80% decrease in cost per click while nearly quadrupling the number of clicks we saw against the control experience.
This higher volume of clicks for the same expense delivered similar more cost-effective results all of the way throughout the process, which boosted ROI by 100%, and decreased the cost to acquire an email by -69% and the cost to acquire a donor by more than -40%.
There was one more observation worth mentioning, as well. This approach had such an improvement in post engagement that it contributed to the lowering of costs for the outcomes we received. The question/answer-based approach created shares and comments that put this ad extended the reach of these campaigns. Specifically, we observed an 88% increase in post comments (LoC: 100%) due to this approach.
Where possible, experimenting with a very simple approach to issues related to a reader's beliefs in the form of a "Yes" or "No" question can potentially provide dramatic increases in clicks that the efficiency metrics on cost per outcome drop to levels not seen before.
It should be noted that Facebook has rejected some ad images that (like above) look like they have buttons on them. So, further experimentation may be required to use banners or something similar when/if future ads like this are rejected.

experiments.title: How intentional Facebook ad copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Intentional copy strategically addressing a reader’s difficult situation will convert more donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2021
As Focus on the Family was launching a new video series, we wanted to test some intentional language in their ad copy to see what resonates most with readers and bring not only more sign ups through the funnel, but more donors too. We tested two different styles of copy.
The first style is reader centered and experiential. It draws the reader in by expressing the emotions and experiences that they are living through and then giving them solutions to overcome the hard situation they are in.
The second style of copy is called Sliding Doors. It shows the reader two different pictures. The first picture is what happens if no intervention is taken, and the bad situations that could come of it. The second picture is what happens when the reader takes action and helps solve or prevent the difficult situation. Then the copy offers the reader a solution for taking action and intervening to make the positive outcome a reality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatments dramatically improved results. The Reader Centered copy lifted the donor conversion by 191%. The sliding doors copy lifted donor conversion by 268%. But it is much more interesting than that.

The click through rate for all three of the ads was relatively the same: Control - .82%; Reader Centered - 98%; Sliding Doors - 85%.

The email conversion rate for all three of the ads were similar as well: Control - 40%; Reader Centered - 34%; Sliding Doors - 39%.

But the instant conversion rate for the ads were dramatically different which led to these exceptional results: Control - 1.57%; Reader Centered - 4.91%; Sliding Doors - 5.88%. That's a 277% increase in instant donor conversion rate between the Control and Sliding Doors.

This means that even though there was not a dramatic difference in the percentage of people that the ads brought through the funnel, the people who came through the Reader Centered and Sliding Doors ads were much, much more motivated to give. The readers could see the real issue and need and then realized that that problem and need was something that could happen to them. The value prop of the donation ask reinforced that that problem doesn’t have to exist for others. They can not only get the content to help themselves but ensure that others don’t have to experience that either. And that inspired them to give.

experiments.title: How experiential language on a facebook ad impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that copy that reflects the experience of the reader for parents struggling in their relationship with their sons will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/09/2021
Focus on the Family had been running Recognizing your Sons Need for Respect video series for over a year with great success. We wanted to take some time to review how could we make this offer work even better. And so we tested new language around the feelings and experiences parents have when struggling with their relationship with their son. Our hypothesis was that by reflecting to them just how they feel and then offering them the tools they needed to make their relationship with their son better, that would increase signups and ultimately conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was that this ad brought more qualified donors through the funnel and increased donor conversion by 53%. It increased click through rate by 35% at a 100% level of confidence. The difference between the 35% increase in clicks and the 53% increase in donors, shows that those people who come to the page are more invested in this offer and ministry.
Our hypothesis is that when you speak to the deep feelings of a reader, and give them the solution they need to intentionally improve their relationships, then they are more likely to give to help others do the very same.
We are going to role out this treatment and continue to expound on it in the The next iteration of this experiment will be to test this kind of language on the landing page.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing value proposition under the donation button affect conversion on a premium acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding value proposition in the critical moment of decision will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2021 - 05/02/2021
FamilyLife was offer a premium book on parenting for a gift of any amount. The audience comes because of their interest in the book, but they also learn about how their gift to FamilyLife will help families that are broken or hurting. So we tested including that value prop underneath the donation submission button to see if in the moment of decision, reinforcing the fact that the donor doesn’t just get a book but helps families in critical need. This was run on a Facebook acquisition offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our hypothesis held true that reinforcing the value proposition in the key decision moment did convert more donors, 75% more were converted by the simple message below the button. This held true with all devices, new and returning visitors, and all demographics.
The next step is to roll this out to all premium acquisition pages and test this on donation pages.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of an ad impacted the motivation of donor traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “alternative to mainstream media” value proposition increase the number of donors from the site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2017 - 03/20/2017
The new “Alternative to Mainstream Media” value proposition was able to significantly increase donor conversion rate by 165%. Interestingly, the click through rate for the ad increased by only 12% (which was not statistically significant). This would imply that the ad was not necessarily more appealing but, instead, it produced a higher motivation for those that it did attract which made them more likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Dear Reader” call out on the DailySignal.com (TDS) website had become a significant source of donors for The Heritage Foundation. The message in the treatment highlighted the nonprofit nature of the work and the benefit it provided to the reader.
The organization had recently come under attack from some of the more mainstream media outlets due to TDS’s level of access within the White House. Using this attack, we decided to test out a new value proposition that depicted The Daily Signal is an alternative to the mainstream media. We also added urgency by mentioning the potential loss of access due to the attacks.

Live Action was running a test on their main donate button located on their homepage. They wanted to see if users responded better to a stronger, but still direct ask (Support Live Action), or a value proposition (Defend the Preborn). All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: “Support Live Action” produced a 24% lift in donations as opposed to “Defend the Preborn.” This is most likely because a more direct ask produced more highly motivated traffic than a softer ask with a value proposition.

experiments.title: How a defense-minded approach with an expanded value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a defense-minded approach with an expanded value proposition affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2020 - 08/08/2020
Hillsdale College wanted to test the copy on the donation page for their online courses site. The control copy was a positive message that put power in the donor’s hands—”You can help educate America”. They hypothesized that a stronger posture that explained the “why” behind the importance of education—defending liberty. The copy also did more to explain the problem to which Hillsdale’s educational efforts provides a solution, and gave extra descriptive copy of the premium. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the extended value proposition and defense-minded headline, decreased conversion rate by 11.5%. This showed that a positive message in this case (plus the benefit of shorter copy) was more likely to inspire a gift.
This could reflect the mindset of donors while they are on the course platform—they are not thinking about defense, but opportunity. Further testing might expand opportunity there.

CaringBridge receives the majority of their donations through individual site pages. However, we believed there was an opportunity to increase conversion on their main donation page. In its initial state, the donation page was just a donation form with the headline “Give to CaringBridge”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered a 128% increase in donor conversions with the additional value proposition copy added to the page. This would suggest that the visitors coming to this page were not 100% convinced to give a donation and required additional explanation before making a commitment. This same statement could be said for most donation pages.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition and increasing relevancy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the relevancy between advertisement and landing page, as well as increasing the force of the value proposition affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 01/02/2018
Compassion International placed a large advertising buy with BibleGateway and received millions of impressions per month. They sought to boost conversion from this display buy. The advertisement (see it here) asked if the viewer shared a birthday with a child in poverty, and let them select their birthday. The user was then placed on a Compassion landing page with the headline “Sponsor a Child Today”. Below that headline was a small-font subheadline that showed that the results were children who matched the selected birthday. From then on, it followed their standard grid design showing many children who shared that day.
They had developed a page designed to do four things:
Reduce page exits. The new page removed the top navigation.
Increase the force of the value proposition. Rather than “sponsor a child today”, the new page asked the user to “Give a child the best birthday ever—a new sponsor!” with confetti and streamers.
Increase relevancy between the ad and the landing page. The new page was birthday-relevant both in design and copy, clarifying the connection between the ad action (selecting a birthday) and the next step (selecting a child who shares that birthday).
Reduce decision friction. Instead of row after row of equally-weighted children, the new page selected one child and featured them at the top, before offering other options below.
They launched an A/B test that sent an equal amount of traffic to each page to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page produced a 214% lift in conversion—showing that multiple factors (a radical redesign) were able to increase the connection between ad and landing page, keep more people on the page, convince more people to give, and simplify the decision process.
While we don’t know the specific contribution of each of these tested elements, we have a significant lift in conversion (and subsequent lift in revenue). This test immediately prompts a similar test on the in-site birthday selector to see if it produces similar results there (where there is much more traffic).

experiments.title: How will the presence of a value proposition banner in checkout affect conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “sticky banner” with any value proposition outperform a control with no banner?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2019
Compassion International has a three-step checkout process to sponsor a child. They have long known that there is a drop-off rate for each step of the process, and have wanted to optimize that—knowing that people who have already selected a child have a high motivation and should be more likely to complete their transaction. One thing they noticed is that once a donor entered the shopping cart, there wasn’t much value proposition copy to motivate them through the transaction. They had seen tests that showed that a value proposition “sticky banner” in the process increased conversion rates for donations—even on high-conversion-rate pages. They planned to test different value proposition messages, but also wanted to aggregate the data to determine if the presence of a banner with any message increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: A banner with any message increased conversion by 13.7%. Most of these increases were realized on desktop, though mobile had no decreases and no banner message had less than a 10% lift. This confirmed Compassion's hypothesis that adding value proposition to the checkout process could increase motivation to complete the three-step process. Next, they looked at a different dataset to determine which messages drove the highest increases.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition for new subscribers through ad service during the welcome series increased downstream donor conversion rate

rows.name: Reinforced Welcome Series (with value proposition ads for duration)

experiments.researchQuestion: Would running brand value proposition ads over welcome series participants improve the new subscriber’s likelihood to convert into a first time donor for the organization?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2019
We wanted to experiment with the value of reinforcing Americans for Prosperity’s value proposition for new subscribers as they participated in the New Subscriber Welcome Series to see if it would have any impact upon their program’s ability to convert new subscribers into first time donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.4% level of confidence, we observed an increase of +760% in downstream donor conversion when compared to the welcome series that did not also include brand value proposition ads running to welcome series participants.
Another very interesting observation we made with this experiment was how the treatment segment performed when measuring the time it took for the organization to secure the first gift from each audience.
The control segment (who received no ads during the welcome series) took an average 29.15 days to secure the first gift. Meanwhile, the treatment segment (who did receive ads during the welcome series) took an average of 17.55 days to secure the first gift.
This means that the treatment segment approach cut the time it took to convert a first time donor by 40%.
Finally, we also observed that the treatment segment's average first gift was +11.5% higher than the control segment's average first gift amount (note: the level of confidence on this portion of the experiment did not validate, but did achieve a 91.6% level of confidence, implying that it would have likely validated if the experiment continued).
To summarize: The value proposition reinforcement ads over welcome series messages being sent to new subscribers (not new donors):
Increased the likelihood that someone would eventually convert into a first time donor,Increased the speed by which they made that decision, and alsoIncreased the amount they decided to give for their first gift to the organization.
Some other things to note about the experience for the experiment is noted below:
The incremental expense per person in the treatment experience increased costs by an average of about $1.50 in ads per participant included in the sample set.The campaign settings were on a fixed daily spend, rather than on a per participant level, which means that the average number of impressions per person in the treatment segment came out to 11.8 impressions per matched contact.There were no standard weekly (or monthly) direct solicitations sent to either audience for the duration of the experiment.There were no high urgency or seasonal fundraising campaigns that included direct solicitations sent to either audience for the duration of the experiment.
Final Recommendation(s):
We would advise relaunching a second experiment that controls costs for the treatment group and looks to cut the cost/participant down by more than 50% of what we actually spent (where we would be targeting around $0.40 per participant in incremental ad services expenses) to bring the number of ad impressions down closer to about 5/participant for the duration of the experiment.We would advise exploring ways to generate a more even split in the segments.
Once these elements are implemented, we would advise that we re-run the experiment to see what this decreased investment does in terms of ad impression counts, as well as downstream performance related to downstream donor conversion, time to secure the first gift and average first gift metrics.
Further, we advise continuous monitoring of these newly converted donors for reevaluation periodically over the next 12-months to see what, if any, difference there is as it pertains to gift frequency, increase in giving amounts, and/or retention of these donors long-term, as well.
In light of these findings, however, it certainly seems advisable to install value proposition reinforcement ads over your new subscriber welcome series of emails, as it has a valid lift in downstream donor conversion rates and increases the speed in which the first time donor converts as opposed to the emails alone.
