This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of homepage pop-up messaging on donor acquisition. The goal was to determine whether leaning into urgency and future challenges would outperform messaging focused on WUNC’s lack of a paywall.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the donation button increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the homepage donate button will achieve more transactions because the added value proposition will reinforce the ‘why’.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could optimize the ‘Donate Now’ button that is on their homepage. To do so, we added some value prop language inside the button itself that will help reinforce the the ‘why’ to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition to the homepage donate button actually led to a decrease in donations, contrary to the initial hypothesis. However, the results are not statistically significant with a confidence level of 80.3%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

Moving forward, it is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the value proposition language before drawing any conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that future experiments reach a statistically significant level of confidence (at least 95%) in order to make informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Will adding the value proposition to the mobile number field harm conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition under the mobile number field for the main episodic donation page form will achieve more transactions because the added value proposition will lead into trust, transparency and credibility with the organization.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of phone numbers that donors are giving us on the episodic donation page by adding some value proposition copy under it. In doing so, we also wanted to see if by adding the copy, it could end up increasing trust and transparency within the organization and result in more transactions.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition under the mobile number field for the main episodic donation page did not yield the intended results. In fact, there was a significant decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 85.6%. This indicates that the control version without the added value proposition performed better in terms of transactions.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of any changes made to donation pages before implementing them broadly. It is also crucial to conduct thorough testing and analysis to ensure that any modifications do not have a negative impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different value propositions or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with donors.

Further testing is recommended to validate the results of this experiment and to gain a better understanding of the factors that influence donor behavior on the donation page. By conducting additional experiments and gathering more data, Care Net can make informed decisions about how to optimize their donation pages for increased donations and donor trust in the future.

experiments.title: How updating the value prop on a donation page with urgency and relevancy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and relevancy to the value proposition for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in the instant donation coversion because there will be more urgency and relevancy making the donor feel more connected to Hoover’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society.
Our hypothesis was that if we update the instant donation form copy to be more related to an immediate need, then the urgency will cause an increase in the instant donation conversion. If we update the value proposition with urgency and relevancy to the times it will encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the instant donation form copy to include urgency and relevancy to a current need resulted in a significant increase in instant donation conversions for the Hoover Institution. The treatment group experienced a 42.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 87.7%.

Why the Test May Have Won:
Stronger Immediate Relevance & Urgency: 
 	The test opens with, "Before you go, we’d like to ask you one more thing," which feels more immediate and action-oriented than the control’s "While you wait for it to arrive."
 	It emphasizes the present need for scholars like Thomas Sowell to continue their work, connecting the ask to an ongoing, timely cause.
 	Clearer Value Proposition:
 	The test provides concrete examples of what donations will support: "Examine the causes of current crises," "Enable our team of expert scholars," and "Advance a strategic communications program."
 	The control remains more abstract, focusing broadly on "data-driven research" and "an educated citizenry."
 	More Compelling Call to Action:
 	The test includes a clear call to action: "Will you consider making a tax-deductible gift to the Hoover Institution today?" followed by bullet points that break down the impact of a gift.
 	The control’s call to action feels less direct and more passive with phrases like, "Would you partner with us?"
 	Focus on Impact Over Philosophy:
 	The test copy shifts the focus toward what Hoover does and the tangible outcomes of its work.
 	The control talks more about philosophical beliefs, like "the importance of an educated citizenry" and "the American way of life."

experiments.title: Does adding a donor testimonial work on an evergreen donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the goaa/soaa donation page during CYE will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”. because  having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/05/2025
For CYE this year, we wanted to see if adding a right rail donor testimonial on the donation pages would make an impact on transactions. We wanted to test this on all three major donation pages for Focus, the main campaign episodic giving page, the main campaign recurring giving page, and the GOAA/SOAA broadcast pages that generate a ton of traffic each day.
For this test, we wanted to see how adding the testimonal on the the GOAA/SOAA donation pages would impact conversion. We thought this would be neat to see more of an ‘evergreen’ type of donation page even during a high urgency campaign
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the GOAA/SOAA donation page during CYE did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 15.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.8%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different types of donor testimonials or placement of the testimonials on the page to see if there are any variations that could lead to increased conversions. It is also important to take into consideration the timing of the experiment and the specific campaign or context in which it is being conducted to ensure that the results are relevant and applicable.

experiments.title: How exclusivity language on a high-urgency pop-up impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including exclusivity language for users on the RTB website will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights the donor’s identification with a group, beyond just being a donor..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s first-ever Giving Day, we hypothesized that the inclusion of exclusivity language — highlighting that the donor is joining a unique or exclusive group of donors to the first RTB Giving Day — will improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including exclusivity language on the RTB website did lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

While the initial findings are promising and suggest that exclusivity language may indeed impact donor conversion rate positively, we should not make any definitive conclusions or implement permanent changes based on these results alone. Further testing with a larger sample size and longer duration is needed to validate the impact of exclusivity language on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts lapsed donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing value prop for lapsed donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
- The experiment aimed to test the impact of reinforcing the value proposition for lapsed donors in an email during WUNC's Fall Fund Drive.
- The treatment group, which received the additional call-out reinforcing WUNC's value proposition, showed a significant increase in clicks for all traffic, with an 81.8% improvement.
- The statistical level of confidence was 89.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, but further testing is recommended for certainty.
-Moreover, while there were only 3 gifts total, the addition of the value prop did increase conversion rates by 100%.

experiments.title: Will aligning the value proposition from the donate button and the episodic giving page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the value propostion from the donate button to the doantion page for main episodic donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because the congruency between the button and the donation page will decrease friction and reinforce motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 11/01/2024
In this series of testing we have found that using Biblical Resources on the main donate button increases the number of clicks to the donation page.
We saw that aligning the value proposition with the main donation button on the monthly page increased transactions by 154%.
We wondered if we could duplicate those results for the episodic donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the value proposition from the donate button to the donation page for the main episodic donation page led to a 13.7% increase in transactions for all traffic. This indicates that having congruency between the button and the donation page can potentially decrease friction and reinforce motivation for users to make donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of aligning value propositions on donation pages to see if this trend holds true across different scenarios. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied approaches to alignment could help validate these findings further and provide more conclusive evidence. It is important to note that while the results of this experiment are encouraging, they do not meet the standard threshold of 95% statistical confidence, so further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.

experiments.title: How positive value-focused headlines affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing positive value-focused headlines for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
The Navigators had been trying to increase email acquisition rate on a offer they had that was doing very well in acquisition. The headline on the page offered the value proposition “Get the help you need to memorize scripture”. They hypothesized that this might imply that the visitor needed help, which might be off-putting to them.
So they decided to try to new approaches. First, they wanted to ask a question that implied that the visitor wanted to gain more knowledge: Do you want to memorize more scripture?
Then, they decided to test a headline that teased the future “superpower” of the visitor after they had absorbed the content: “Be equipped with the right scripture at the right time.”
They tested all three with the torrent of Facebook traffic that the page was receiving.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither headline was able to validate, with more than 21,000 conversions. This was partially because the motivation from the Facebook ad was very high and the email conversion on the page was more than 35%. However, it also showed that the changes might not have been radical enough to warrant a large difference. In addition to testing upstream and downstream of this page, they wanted to next test a more radical approach.

experiments.title: How reminding lapsed members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can significantly increase the click-through rate for lapsed members. The treatment in this experiment saw a 41.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, indicating that reminding members of the benefits they receive can be a compelling reason for them to donate again.

Note: While the treatment email had a higher click-through rate, it also had a slightly lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test and refine the messaging around the value proposition of a membership card. This could include highlighting specific benefits, such as exclusive content, special discounts, or access to events. By effectively communicating the value that members receive in exchange for their donation, we can increase the likelihood of them re-engaging with the organization.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to further test different variations of the value proposition messaging to optimize the impact on member reactivation. By continuing to experiment and analyze the results, we can uncover the most effective ways to encourage lapsed members to donate and renew their support for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for lapsed members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership for lapsed members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what lapsed members will lose by not reactivating their membership can be a powerful motivator for increasing donor conversion rates. The treatment in which the loss aversion approach was used saw a 105.3% increase in donations compared to the control with an 89% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to refine the messaging and presentation of the loss aversion approach to optimize its impact. Testing different variations, such as emphasizing specific benefits or highlighting the potential negative consequences of not reactivating, could help identify the most compelling approach.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the characteristics and preferences of lapsed members who responded positively to the loss aversion strategy. Understanding their motivations and demographics can inform targeted marketing efforts to similar segments in the future.

Overall, this experiment suggests that leveraging loss aversion can be a successful strategy to reactivate lapsed members. Continued testing and refinement will provide more conclusive evidence and insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate (Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity for site visitors  will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate  because it reduces anxiety and increase the perceived value of what they are receiving by subscribing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate. We ran this test once and saw a lift in acquisition rate among returning visitors but a decrease among new visitors. The initial test had header language that encouraged people to commit to disciplemaking. Our hypothesis was that it was too large of a commitment for new site visitors to make. So we kept the spirit of the test (adding clarity) and changed the headline to be more accessible for people new to the Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a directional 30% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 89%. While not a statistically valid result, it affirms our hypothesis that the initial header may have been too much of a commitment for newer site visitors. New visitors experienced a 33.2% increase in name acquisition with a level of confidence of 87.7%. Using the new header and continuing to add clarity in the CTA copy resulted in an increase in names acquired. In fact, new visitors experienced the larger impact in name acquisition.

experiments.title: How unique value propositions impacted clicks for a direct ask sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the giving motivation of equipping the next generation of Christian leaders for website visitors will achieve an increase in click conversions because that value will resonate the strongest with our audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/30/2023
DTS is amid their fiscal year end fundraising campaign. With 15 days remaining, we want to add a website sticky bar as an additional revenue opportunity. We’ve built two different sticky bars unique in value proposition. The control uses the value proposition of “reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible” and the control used the value proposition of “equipping the next generation of Christian leaders.” We want to see which value resonates the strongest with our website audience and generates the most clicks to the FYE donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment sticky bar resulted in a 33.3% decrease in clicks with a nearly valid 90.6% level of confidence. The treatment produced 22 clicks compared to 52 to the control.
We noticed the treatment heading in the wrong direction so we ended the experiment after 11 days in order to send 100% of traffic to the control variant.
It seems the control value proposition of "reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible" resonates the most with our online audience. There are other factors to consider with the experiment, however.
The treatment led with "SPECIAL OPPORTUNITY" while the control led with "DEADLINE APPROACHING". Perhaps the control was more effective in this case as it notified users of an important deadline immediately. The treatment lead-in is much more broad which could lead users to overlook it or move casually past it. Also worth noting is the treatment mentioned the Challenge Gift amount of $600,000 while the control only referenced the Challenge Gift without stating the amount.
It's possible that including the $600k amount turned visitors off as they may have felt as if their modest gift would not make a significant difference in the overall goal.
These are all isolated elements that would be worth testing into to get more refined results. Though, in this high urgency scenario, we wanted to act fast and get our best two sticky bars copy versions out to the public.

experiments.title: How donation page value proposition led by evidentials impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that angling donation page value proposition based on evidentials for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain a better grasp of the problem and become more motivated to contribute to the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI has launched a state fiscal responsibility survey that culminates with a donation opportunity once someone completes the survey. We want to test two donation pages each with a different approach.
The control will test into clarity: Painting a picture for potential donors of why we put together this survey for them, what the plan is behind it, and why their help is needed.
The treatment will test into evidentials, alerting potential donors to the extent of the problem by pointing to multiple facts and research. We want to see which approach resonates best with them and leads to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control approach of painting a clear picture and explaining the "why" for potential donors is more effective in achieving donations compared to the treatment approach led by evidentials.

The treatment resulted in a 68.0% decrease in donations for All Traffic with a nearly valid 93.0% statistical level of confidence. Revenue also saw a 29% decrease through the treatment with a 66% level of confidence.

Donors appreciated the clarity of the control which gave them a clear understanding of why we're offering this survey, how it will help improve Illinois fiscal responsibility, and why we need their help. One possible reason that donors did not respond as well to the treatment is because they already have a firm understanding of the problem at hand and did not need the additional background or context.

We should keep these experiment results in mind as we build value proposition for future offer donation pages. It may be wise to test into these approaches again to gain more data. But in this instance, clarity seems to be the key!

experiments.title: How adding value proposition leading into a survey impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page will increase email acquisition rates because it will provide visitors with more clarity and appeal on the impact of their survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2023 - 06/18/2023
IPI’s School Choice Survey is producing a low 14.4% sign up rate. In an effort to acquire more emails and increase this metric, we’ve built a treatment that adds value proposition copy leading into the survey.
The current survey page includes just two short sentences leading into the survey. Our analysis dictates that these sentences feel transactional, lack personability, and due not do a good job of expressing the value to someone for taking and completing the survey.
That’s why we’ve built a treatment that we believe does those things. We added a call-to-action headline, focused the copy on the reader, and communicated the value and purpose of the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page has a significant impact on email acquisition rates. The treatment with a call-to-action headline, personalized copy, and clear communication of the value and purpose of the survey led to a 24.7% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 82.5% statistical level of confidence.
People are much more likely to complete the action you want them to complete when you express value and the "why" behind the request. If they don't have good reasons to take that action, then they're less likely to take it. By expressing value and focusing on the reader through "you" language, we got more people to complete the survey!
It's also worth noting the the treatment variant led to two donations through the subsequent instant donation page compared to none from the control variant.
We will roll out the treatment variant to the page given the direction lift in email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for study guide enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
For this theological seminary client, we tested if adding more detailed copy to fund the next biblical resource can increase donor conversion rate on the current Revelation Study Guide donation page.
The control donation page copy shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the following biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”. We have seen success with previous experiments that go deeper into their copy about what they are offering, which is why we believe this treatment will lead to a greater donor conversion rate.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed copy on the funding of the next resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 66.2% decrease in conversion rate with a 90.4% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include an ebook mockup image on the donation page so that viewers can physically see what their donation is going towards and be more motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How a slide-out with value prop affected Co-author sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prominently featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author will achieve an increase in Co-Author shares because the slide-out not only educates on the value, it makes it easier to share..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge allows users to share their page access with a friend or family member—making them a Co-Author on their CaringBridge page. We hypothesized that making this a more prominent feature on the user’s homepage, would not only help inform them what a Co-Author is—but would also increase the sign-up/share rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main finding of the experiment is that featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author led to a statistically significant increase in the rate of sending invitations to co-authors.
However, the level of confidence suggests that further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of the slide-out feature in promoting co-author shares and to identify the conditions and segments where it works best. Future experiments could focus on refining the design of the slide-out or testing different formats or placements to assess their impact on co-author shares.

experiments.title: How prioritizing value proposition on an instant donation page’s header impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because we are reducing anxiety by focusing on “fund” language and giving people a clear call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2023 - 04/30/2023
This experiment serves as a follow up a previous experiment (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/8GVA) where we moved the transactional fund ID language from the header. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
When we initially ran the experiment we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that there were several factors that contributed to the decrease in donations:
The change headline actually made the “thank you” line not seem like a an actual header. 
Though the fund ID sounds transactional – the first sentence says “Support the Navigators” and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they’re supposed to do on that page 
Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
So we ran a followup experiment where we ran 2 treatments.
The first treatment enlarged the “thank you” line so that it looked like an actual header. The second treatment enlarged the copy and  added a transitional sentence to let people know that this was not just a confirmation page, nor is it the end of the interaction. This transitional copy served as a replacement for the “Support the Navigators” copy and had more value prop-based language.
This was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors can achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. By providing a clear call to action in the header and removing the transactional language (fund ID), the treatment resulted in a 60% directional lift in donations for all traffic, with a 92.0% statistical level of confidence.
Results indicate that the font size of the header had no real impact on conversion rate but when paired with the transitional sentence we saw a significant impact. Our initial hypothesis was correct in that it's likely that ebook downloaders believed that this donation page was actually a confirmation page with no further action to be taken. Additionally, the use of more value based language ("do you want to make resources like this available to others who want to grow in their faith") inspired more people to take action and refocuses people on the "why" of their gift.

experiments.title: Which value prop is more affective in a sponsorship ask for PFI–religion or justice?
experiments.researchQuestion: An appeal to someone’s sense of justice will work better at converting PFI’s audience into sponsors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2020 - 12/02/2020
On Giving Tuesday, PFI was going to send out a sponsorship ask to their general donors. Since they are a religious organization that also deals with the justice system, we wanted to test whether an appeal based on religious reasons or on the basis of justice was more motivating.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have enough results to validate, and need to be careful in using such a small scaled response as proof that the religious element is more motivating. We will continue to test as their file grows.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a high urgency campaign website sticky bar impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to sticky bar copy promoting a key fundraising opportunity for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition will appeal more to potential donors and motivate them to learn more and make a gift through the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2023 - 03/31/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute was running a high urgency campaign through the end of March. To alert website visitors and get more people to the campaign donation page, we’re testing two sticky bars: One that is very brief and simply alerts users of an urgent matching gift and campaign deadline, and the other includes more value proposition copy, appealing to issues that the user likely cares about and telling them how they can learn more about a way to help – while also pointing out the urgency of a deadline.
Which angle will produce more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky bar copy can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment version that included more value proposition copy saw a 304.6% increase in donations, with a statistical level of confidence of 94.4%.

All donations completed in this experiment were completed by desktop visitors and returning visitors. It is clear that the treatment sticky bar (with value prop) spoke more effectively to visitors through appeal and motivation. A "matching gift" does not mean as much to our audience as improving certain issues that they are passionate about.

Future sticky bars run for high urgency campaigns should include value proposition language, as that's what our audience wants to hear. It may also be worth testing variations of the treatment version to further optimize the messaging and achieve an even greater increase in donations.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page will increase donations by providing more “reasons to give” to prospective donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2022 - 01/21/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation by improving the “reason why” they should give.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed 12.5% decrease in donations from traffic that was redirected to the treatment donation page with more value proposition language. The low sample size of the experiment prevented us from validating these results, however, it does not appear that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page motivated more people to give and may even have demotivated visitors from giving.
And while we cannot draw statistically valid conclusions about these results, it is worth noting that this outcome may have been influenced by user motivation. Specifically, after reviewing results, we are led to hypothesize that because someone who clicks a "make a donation" button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to "stay out of their way" by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
This suspicion is supported by the fact that "new visitors" who we may assume would be the exact segment that would benefit most from increasing the "reason to give" gave 28% less (86% level of confidence) when visiting the treatment with longer copy than they did when they visited the control with minimal copy above the donation form.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition copy on a high urgency donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the value proposition for potential donors on a monthly appeal page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 10/01/2022
The Boy Scouts of America ran a monthly appeal campaign in September that consisted of one email and one follow-up to that email. The first email only received two donations therefore we needed to test changing the language on the donation page to see if this would increase donor conversion rate.
We felt like the donation page copy on the original page was too long to read therefore causing friction when someone landed on this page to potentially convert.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 43.7% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of significance of 84.9%.
After sending the follow-up appeal that had more urgency within the copy and was shortened compared to the first email, we believed that the issue actually laid within the email content of the first email, not the original donation page. Therefore, by removing a lot of the value proposition from the treatment donation page, users coming from the second email did not have enough value given to them at this step in the funnel in order to convince them to make a donation.
Next, we would like to test keeping the email copy shorter that way we're more likely to get users to the donation page where they can be presented with more value proposition.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding Value Prop language to sign up form impacted emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding value prop for website visitors will achieve increased emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 09/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on emails acquired through the sign up form on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 25.0% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 13.7% decrease in email acquired with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 22.1% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 56.3% decrease in email acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How will adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form affect acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form will increase email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2022 - 07/28/2022
In an effort to increase email acquisition rates for a public policy client, we wanted to test the impact of adding language that illustrates the value of signing up to receive email communications on email acquisition rates.
We tested this concept on a pop-up form that appears after website visitors click a “get updates’ button on the header of their site. The “control” version is a plain form with no headline or body copy above the form fields.
The treatment adds a headline and body copy to reinforce the value subscribers receive by signing up for email communications. We set this experiment up as an A/B test, splitting traffic evenly between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 34 days, we observed a 60.8% lift in email acquisition rates, with an 81.7% level of confidence. And encouragingly, new site visitors experienced a 284% lift in form submission, with a 94% level of confidence.

And while we were unable to collect enough samples to validate our hypothesis, the results of this experiment suggest that conveying the value of signing up for email communications may provide prospects with more motivation to share their email address. As data privacy becomes increasingly important to internet users ... and as inboxes become stuffed with more and more junk and graymail, it becomes increasingly important for organizations to place the value of signing up for your email communications front and center.

While reviewing the data we did realize that more than one concept was tested in the treatment version of the form. Not only was value proposition language added, but the form fields were also reduced in an effort to reduce friction. And while it always feels great to improve performance, testing these two concepts concurrently makes it impossible to know with certainty which of these changes produced the observed lift, and which to what degree. However, due to the encouraging results of this experiment, we decided to roll the treatment version out to 100% of traffic going forward.

experiments.title: Does adding value proposition language affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign-ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared calmer and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.

Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How a yes/no pop up with value proposition language impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value prop based pop up  for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate during calendar year end.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila wanted to try an exit intent pop ups in lieu of a homepage takeover for the last few days of the campaign. There was concern that constituents who come to their site for content, may not want to contribute to a campaign. So we decided to test the language in the yes/no exit intent pop up. The control asked the site visitor if they wanted to donate to Avila to support their on-going efforts. While the treatment would have value proposition based language, asking the site visitor if they want to bring spiritual guidance to those looking for truth.
Site to their page was organic and driven by the blog content and resources they continuously provide to their constituents.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results indicate that Avila's site visitors were more likely to give to their calendar year end campaign when presented with a yes/no pop up that identified Avila's key value proposition. Without having any ask attached to the copy, that audience segment produced a 3.1% conversion rate resulting in a 125% increase in donor conversion rate with level of confidence of 94.8%. This tells us that Avila's audience responds to their core mission and cause over just being asked for a donation. It is recommended that this concept is retested outside of calendar year end in order to determine if this is also true in a different context.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition impacted email signups for a newsletter – Part 1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal and clarity for a weekly newsletter offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 03/27/2022
In looking at ways to acquire more email addresses through a weekly newsletter offer, we looked at the current value prop offered on the landing page. With just a brief headline on the control, we saw an opportunity to develop a treatment that would provide additional language to increase specificity and exclusivity in order to get more people to subscribe. We developed a version with more appeal, outlining the benefits to the reader and giving them more details about the newsletter’s contents. We split email traffic to this page from the new subscriber welcome series.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 3 weeks, we observed a directional decrease of 19.9% in email acquisition with a 92% level of confidence. Specifically, we saw valid decreases for desktop visitors and email visitors. Because we had already "made the sale" in the body copy of the email where our visitors were arriving from for why someone should subscribe, we believe the additional value proposition was actually causing unnecessary friction on the page. We would like to run a similar test in the future to see how this would impact visitors from other channels or calls to action in other website placements that do not lead with clarity and value proposition like the email experience provides.

experiments.title: How a stronger value proposition in the website footer affects email acquisition

rows.name: Value Prop Angle – Tested and Proven Strateiges

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity to the website footer opt-in value proposition for website visitors will achieve greater email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2021 - 02/10/2022
In this experiment, we wondered if users were really understanding the value that they would get from signing up for NextAfter emails. We hypothesized that a clearer and more specific value proposition would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer and more specific value proposition in the footer led to a 33% increase in email signups over a 90 day period - reaching a 94% level of confidence. And it appeared to have the most significant impact on new visitors who would be learning about NextAfter for the first time.
This is a great experiment to further confirm the need for clear articulation of a value proposition - especially when every other company, business, and organization has a "newsletter" as well. There is a lot of noise out there about best practices and training for nonprofit marketers and fundraiser. We must always articulate why our offerings are both different and better.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 28.5% increase in footer subscribe with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 42.3% increase in footer subscribe with a 94% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had a 194.4% increase in footer subscribe with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How specificity in the value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity to the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2021 - 08/06/2021
Prison Fellowship International was running a campaign to acquire donors for their Lamplighter program, which told prisoners about Jesus through a Bible study. The page they had developed  had the headline “Bring Light Into the Darkness”. Since the audience they were targeting was primarily Christian, they wondered if they should be more specific and give the donor the opportunity to “Share Jesus with a Prisoner in Darkness”.
They developed this new headline and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though it did not validate, the new copy produced a 23% decrease in conversion rate. There are a few possible reasons for this:
First, the new copy, while clearer, might be less engaging to the reader and not bring them into the body copy as much. Second, the new value proposition might not align with the rest of the page. Finally, it might come across to the reader as negative to cast the "prisoner" as someone "in darkness".
Ultimately, this experiment was directional and slow to validate, so more radical approaches must be tested to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects email acquisition on a homepage ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition that conveys more urgency and focuses on the revenue at stake for homepage visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2020 - 12/30/2020
In this experiment, we were promoting a new ebook focused on fundraising ideas that can help increase revenue during the last week of the year. We had two main ideas as to how to message the advertisement.
In version A, we used the homepage ad itself to illustrate how fundraisers can use a similar homepage ad to promote their year-end campaign.
In version B, the messaging emphasis focused on the urgency around the biggest giving week of the year being right around the corner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn't quite hit our desired 95% level of confidence, but the result was a strong indicator that it was more relevant to emphasize the urgency around the last week of the year – leading to a 50% increase in conversions. As noted below, it was especially effective for website visitors that already had some affinity towards us and our content.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 77.7% increase in all form submissions with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 213.3% increase in all form submissions with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 66.7% increase in all form submissions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign for two days as part of their holiday appeal. They had previously tested a sticky bar during a previous match that employed “communal” language (as opposed to transactional language) that increased both clickthrough rate and conversion rate during the match. They wanted to extend that language to their tribute donation pages, where people can give in honor of a specific journal author.
The original page positioned CaringBridge as the hero—it helped the author stay connected to family and friends. Then, it positioned the reader as the one who makes that happen. Instead, they decided to double down on language that addressed the prospective donor as the hero, removing CaringBridge entirely. First, they changed the headline to thank the donor for directly helping the author stay connected to their loved ones. Then, they reinforced that (and introduced CaringBridge) by adding exclusivity to the subheadline.
Finally, they applied the communal language theory to the call-to-action that mentioned the match. The original CTA offered a “dollar for dollar” match if the donor gave—which appeared to be transactional language. It also brought the brand “CaringBridge” back into the conversation. They removed the brand name from the CTA and provided a “pressure release valve” that put the power of whether to participate or not into the hands of the donor. Finally, they removed the “dollar for dollar” language and launched a test to see if their hypothesis was proven correct.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language did not appear to have a significant impact overall. However, when the team dug into the device types, they found that desktop visitors—which made up the majority of donations—showed a 20.2% increase in conversion rate. On top of this increase in conversion rate, desktop visitors showed a 29.6% increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Not only did more people give—they gave larger amounts!

This showed the potential impact of replacing transactional language with communal language. Next, the CaringBridge team wanted to re-test this language in a non-match scenario to see if it would have year-round impact.

experiments.title: How more succinct copy affects total revenue on a homepage takeover
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing more succinct value proposition copy for homepage takeover visitors will achieve more sales and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2020 - 12/31/2020
During the last week of the year, we ran a homepage takeover to promote our last week of the year sale: 50% off any course, membership, or NIO Summit ticket.
After creating the control, we wondered if shortening the copy to be more succinct (without removing any of the core reasons to purchase) could actually lead to more sales and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although on the surface it appears that the treatment saw a big lift in revenue, the number of transactions are the same and only a single transaction made the difference in revenue. Pairing that with a small sample size, we can't really say that shorter copy was better performing with any confidence.

experiments.title: How breaking your fundraising goal down into a smaller ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting a specific goal of donors and targeted gift amount for campaign prospects will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2021
During the Leadership Institute’s “2021 Kickoff” campaign, we wanted to experiment with asking donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific gift amount to see if it would increase the donor’s likelihood to give, or their generosity altogether.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 88.9% level of confidence, we observed a revenue increase of +52.1%.

This was driven by an increase in the donor conversion rate of +24% (LoC: 73%) and an average gift that was 22.6% higher than gifts made from the control segment.

Although this didn't fully validate, the results were directionally sound, which means that through the course of a longer campaign and more gifts, we could expect to see this hold true in 17.7 of 20 times that we ran this experiment.

When looking at what donors gave against what they were asked for, we noticed some interesting facts, which are:
40% of treatment gifts received gave more than what we asked for, representing 43% of the dollars raised in treatment experience.
 	38% of the treatment gifts received gave exactly what we asked for, representing 37.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
 	22% of the treatment gifts received gave less than what we asked for, representing 19.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
74% more donors decided to give "more than" their MRC when in the treatment experience.
 	These donors produced 86.5% more revenue when in the treatment experience than donors in the control experience.
 	3x as many donors decided to make their largest gift ever through the treatment experience. And,
 	This represented a 257% increase in revenue from the treatment segment who decided to give a gift to this campaign that was higher than their HPC value when compared to those in the control segment.
When a donor is left to their own decision on how much to give, they often choose to give less than they could — so we should tell them exactly what we want them to give in this sort of way.

experiments.title: How prequalifying stickybar audiences to a campaign impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that buyer language used on-site to promote a campaign for web visitors will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2021
The Fund for American Studies was running on-site stickybar and home page popup promotions for their Founders’ Day Campaign and wanted to experiment with whether “selling the click” or “selling the donation” would be most effective for their audience visiting the website.
We decided to call these treatments “shopper” language (to sell the click) and “buyer” language (to sell the post-click donation).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the deadline approaching for the campaign, we decided to take the version of the stickybar/popups that was showing a lead for the remaining few days.

We did observe some other statistics that are worth noting:
Ad clickthrough rate on the "buyer" version of the ad copy did see valid decrease during the valuation period — showing 39.1% less clicks (LoC: 99%).
 	However, because the fewer people clicking on the "buyer" version of the ad did convert (as shown above) at a higher rate, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate after they clicked the on-site ads.
 	Combining more donors (as noted previously), the "buyer" version of the ad produced a more generous donor to the campaign — showing an increase of +18.4% increase in the size of the average gift.
 	Because of that higher donor total and a higher average gift, the revenue for the "buyer" ads actually was a lift of +183% (LoC: 93.6%).

experiments.title: How breaking your fundraising goal down into a smaller ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting a specific goal of donors and targeted gift amount for campaign prospects will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2021
During the Leadership Institute’s “2021 Kickoff” campaign, we wanted to experiment with asking donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific gift amount to see if it would increase the donor’s likelihood to give, or their generosity altogether.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 88.9% level of confidence, we observed a revenue increase of +52.1%.
This was driven by an increase in the donor conversion rate of +24% (LoC: 73%) and an average gift that was 22.6% higher than gifts made from the control segment.
Although this didn't fully validate, the results were directionally sound, which means that through the course of a longer campaign and more gifts, we could expect to see this hold true in 17.7 of 20 times that we ran this experiment.
When looking at what donors gave against what they were asked for, we noticed some interesting facts, which are:
40% of treatment gifts received gave more than what we asked for, representing 43% of the dollars raised in treatment experience.38% of the treatment gifts received gave exactly what we asked for, representing 37.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.22% of the treatment gifts received gave less than what we asked for, representing 19.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
Additionally, when looking at what we would have asked for from donors who gave in the control experience, we saw that donors gave only 48.6% of what we would have asked them to give.
Meanwhile, donors who were in the treatment experience ended up giving 71.6% of what we would have asked them to give.
What does this mean? It means that we're 42% more likely to receive what we ask for when telling donors what we want them to give. Otherwise, when left to their own devices, donors are less likely to give at all, and when they do, we're less likely to raise the dollars that we would have otherwise asked them to give.
Finally, we decided to compare the gifts made to this campaign to the donor's most recent contribution (MRC) and highest previous contribution (HPC) values. The findings were incredible!
When comparing donors in the "control" experience against those in the "treatment" experience, we observed the following:
74% more donors decided to give "more than" their MRC when in the treatment experience.These donors produced 86.5% more revenue when in the treatment experience than donors in the control experience.3x as many donors decided to make their largest gift ever through the treatment experience. And,This represented a 257% increase in revenue from the treatment segment who decided to give a gift to this campaign that was higher than their HPC value when compared to those in the control segment.
All of this is to say: When a donor is left to their own decision on how much to give, they often choose to give less than they could — so we should tell them exactly what we want them to give in this sort of way.
It is through this clarity that donors know exactly what they can do to help you (the organization).
With this in mind, further experimentation may be required through a longer or larger campaign, but it stands to reason that we're likely to see consistent results on future campaigns with rolling out this treatment experience moving forward.
Where possible, increase the clarity in how donors can contribute to the overall goal and make change in the world happen. Don't make them come up with what they should give, but rather ask them to do something that is attainable and within reach for them to contribute to success, and overall more donors will say yes.

experiments.title: How value proposition in a handwritten note about the impact of a higher gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the direct impact of a higher gift will increase the average gift and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights needed to increase its average gift and in turn its revenue from acquisition, that in turn will decrease the net cost per donor. We were looking for ways to increase the average gift without damaging the number of donations. To encourage higher gifts, we devised concise copy that would show the impact and worth of their $50 gift. Then we used a handwritten note to set it apart and catch the reader’s eye. This was run on three donation pages to acquire the needed samples. All of them were acquisition offers for eBooks that were advertised on social. We had to run it for two month, longer than typically advised, to acquire the needed samples.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tactic worked primarily on desktop and for new visitors. It increased the revenue of new visitors by 55%, with an $11 increase in average gift. However, it decreased revenue for mobile visitors by 35%. Our hypothesis is that the required placement for mobile led to a less desirable user experience. It also slightly decreased revenue for returning visitors, but not at the same rate as it increase gifts for new visitors. The average gift was only $4 lower for returning visitors. It also decreased the number of gifts.

Our hypothesis is that returning visitors who see this more than once are less likely to give, because this message is no longer new, but is instead a turn off. But new visitors, who haven't seen this element before, are intrigued and inspired.

We will roll this treatment out to just new visitors and desktop experience. And we will continue to search for other ways to increase giving for returning and mobile, that also works for new visitors and desktop.

experiments.title: How communal language on a high-urgency campaign sticky bar affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase revenue during a high urgency campaigns.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/30/2021
FamilyLife was running a matching gift campaign with a focus on recurring giving. Since communal language—copy that shows the reader that they are united with the organization in the most important beliefs and causes that they care for, all spoken with a familiarity of friends—works well for other faith-based organizations. We’d seen lifts of donations and revenue during high-urgency campaign and wanted to test if this would benefit FamilyLife as well. We tested a 50/50 split in traffic on their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was a directional lift in revenue. This did not fully validate because of the 23% decrease in revenue for new visitors that pulled down the full validation of a 59% increase in revenue for returning visitors.
Our hypothesis is that communal language, invoking the tone of friendship and care, works very well with returning visitors because they are more closely acquainted with the organization. Whereas a intimate tone with a new acquaintance will not resonate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 21.5% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 57.7% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had an 89.5% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Blackbaud]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting Blackbaud followers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach full validity as it was turned off due to increase cost-per-registration. The result was leaning towards the bulleted copy as the winner, which is the only result in this series of experiments that runs counter to our hypothesis that bullets keep a potential new subscriber from understanding the full weight of the value proposition.
Although, since this experiment did not reach full validity, I’m more inclined to trust the results of the valid tests in this series.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Web Traffic]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our website traffic, excluding the house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach validity. It was pointing towards the control as being the winner, further validating the hypothesis that bulleted does not help a potential new subscriber to understand the full value proposition. Whereas, with a contact on our house file, they have a pre-established trust and are simply looking to see if this offer is relevant to them.
This experiment was ended before full validation due to the escalating cost-per-registration.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the conversion rate on an email acquisition page

rows.name: Learn to Answer Your Donor's Number One Question

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on the donor affect the conversion rate of the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2016 - 09/06/2016
With the release of the Why Should I Give to You? study of nonprofit value proposition, we decided we needed to drink our own medicine and put the value proposition of the study to the test.
The control version of the landing page to download the study focused on communicating to the visitor that this research study will help them craft an effective value proposition for their organization.
We hypothesized that the control assumed that visitors to the landing already knew the value of crafting an effective value proposition. So a treatment was created that focused on a more universal goal for the type of traffic visiting the page: “Answer your donor’s number one question: Why should I give to you?”
We split the traffic 50/50, and put them to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a decrease in conversions by 10.5%.
This shows us the the type of traffic being driven to this landing page is more motivated to download the eBook when the copy focused on helping them construct an effective value proposition.
Follow up tests from this will focus on clarifying and reinforcing the value proposition of the control.

experiments.title: How call to action and value proposition affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the headline and intro copy strengthen the value proposition and increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 06/16/2016
We were hosting a live broadcast to unpack the first article of the Fundraiser’s Creed: People Give to People. We had set up a landing page to allow people to register for the broadcast. Upon peer review, we thought that a few small tweaks could increase the force of the value proposition on the page.
The first hypothesis had to do with the text above the headline. On the control, this copy said “Upcoming Live Internet Broadcast”. This was correct, but it gave the user no motivation or orientation in regards to what we wanted them to do. For the treatment, we changed this copy to “Register now for the live broadcast”.
The second hypothesis centered around the first paragraph. It was intended to address the reader and frame the problem, but we hypothesized that it might bury the actual value proposition for the broadcast, without adding much value. In the treatment, this paragraph was removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 24% lift in email acquisition. This shows the importance that each piece of copy has — copy can either orient and direct the visitor, or distract them and weaken the value proposition.

experiments.title: The importance of reinforcing the value proposition for sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which petition landing page would acquire more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2016 - 04/20/2016
As part of their moneybomb, SBA List created a petition to help drive more traffic to their donation page. Traditionally, the petition landing page has value proposition copy and the CTA to sign it. They wanted to test a treatment of the petition landing page that didn’t have the additional value proposition language and looked more like a letter to the people they created the petition for. Traffic for both of the petition landing pages was pushed through an email that had value proposition copy and a call to “sign the petition”. They A/B split test the petition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ended up getting twice as many emails to it than the control. But once people got to the donation page, they weren’t motivated enough to make a donation. We want more donations, not more emails for this campaign.  Since this test is approaching validity, we can learn from this that the presence of the value proposition  on the control motivated people to not only sign the petition but to give a donation as well and increased donor conversion by 25.1%. We need to reinforce value proposition for the sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.

experiments.title: How the email acquisition value proposition impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
For Susan B. Anthony List, we wanted to see if the existing value proposition around the home page email acquisition form was the most appealing to its audience. From previous experiments with the email file, we had found that the more casual tone was more appealing to their audience and they also like specificity. They wanted more details about the offer before they would take action. We decided to craft a value proposition statement that emphasize these two traits and test it against the existing value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for over two months, we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence with the test. The change to the design increased the number of factors that we were testing so it is difficult to say that it is the result of the value proposition alone. It is worth noting that with a control conversion rate of 2.4%, this is an already high email acquisition rate for a homepage audience. It may just be that the motivation of the audience was high enough that the value proposition did not have much impact.
In future testing, with a conversion rate this high, it may be worth focusing the tests on lower converting sections of the website so that we can observe a more significant impact.

experiments.title: How copy on a donation page affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: By providing a clear and credible reason to give, we can increase both the average gift and conversion rate of the main donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2014 - 04/30/2014
John Cornyn has served as a U.S. Senator from Texas for more than 14 years and is the current Senate Minority Whip for the 113th Congress. As election season begins, a torrent of traffic comes to JohnCornyn.com. This traffic includes likely voters looking to confirm their affiliation, donors looking to support the Senator, and people seeking information about all of the candidates. The Senator Cornyn’s team wanted to make sure that friction and anxiety were minimized on their main donation page and that credibility and clarity were increased so that the most motivated potential donors would make a gift to support the Cornyn campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment delivered a 258.1% increase in donor conversion, with a statistically valid sample size. Team Cornyn learned a few things about their donor base through this test:
1. Experience matters. Even though the donor might be motivated to get to the donation page, they need to be reminded throughout the process that Senator Cornyn aligns with their core beliefs and is worthy of their donation. Donors to Senator Cornyn are thoughtful — they can’t be expected to just react to a donation opportunity, they must be persuaded along the way.
2. Design matters. Simply adding the copy to the page hurt results. Keeping continuity in the user experience throughout the entire donation process is crucial to a positive lift.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2016 - 05/05/2016
Jews for Jesus sends out a monthly newsletter via email. The newsletter has articles and videos people click-through to read and watch, and they recently added an appeal to the newsletter. In efforts to improve donor conversion, they developed a treatment of the donation page for the newsletter appeal. The donation page that was the control didn’t have any value proposition language on it. For the treatment they added value proposition copy to the page to reinforce the message in the newsletter appeal and used large buttons for the gift array instead of radial buttons. They split traffic and tested the donation pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page saw a decrease in donations by 38%. While value proposition usually helps improve conversion, these results suggest that the presence of the value proposition copy may have actually created friction for this highly motivated audience. This audience was already motivated to give once they arrived to the donation page and didn’t need or want additional reasons why they should make a donation.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions for an eAppeal affected donor conversion.

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a Christ-follower
