experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christ-follower in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel. They decided to test these two different value propositions in the eAppeals and donation pages to see which their audience was most motivated to give to.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this test didn’t validate, initial data tells us that people were more motivated to give to a tangible need and event than the general value proposition of the ministry. In fact, the treatment that was focused on the Israel outreach event saw a 47.3% increase in donations. This was our hypothesis, but because of the relevancy of the control, it was important to test our hypothesis.
We championed the treatment and sent it as the resend to non-opens with a new subject line.

experiments.title: How directly connecting the value proposition to the ask impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a stronger value proposition increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2017 - 05/23/2017
As Mother’s Day was approaching, Live Action sent a fundraising email asking people to give a gift in honor of a mom in their life. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase clicks from the email to the donation page by better connecting the Mother’s Day holiday to the value proposition of Live Action. We developed a treatment with this strengthened value proposition and tested it against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but the data we did collect shows that by strengthening the value proposition of the email, we were able to increase clicks by 22%. Here is a great takeaway from this experiment: When we present an ask, more people are willing to click through (and hopefully give) when we can connect the ask to a strong reason to give. If we don’t do this, the ask sounds and feels transactional. People give not just to give, but to have an even greater impact. A strong value proposition can clearly communicate this and result in more people being motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects the value proposition and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/20/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to test it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The year-end countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask and it also reinforced the value proposition of the donation page. This lead to the increase in conversion rate by 41.6%, but a decrease in average gift by 4.7%. However, overall, the addition of the year-end clock with messaging that supported it resulted in a 35% increase in revenue.
In the future, the countdown clock with financially focused messaging will be deployed towards the end of large year-end campaigns to increase revenue in the final days of giving.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition on a landing page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a revised value proposition on the landing page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2019 - 03/31/2019
DTS was running a value proposition test on their Hebrews landing page, that focused on how the course would benefit the reader rather than focusing on the teacher of the course or the course content itself. We also adjusted the headline of the landing page. The end donation page and following workflow remained the same in both the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had little to no impact on landing page signups, but something surprising happened in this test. The instant donor conversion rate on the following page from people who signed up on the treatment landing page increased by 35% (not statistically valid, but really close). This could be directly related to the fact that we've just told them the value they're going to get out of this course and the impact it will have on their walk with Christ, rather than focusing our value proposition on course content or course teacher.
Moving forward, further testing is necessary on the value proposition of the landing page and how that flows into the donation page content. Continuing to reiterate the eternal impact of the course is key to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/16/2019
Back to the Bible sent out an email to introduce the new Bible teacher for their radio broadcast. The email linked to a donation page that continued with the biographical information of the new Bible teacher and had a donation form at the bottom of the page. We hypothesized that if we were linking people to a donation page a stronger connection between the Bible teacher and the donation ask needed to be made in an effort to motivate people to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Engagement with the email appeal was fairly healthy, but when it came to giving from this, the audience was pretty lethargic. With only a couple of donations coming in from this email appeal, the donation page treatment had no impact on conversion because overall motivation was so low. What we learned from this experiment is that it is a far stretch to go from introducing an audience to someone they never knew in a few short paragraphs to then asking them for a donation. This kind of email would have better been suited as a cultivation piece rather than an appeal.

experiments.title: How a new value proposition focus in a sales email affects mystery donor audit applications

rows.name: What are your 2019 online fundraising plans?
Here’s a great way to increase giving for 2019 and beyond

experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing the value proposition on the lack of time to plan and research lead to greater applications?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/20/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if a different value proposition approach to a mystery donor audit sales email would lead to greater applications. The control we had tested into already focused on how expert opinions are really just that : opinions. If your fundraising isn’t based on research, data, and testing, then you’re just relying on intuition.
The treatment focused on the lack of time to the necessary research to create a plan for 2019. We could step in with this mystery donor audit and do the leg work for you to help you create a strategy for your next year of fundraising. Both the subject line and body copy of the email were included in the treatment variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version saw a 70% decrease in clicks through to the landing page. While we didn’t reach full statistical confidence, we were approaching confidence at 89.5%. While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a free offer-focused value prop convert more donors than a mission-focused value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2018 - 11/05/2018
Hoover Institution has implemented a one-click sign up for Defining Ideas newsletter in their offer chaser emails. Essentially, it takes them right to a donation page instead of requiring them to fill out a landing page before getting to the donation page. They wanted to increase donor conversion on this page, and hypothesized that changing the value proposition on the page to a more resource-focused value prop instead of mission-focused value prop would fulfill this.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn't validate, but saw a directional lift of 120% in donations with the revised value proposition on the page. This version of the value proposition focused on the immediate value of current and future free resources that Hoover would be able to provide because of their gift, rather than focusing on the greater mission of Hoover. This concept should be tested again on future offer donation pages to fully validate the learnings.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing an image impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding value proposition and removing an image increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 04/08/2018
Pregnancy Decision Line gets significant traffic to their “Abortion Costs” webpage. Looking at the original page, we hypothesized that adding more value proposition to the page, and removing the header image might increase conversion. We tested the original page against a treatment that had conversational style copy, a larger phone number at the end of the copy, and no image on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased conversion by 15.6%. Adding conversational value proposition has been known to help reinforce the message and motivate people to take action, but that isn’t what happened on this page. What this tells us is that the people coming to this page are coming to it for answers. Their motivation is high and the additional copy acted more as a point of friction to the decision process than a motivating variable.

experiments.title: How an emotional value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more emotional value proposition generate more donations than the more tactically-focused value proposition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2018 - 03/28/2018
In a recent experiment, Heritage Action for America had found a new value proposition that had appealed to Heritage Foundation donors in the serial ask found on The Foundation’s donation page. This experiment had nearly doubled the number of donors that were now giving to Heritage Action via the serial ask.
With the success of this experiment, we wanted to see if it would have the same impact on the main Heritage Action donation page. When comparing it to the copy found on that page, the new treatment relied far less on the tactical elements of Heritage Action and focused more on the emotional reasons to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we found that the new value proposition was able to increase giving by 69%. Now, it should be noted that there was a relatively low number of visitors and conversions compared; we generally want to see more visitors before turning off a test. However, with a long running test, we have to start being concerned about historical distortion and given the low traffic volume sent to the Heritage Action main donation page, it wasn’t likely that we would see a significant boost in conversion or traffic.
With that said, this value proposition should be further validated with additional experiments in areas that will experience greater volumes of traffic like email campaigns.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition on the acquisition and donation page impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a value proposition specific to the content offer help motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2018
Care Net developed a Lenten Devotional content offer and used Facebook to push people to their site to get the content offer. After people signed up for the content offer, they were then presented with an instant donation ask. This instant donation page used a value proposition that was Care Net focused. We hypothesized that having a donation page specific to the actual content offer might increase donor conversion. We created a treatment where the value proposition was specific to the Lenten Devotional and tested this against the control
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did see a significant directional lift with the treatment of 142%! This directional lift tells us that when we align our messaging with the value that people just experienced, we are able to motivate more people to take action and give.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition on a donation page can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would acquire more donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 06/20/2017
The treatment value proposition that was focused on the spiritual impact of a person’s gift increased overall revenue by 50%! There were not only more people who were motivated to give, but these people were motivated to give more. People were willing to invest in a kingdom-focused mission more generously.
While people cared about the event being successful and effective, they responded more when they knew their investment would have a larger impact – an eternal one.
This experiment didn’t validate, but it was leaning towards being valid and was rolled out for the rest of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency campaign leading up to their large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. On the donation page for the campaign, the value proposition and call-to-action was centered on making the event a success. It had more of a logistics angle to it. Because the event was an evangelism outreach, we hypothesized that people might be more motivated to give if the value proposition was focused on the spiritual impact. We developed a treatment and split the traffic coming to the donation page.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition in the body copy and CTA affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/26/2016
For Harvest Ministries mid-month appeal they wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on the landing page. The control and the treatment had the same value proposition, but the treatment had subtle additions to the copy of the headline, body and CTA to stregthen the value proposition of the page. They ran a split test to see which landing page would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment landing page had an increase in donations by 22.8%. The reinforcement of the value proposition throughout the treatment landing page strengthened the message and ask, resulting in more people giving a donation.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct copy approach that increases clarity improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 12/02/2015
Harvest Ministries was planning to send fundraising emails on Giving Tuesday. They had crafted an email to the housefile that utilized Greg Laurie’s traditional storytelling style, but wanted to see if a more direct approach that educated their donors about Giving Tuesday would increase results. They tested a new subject line, but also new email copy that took a more direct approach to see if increased clarity improved donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This showed that our donors respond financially when we are straightforward and direct. This 60% lift in revenue was a product of a subject line test that slightly increased open rate and a copy test that increased both clickthrough rate and donor motivation, as evidenced by this experiment.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity and specificity of the value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/04/2015
Hillsdale College launched a popular new course studying the life of Christian apologist C.S. Lewis. Donor conversion was low in comparison to other courses, so they investigated the copy on the donation page. The value proposition presented described Hillsdale’s online courses in general. They knew that this course attracted a lot of people who might not be familiar with the Hillsdale brand so they drafted a second copy treatment to explain why they teach the course, and how it is free. Then they launched an A/B test to see which copy treatment performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that specificity does increase the force of the value proposition, which in turn increased donor conversion rate. This may not be the case with all of Hillsdale’s courses, but when attracting a new audience, we may need to be more educational in why the courses are presented, and how they are funded.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”.
A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue -- but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate.
Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner. The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment's gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the "Other amount" option.  This is an example of "unsupervised thinking" which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale's next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How removing elements of friction and strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2017 - 09/26/2017
The primary donation page for FamilyLife used a large image at the top of it with their traditional value proposition copy on it. They recently developed their value proposition and wanted to test a radical redesign of their primary donation page that tested this copy and removed other elements of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 14.3%. By removing elements of friction on the page and strengthening their value proposition, we were able to not only increase the number of people giving a gift, but we were able to also motivate them to give more generously resulting in a 94.4% increase (77% valid) in revenue as well because the average gift increased.

experiments.title: How adding scripture reinforces value proposition in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding scripture reinforce the value proposition to the donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test the impact of adding a scripture reference into the email on donations.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused banner increase donations more than a match-focused banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that focused more on the value a gift provided would increase donations more than a match-focused banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, but the treatment banner produced a 21% decrease in donations. Because there were multiple factors tested in the banner, it’s hard to say exactly which one produced a decrease, but the removal of matching gift language and a less urgent color on the banner likely contributed to the decrease, because the matching gift language proved to be a motivating factor for FL donors in other testing.

experiments.title: How the right value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 08/05/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to increase donor conversion rate on their main donation page. Two previous tests had failed to do so by adding value proposition language and changing the open gift field to pre-selected buttons.
The question was raised — what if the first value proposition test had the right language, but the wrong presentation? DTS crafted a new version of the value proposition that started with a fundamental truth that the audience could agree with: “The world needs Biblical truth now more than ever.” This copy also included a quote from Chuck Swindoll that was inline with the text, as opposed to offset in the previous test.
Finally, DTS added a subheadline and strengthened and bolded the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new landing page produced a 186.5% increase in donor conversion. This told us that presenting the right value proposition in the right way has a massive effect on donor conversion. The results of this test inspired many more tests across different donation pages and acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How the wrong value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will collect the highest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2015 - 06/22/2015
The primary donation form for Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) had remained consistent for the last several years. It was a well-designed form but it made the assumption that those donors that make it to the page are already convinced to give their gift. This was evident from the lack of any copy or significant calls to action at the top of the form.
In an effort to increase the perceived value of the donation to potential donors, we decided to create a new treatment. This new page would utilize the same donation form but would add copy to the top that discussed one of the unique value propositions of DTS. It also included quotes from well known figures in the Christian community supporting the work of DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation form treatment resulted in a 28.1% decrease in donor conversion. This would indicate that the value proposition communicated on the page did not appeal to the potential donors. Additionally, by adding the wrong copy and pushing the donation form further down on the page, we introduced friction into the donation process. These two factors combined to reduce the donors and revenue to the page.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing perception of cost and work increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2016 - 02/09/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had a great response to their first free online course on the Gospel of John. But objectively looking at the headline 0f their signup page a few weeks later, they wondered if the value proposition could be improved — specifically, by using something other than the word “study”.
Other experiments had shown that offering the benefits of the offer often outperform words like “study” that make the offer seen like work. So they launched two headline tests to see if they could affect conversion. Both headlines sought to show the real opportunity of the course — to “step inside the classroom”, but with different desired effects. The first headline offered the opportunity to “see the gospel of John like never before” and the other to “grow your understanding of John’s gospel”.
They launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, neither variation outperformed the control. This tells us something very interesting — “study” might actually be a positive factor of the value proposition. It suggests that prospects for this course might be looking to “study” and really spend time with the course.
It may also suggest that the value proposition to “step inside the classroom” is unclear or undesirable. This concept lends itself to further testing.

experiments.title: How additional premium-focused value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of premium focused value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2016 - 09/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had found a popular offer in their free Gospel of John course. After the user signed up, they were given the opportunity to make a gift.In exchange for a gift of $100 or more they were offered a commentary as the course companion book for the Gospel of John course. DTS decided to try to see if they could increase conversion rate by integrating review content for the commentary into the donation page. They pulled some reviews from a popular book review site onto the page, showed five stars, and gave a positive review for the commentary, hoping that this would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 47% decrease in conversion, although these results were not statistically valid due to low traffic to the page. However, the treatment also produced an increase in average gift, which may suggested this motivated a certain segment of people who enrolled in the course to increase their average gift to receive the commentary. However, this additional value proposition appeared to distract from the main value proposition, which converted more registered users into donors. Wally learning to not produce a positive impact on this donation page further testing for how the book is offered throughout the course might produce a lift.

experiments.title: How personalized value proposition affects offer conversion rate

rows.name: Pray God's Word for your Child

experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more motivated to download by a claim of value that benefits them personally?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2017 - 06/30/2017
Buckner International released a new content offer named 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in Your Life. They wanted to test if people were more likely to download the offer if the headline on the landing page promised something specifically for them, or if they were more likely to download if it would benefit their child.
The control copy led with, “Pray God’s Word for Your Child” and the treatment copy led with, “Get this 7-day devotional to grow in your faith and pray God’s Word for your child”. The treatment copy also included an intro body copy for parents whose bedtime prayers had “gotten stale.” Other copy and elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment did not reach statistical validity, it was quickly approaching the 95% level of confidence that we strive for. As a result, we can draw some insights from this test.
After analyzing the two different versions, it is our hypothesis that the treatment diluted the primary product-level value proposition- praying for your kids- by with an additional offer – growing in your faith. The first headline is high in clarity, while the second headline and additional intro copy likely reduced this clarity.

experiments.title: How selling the value proposition in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding additional copy around the value proposition of Heritage will increase the visitors to the page and the overall number of donors once they get there.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2014 - 01/01/2015
The end of the year deadline serves as a way to increase the urgency around the year end campaign. As the clock ticks closer to midnight on the 31st, there is increased urgency to give.  With that increased urgency, we wondered if it was still necessary to sell the value of Heritage to the reader in the email or would a shorter email get them there faster. So we decided to test that.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the test did not validate, it would indicate that the additional value proposition is not necessary so close to the deadline.

experiments.title: How reducing the value proposition copy affects the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/07/2015 - 11/15/2015
A previous experiment for Alliance Defending Freedom had found that adding a value proposition, or a reason to give their email, on the homepage was able to increase the number of emails acquired by 44%. With the launch of the new website, we wanted to see if would could decrease the amount of copy required to communicate the same value proposition.
Our hypothesis was that by reducing the copy on the page, we could reduce the amount of mental friction associated with reading it. If it had the same level of value, this reduction in friction would be able to further lift conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the shorter value proposition actually reduced the conversion rate by 29.5%. It is our hypothesis that while the treatment was visually shorter, by removing the bullet points, we made it difficult to quickly scan. There was also added value communicated in the headline of the Control treatment with the perceived exclusivity of “Be the first to know”. This tells us that, at least with email acquisition, the added value that comes from the longer value proposition and the additional exclusivity translates into increased conversion.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition language impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition copy will be most appealing (and subsequently have the highest conversion rate) with visitors to the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2016 - 05/02/2016
In an effort to capture more data from visitors to their blog, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to test out a modified version of the existing email offer. Through a series of experiments, we had narrowed in on the active email offer which is a general email opt in that offers news and updates for religious freedom cases. We felt that we could increase the relevance of this offer by highlighting that ADF was truly the “boots on the ground” in these situations and could offer first-hand perspectives, not just an update.
experiments.keyLearnings: The updated value proposition copy led to a 37% increase to the number of emails acquired on the blog. In addition to the increased value that the concept of “first-hand perspectives” brought to the offer, we implemented an element of urgency to the headline. By changing “Get updates on your religious freedom” to “Stay informed on changes to your religious freedom.” we introduced the idea that the “changes” may have (and often do have) a direct impact on their day to day life. This made the email offer more important and personally relevant to the visitors.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage's efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage's mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How a concise value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified renewal page improve conversion over a campaign specific page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2016 - 01/29/2016
For the Heritage 2016 membership campaign, we created a campaign-specific landing page that laid out the various reasons that a donor should renew their membership in 2016. We identified not only the benefits to the donor but also the specific challenges that Heritage was going to be addressing in the coming year.
We discovered, from earlier testing in this campaign, that the benefits did not truly impact the conversion rate on the page for existing donors. With this in mind, we developed a hypothesis that the existing members may not actually need the entire value proposition we had laid out on the page given their knowledge of the organization. If this hypothesis was true, the added copy would actually be acting as a point of friction instead of adding additional value.  To validate this hypothesis, we tested the campaign-specific page against the standard renewal page that had been refined over time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more concise renewal page was able to increase the donor conversion by 5.9% without negatively impacting the average gift. The existing donors already had enough of an understanding of Heritage’s unique value proposition that the longer page produced more friction than value in the donors’ minds.
As a side note, we were careful to monitored the average gift of both treatments in this experiment. The clarity of the value proposition can directly influence the generosity of the donor. Since we were removing much of the copy, we wanted to make sure it didn’t have any negative impact on that aspect of the giving experience.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors to increase the donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 06/05/2015
For Hillsdale College, the Constitution 101 online course has proven to be a great source of new email addresses and new donors for their file.  In particular, the landing page that is shown after someone signs up has the highest volume of traffic compared to their other donation offers. As a result, this page has been the focus of multiple testing iterations over the past several months.
The first big lift to conversion rate came from modifying the value proposition displayed on the original page. After that initial experiment, we tried experimenting with different tactical elements of the page. None of these experiments were able to boost results.
Since the value proposition was proven to be the best way to boost results so we wanted to try refining this value proposition even further. We created a new copy treatment that removed the organizational goals and instead explored the reasoning for the course in greater depth.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the organizational goal of having 50 million people take the class and instead emphasizing why it is so critical that the class be offered for free, we were able to boost conversion rate by 22.6%. This is a good example of examining the message through the eyes of the donor to focus in on what is most important to them.  The “50 million people” goal wasn’t necessarily bad but this test proves it was the deeper value of the course that they valued more.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”. A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue — but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate. Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner.
The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment’s gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the “Other amount” option.  This is an example of “unsupervised thinking” which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale’s next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage’s efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage’s mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How a broader value proposition in the headline affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a broader value statement in the headline increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2016 - 05/03/2016
Hillsdale College was promoting their free Constitution 101 course through multiple online channels. The control signup page converted nearly 40% of visitors to enroll in the course, but it also assumed that the primary value proposition, “Understand the Constitution like never before” resonated with each visitor.
They wanted to test whether a broader statement might capture the attention of more people who did not already have an inclination to “understand the Constitution like never before” and convince more to enroll. They also hypothesized that a “value statement” in the headline might result in more donations. So they created a second page, with the headline “The Constitution secures your freedoms as an American” and the subheadline “Understand it like never before in Hillsdale College’s free online course”.
They launched an A/B test to determine the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page with the broader headline actually decreased email acquisition conversion rate by 2.7%. However, looking at the entire funnel, we noticed that visitors to this page were 14.4% more likely to donate.
This presented an interesting finding: while the broader statement resulted in fewer course enrollments, it increased the motivation of those who did enroll. This seems to suggest that while the new headline was more divisive among initial visitors (which caused slightly lower enrollment), it increased the appeal of the course by playing to the values (rather than just the surface desires) of those who accepted the offer. This reiterates the importance of measuring the entire funnel when there is both email acquisition and donor acquisition at stake.

experiments.title: How removing value prop copy on a mobile donation form impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for mobile donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 06/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action and thus encountering more copy on the donation form was adding friction for the user.  So for this experiment, the treatment variant anchored directly to the gift array section of the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test looked at what happens when the donation form copy is removed for visitors arriving via SMS. The thinking was that since SMS messaging already motivates the donor to act, repeating copy on the form may create unnecessary friction — particularly for donors considering a recurring gift.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate

What the Results Suggest:
Overall, one-time giving dipped slightly, but the sample size wasn't large enough to draw firm conclusions. The more interesting finding is the sharp increase in recurring donations. This suggests the existing form copy — which is oriented toward one-time giving — may be nudging donors away from choosing a recurring gift.
Next Steps:
To focus on growing recurring giving, expand testing to all mobile users. This will also ensure statistical significance.

experiments.title: Does Problem–Solution Messaging Increase Year-End Main Donation Page Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and sharpening the problem statement on the year-end main donation page will increase donor conversion because clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and their motivation to give. because the clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 11/25/2025
Recently, we tested stronger problem–solution messaging on the main donation page, clarifying families’ challenges and positioning the donor’s gift as a direct solution. This language led to a 20% increase in gifts, suggesting that clearer articulation of the problem and the donor’s role effectively boosts  motivation to give.
Based on these results, we aimed to test if applying problem–solution framing to the year-end donation page would also increase donor motivation and improve conversion rates.
Control Version: focused on the “Help Deliver Hope and Joy” theme, highlighted the matching gift, and listed ministry activities supported by donations.
Treatment Version: reframed the message to highlight the crisis families face and positioned year-end gifts as ways to restore hope, strengthen marriages, equip parents, and help children experience Christ’s love. It also reinforced the ministry’s impact and directly linked gifts to outcomes.
The test goal was to see if clearer problem framing and a stronger donor-impact message would boost conversion during this critical year-end fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the page generated 119 gifts with a 9.6% conversion rate, compared to 138 gifts and a 10.8% conversion rate on the control, representing an 11.2% decrease in gifts. However, this result did not reach statistical significance (68.4% level of confidence), meaning the two versions of the page performed very similarly overall.

The test also did not reach the required sample size of 4,807 visitors, receiving only 2,513 total visitors before it was paused. Because of this limited traffic, it is difficult to draw a definitive conclusion about the impact of the messaging change on donor conversion.

In terms of donor behavior, both versions performed similarly in generating monthly sustainer gifts (5 on the control vs. 7 on the treatment), suggesting the messaging change did not meaningfully affect donors’ likelihood to start recurring giving.

However, the test was paused after six days due to a concerning trend in revenue performance. While conversion rates were relatively close, the average gift size differed significantly:
Control average gift: $44.95
 	Treatment average gift: $24.38
45.8% less revenue
93.6% level of confidence
very large gifts ($1,000+)
Control adjusted average gift: $14.84
 	Treatment adjusted average gift: $12.67
14.6% decrease in average gift
statistically inconclusive 78.4% level of confidence
directional rather than definitive
20% increase in gifts
continuing to test stronger value propositions and problem–solution framing

experiments.title: Does refreshing the homepage value prop with topical messaging impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value prop and a donation module for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 09/22/2025
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly revised the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly changed the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
Key Results:
35% non-statistically significant decline (88%) in recurring conversion rate.
 	33% non-statistically significant decline (86%) in recurring revenue per visitor. This test showed that a topical value proposition likely created urgency and relevance, making more visitors feel that giving was timely and impactful.
While urgency drove immediate one-time giving, it may have framed the donation as a short-term crisis response rather than an ongoing need. When the value prop emphasizes immediacy, donors are more inclined to act once rather than commit long-term.
Recommendation/Implementation: Given our focus on recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.
Overall performance:

experiments.title: How updating a callout with value prop on a campaign page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value prop more topical for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2025 - 09/01/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the current value prop on the homepage from being a general organization focused to topical focused. With changes on the ground in Gaza, we wanted to test whether referencing that situation would make an impact. We hypothesized that this would help the user connect more with the organization by presenting a topic relevant and important to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of shifting the hero value proposition from a general, organization-wide focus to a topical, issue-specific focus. The hypothesis was that surfacing a topic personally relevant to the user would strengthen their connection to the organization and drive higher engagement.
Key Results
Overall Conversion Rate: -14% decline (65% confidence) — not statistically significant
 	Revenue Per Visitor: -3% decline (55% confidence) — not statistically significant
Insights
The results suggest that a topical focus may inadvertently alienate users who don't feel personally connected to the featured issue. Rather than a narrow topic, users appear to prefer a holistic snapshot of the organization — who we are, what we do, and the breadth of our impact. A general value prop may better serve the range of motivations users bring when they arrive.
Recommendation & Next Steps:
We plan to retest this module with two adjustments: reintroducing the donation module within the hero and refining the copy. This will help us isolate whether the topical framing itself — or the absence of a clear giving prompt — was the primary driver of the decline.

experiments.title: How removing value prop copy on a mobile donation form impacts conversion rates – part 2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 07/02/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action, and that encountering more copy on the donation form adds friction for the user.  So for this experiment, the treatment variant is anchored directly to the gift array section of the donation form.
This test was run before on a different form, so we wanted to see if we got the same results on different forms tied to different SMS messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action, and that encountering more copy on the donation form adds friction for the user.
Key Results:
decline

increase

Recommendation/Implementation:

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition to reflect the impact of the entire organization—rather than a single brand—affects donor conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the “One Moody” approach for donation page visitors will not change donor conversion rates because most donors support Moody’s mission as a whole rather than a single ministry, making the broader institutional language unlikely to create friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2026
To better understand the impact of consolidating the brands across Moody, we a tested more unified “One Moody” language to see how the Moody Bible Institute’s audience responded to this shift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment suggests that implementing a more unified “One Moody” approach on the donation page was associated with a 22.6% decrease in total donations across all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level was only 69.5%, well below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, indicating the results are not statistically reliable and should not be used to inform future decision-making. While overall donations declined in the treatment, the average gift increased. The control page generated an average gift of $20.50, while the “One Moody” treatment yielded an average gift of $23.79—an increase of approximately 16%. Because the test did not reach statistical significance, additional testing is needed before drawing conclusions about how unified “One Moody” messaging may influence donor behavior. Future experiments should prioritize achieving higher confidence levels and may benefit from further exploration of how branding and messaging influence donor perception, motivation, and giving behavior.

experiments.title: How does specific value prop for Youtube traffic impact conversion on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “video-specific” value prop for Youtube traffic coming to the main donate page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it acknowledges where they’re coming from and focuses in on an aspect of the mission that they have experienced themselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2025 - 04/28/2025
For Time of Grace, we observed an increase in the amount of traffic coming to the main donate page from their YouTube channel. We hypothesized that leading this traffic to a main donate that was “video-specific” acknowledging the type of content they were coming from and focusing on this form of outreach in particular would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the "video-specific" value proposition for YouTube traffic coming to the main donate page did not increase donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

One way of understanding these results is that perhaps focusing the copy too much on one aspect of the mission, left the overall value prop less effective, indicating that users respond better to copy about video, but also the other missional outreach Time of Grace is doing.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying the value proposition for perspective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world. because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 04/12/2023
MAF is running an offer through paid Facebook ads that asks people to leave a note of encouragement for MAF missionaries. On the landing page, they are provided with four pre-written messages that they can select from and then given an optional chance to write in their own note.
MAF wanted to see if we could increase the ICR on the donation page. After reading the value proposition on the control page, we found that the copy was confusing because it went from talking about the work that missionaries are doing to the work that pilots do for MAF. Because of this, there wasn’t clarity on what the user’s donation would do for MAF or isolated people.
To achieve this, we decided to run a radical redesign test and changed the following:
We removed “Thank you” from the headline because we hypothesize that this causes people to feel done with the process and leave the page.
We asked them to stay on the page by saying “Before you go, can we ask you something?”
We clarified the donation ask by stating A) Why missionaries are important B) That these missionaries can only continue this important work because of people who support MAF with a donation and C) What a donation to MAF will do.
We moved the ask for a donation to the end of the copy rather than asking for a donation first then explaining why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment A page saw a 51.2% increase in conversion rate with a 76.4% level of confidence. It also saw a 108.4% increase in revenue with a 89% level of confidence. The treatment B page (which had the same copy as treatment A) saw a 46.4% increase in conversion rate with a 71% level of confidence, and a 15.6% increase in revenue with a 60.5% level of confidence.

We believe that treatment page A had such an increase in conversion rate and revenue because people would more clearly understand what MAF was asking for on the donation page. Therefore we believe providing clarity increases donations and revenue.

We believe treatment B did not produce as much of a lift because donors didn't have a chance to make a more emotional connection by seeing isolated people in the header image.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for Avila’s donation page viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the audience will feel more aligned with the mission and goal of the Avila Foundation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2025 - 03/10/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether enhancing the donation page copy on their Ecce Homo offer with value proposition would increase donor engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding a Value Proposition Increased Conversions – The treatment led to a 16.6% lift in conversions, suggesting that highlighting Avila’s mission resonated with donors.
 	Higher Average Gift Amount – Donors in the treatment group gave more on average ($43.46 vs. $37.77), indicating that mission-driven messaging may encourage larger gifts.
 	Results Show Potential, But Need More Testing – The 57.4% confidence level suggests further testing is needed to confirm the impact.
 	Mission-Driven Appeals Strengthen Donor Alignment – Emphasizing Avila’s role in addressing cultural and spiritual challenges may have helped donors feel more connected.
 	Framing Matters – Positioning donor support as essential to the mission (rather than just covering costs) likely contributed to the increase in engagement.
Next Steps:
Test with a larger sample size to improve statistical confidence.
 	Experiment with different messaging elements (e.g., urgency vs. donor impact).
 	Consider adding donor stories or incentives to further boost conversions.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the donate button increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the main donate button for cye 2024 campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the added value proposition will reinforce the ‘why’ . because the added value proposition will reinforce the ‘why’ .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 campaign we wanted to see if we could increase donations by adding more value proposition under the homepage’s main donate button.
For this test, we only ran it while there was no other elements on the homepage. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding a value proposition to the main donate button can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates.

2. In this specific case, the treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in donations for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence was only 21.8%, which is below the recommended 95% for valid results.

4. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 85% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different value propositions or test the placement of the value proposition on the homepage to further optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding more value proposition to the donate button increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value propostion to the main donate button for cye 2024 campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ . because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 campaign we wanted to see if we could increase donations by adding more value proposition under the homepage’s main donate button.
For this test, we only ran it while there was no other elements on the homepage. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition to the main donate button for the CYE 2024 campaign resulted in a decrease in donations by 13.5% with a statistical confidence level of 54.4%. However, since the level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results, and that the statistical confidence level is high before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the findings. It is also important to consider testing different value propositions or strategies to see what resonates best with donors, and to continually iterate and improve based on the results of experiments.

While it's important to note that this test did not cause an increase in transactions, it did cause a significant increase in revenue. We're digging in with the moody team to see if there are any outlier gifts that would have made this increase.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the homepage donate button increase conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value propostion to the main donate button for cye 2024 campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ . because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 campaign we wanted to see if we could increase donations by adding more value proposition under the homepage’s main donate button.
For this test, we only ran it while there was no other elements on the homepage. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that adding a value proposition to the main donate button for the CYE 2024 campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 62.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to always ensure that the statistical significance level is met before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further analyze why the value proposition did not resonate with donors in this particular experiment. This could involve gathering feedback from donors, conducting qualitative research, or testing different value propositions to see which resonates the most. By continuing to test and learn from different strategies, we can ultimately uncover what drives donor conversions and improve our fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: How testimonials on the donation page increased donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they can clearly see the impact of their gift during CYE.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/16/2024
During the high-traffic month of December, we wanted to know if adding an “end user” testimonial would further inspire potential donors to give or give at a greater amount because they were reading about the impact of their gift at the time of decision. Similar to how an e-commerce page might use testimonials for a product, we wanted to know the impact of adding this additional copy from the end user to the Today in the Word CYE donation page would make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the main campaign donation page resulted in a 17.3% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that having end-user testimonials on the page can increase credibility and potentially encourage more people to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials or other forms of social proof on donation pages. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform our strategies for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does adding value prop and description of a premium to the donation page impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop and description of the devotional for web visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because there is more information about the offer which leads to confidence in knowing what you will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/06/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During November and December, PTV promotes their annual Daily Devotional. During this time, we decided to test adding copy to the donation page.
For this test, traffic came from the PTV website, email, and paid media channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop and a description of the devotional on the donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group, although the statistical level of confidence was only 42.6%.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration in order to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment and the specific audience targeted.

When designing future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different versions of the value prop and description to see if there is a more effective way to communicate the benefits of the devotional. Additionally, exploring other elements of the donation page, such as the layout and design, could provide further insights into what encourages visitors to complete a transaction.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2024 - 11/11/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding some additional value prop, especially around becoming a sustainer, impacted donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors showed a positive impact on donor conversions, with a noticeable increase in Transactions for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 27.6% is below the recommended threshold of 95%, indicating that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to generate reliable results. Additionally, clear and measurable key performance indicators should be established to accurately assess the impact of any changes or interventions. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and variability in donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit from adding value prop to donation forms, further experimentation with a stronger design and methodology is needed to draw meaningful conclusions and make informed decisions for future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How simplified value prop on a premium donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop for users on a premium donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it introduces them to the impact of their gift regarding the premiums specifically, rather than a more general “org-centered” value prop.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/23/2024
On Reasons to Believe’s premium donation pages, we noticed that multiple pages had an old value prop (one now replaced in newer copy) that outlined a series of bullet points demonstrating the impact of a gift. This language tended to be longer, more focused on how the org is impacted and the full mission of RTB, versus the newer premium specific value prop which simply talked about the impact of a donor’s gift regarding the premium specifically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the value proposition on a premium donation page can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with a more focused and specific value prop regarding the impact of the donor's gift on premiums saw a 61.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 67.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to conduct similar tests with a larger sample size to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the value proposition to see if there are specific language or messaging that resonates best with donors on premium donation pages. By continuously testing and iterating on different approaches, we can gather more robust data and make more informed decisions to optimize donation conversions.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts active donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional reinforcement for active donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which included additional reinforcement about WUNC and its purpose, resulted in a 16.0% decrease in click-through rates compared to the control group.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.6% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for decision-making.

3. Moreover, the treatment version had a 50% decrease in donations.

This experiment shows us, based on a small sample size, that active donors to WUNC do not need to be reminded about what WUNC is and does to be motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts prospects from a political newsletter list during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop for political newsletter prospects will achieve a change in email clicks and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted prospects that had signed up for a political newsletter before the elections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional call-out reinforcing the value proposition of WUNC did not have the intended effect on click-through rates and conversion rates during the Fall Fund Drive. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

Moreover, the donation conversion rate was also lower for the treatment option, with a 33% decrease in conversions and a 35% level of confidence. However, the sample size was extremely small (only 5 total donations).

This test bears refinement and repeating with bigger audience sizes.

experiments.title: Will adding in the value proposition copy from the donate button to the monthly donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding biblical resources for the monthly donation page will achieve will increase transactions because their is congruency between the donate button and the copy on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/29/2024
During our testing travels with the duel donation buttons in the main navigation bar we found that the value proposition of providing biblical resources led to an increase in clicks to the donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we adding that same value proposition to the donation page copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the value proposition of providing biblical resources to the donation page copy significantly increased revenue for all traffic. The treatment group, which included the added biblical resources copy, saw a 157.6% increase in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence, indicating that this change had a substantial impact.

This indicates that there is a strong congruency between the messaging on the donation button and the copy on the donation page, which likely contributed to the increase in transactions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to align the messaging throughout the donation process to maintain this congruency.

Additionally, it would be worthwhile to test other potential value propositions or messaging strategies on donation pages to see if they also lead to an increase in donations. By continuing to test and optimize the messaging and value propositions on donation pages, and can further enhance its donation performance and maximize its impact.
