experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in overall donations because they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/17/2024 - 09/02/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Root Sin. We decided to run a test changing the copy on the donation page. Recently, we updated their main donation page with more value proposition; we decided to add that to the Root Sin page to see if this would have a positive effect on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved adding a value proposition to the donation page copy, led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic by 36.2%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.3%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. It is also crucial to consider the timing and duration of the experiment to account for any external factors that could influence the outcomes. Additionally, further testing and iterations may be necessary to determine the impact of adding a value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila's audience accurately.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield conclusive results, the learnings can be used to inform future experimentation strategies and help optimize the donation process for Avila Foundation.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a donation page and making it a single column experience for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 10/13/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift.
This is the second iteration of this experiment because we noticed that desktop experienced a large decrease in conversions. So, we hypothesized that we could use the same language but change the layout of the page so that it was a single-column experience so that the user could read through all the value prop language before making a decision
experiments.keyLearnings: Our first results showed a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. However our secondary hypothesis did not prove to be true. This experiment resulted in a 7% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 46% - meaning there is not statistical difference in the treatment. Upon closer examination it seems as if the donation form inadvertently got changed from the control's donation form. The header of the form changed and the gift designation selection was removed. These additional variables may have also impacted the results of our experiment.

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion rate on a calendar year end page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for year end donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators were running a year-end campaign and wanted to test whether additional value proposition copy on the donation page would increase conversion. While the page already had visuals, a story, and value proposition copy at the bottom, they wanted to add it to the top of the page to motivate people who might arrive at the page unconvinced to give.
They drafted copy that outlined their plan for the next year, making a case for support, and tested the two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no significant increase or decrease on the page. Early on, there was a notable increase—but as the month went on, and motivation (and conversion rate) increased, the copy had less and less impact, as the visitors were more motivated to give regardless of what was on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to the main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators historically had very little copy on their primary donation page. As they had set an initiative to generate more “where most needed” gifts (as opposed to staff gifts), they knew they might need to convince donors who arrived on the page to make a gift. However, they did not want to do this at the expense of staff giving. They developed a value proposition that would encapsulate the mission of the organization and placed it on the page. During the month of December they ran an experiment to see how the additional value proposition affected project giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though there was a slight increase in project giving (that was not statistically valid), the added copy to the page produced across-the-board decreases in overall giving, which led them to end the experiment. This showed that the additional copy might have confused staff donors, even if it did motivate more people to make one-time gifts.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 21.0% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.
Desktop visitors had a 4.9% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 7.4% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 7.1% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Direct Traffic visitors had an 11.8% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 12.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.7% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How concise copy and additional clarity impacted donor conversions for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points for visitors will achieve an increase in clarity and comprehension.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2024 - 01/16/2024
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar campaign, we decided to run a copy test to condense some of the copy. Our hypothesis was that the more concise the copy, the higher level of comprehension for the potential donor therefore, leading them to the donation form quicker.
We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points will lead to increased clarity and comprehension. As a result, we believe the treatment version will outperform the control version in terms of donor understanding and response, leading to a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points in the copy had a very slight lift in the number of transactions. While the treatment version of the campaign showed a 1.1% increase in transactions, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.1%, falling below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

Furthermore, we saw a a drop of 35.3% in revenue between the control and treatment at a 12.1% level of confidence. While this did not validate, it is important to consider in future decision making that IMB's donors give less when they are given less value proposition, and more concise copy may lead to a lower average gift.

In future experimentation, it is important to carry out the A/B test for a longer duration to gather a larger sample size and increase the statistical power. This will help establish a more reliable and accurate conclusion about the effectiveness of the treatment version. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine and optimize the treatment version based on the insights gained from this experiment. Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further iterations and testing to drive improved results.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How Changing a Headline to a Universal Value Proposition Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the headline will not be talking about the transaction, but rather the value that a person would get if they choose to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/31/2024
Focus on the Family has several GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount/ sustainer of any amount) pages that they will drive traffic to each day from various sources. Each page is only advertised for a day or two. For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could create a headline that could lead people to convert more, but also make the headline broad enough that it could be used for all of the pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the donation pages can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this particular case, the treatment version of the headline resulted in an 18.5% increase in Transaction - Recurring for All Traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was only 59.5%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing headlines that focus on the value proposition for donors rather than the transaction itself. This approach seems to resonate with potential donors and may lead to higher conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that the experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive conclusions.

experiments.title: How Adding More Value Prop to a Campaign Donation Page Affects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the donaton page for the See Life campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the value of what is on the page will increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2024 - 05/15/2024
Focus on the Family runs a SeeLife campaign each year that receives a ton of traffic to its campaign donation page. The original campaign donation page was very short and did not have a ton of value proposition on it. We wondered if adding more value proposition, and length to the test could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more value proposition to the donation page for the See Life campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 76.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the donation page with varying levels of value proposition to see what resonates best with donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donor conversion, such as the overall user experience, the donation process, and the clarity of messaging on the page. By conducting thorough and valid experiments, Focus on the Family can gather meaningful insights to optimize their donation pages and ultimately increase donor conversion in the future.

experiments.title: How a simplified and user-focused value proposition affects sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop about the user’s journey and desired impact for the DDJ landing page  will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because this will better communicate the value and focus on answering why they should be interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2023 - 08/16/2023
After a previous test on the DDJ landing page that tested into layout, The Navigators wanted to turn their attention to testing the elements themselves. In this copy test, we hypothesized framing the language around the user and simplifying the copy to their desired impact rather than the Navigators would increase sign-up rate. Rather than answering why the organization created Digital Discipleship, the treatment is focused on asking questions directly to the user and positioning the resource as the answer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifyign the value prop   did not achieve the desired increase in sign-up rate for the DDJ landing page. The treatment actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control, however this was not at a valid level of confidence. More testing is required.
Some questions to consider are the ways in which this framing shifted the responsibility onto the reader. Although the attempt was to not focus on why the organization created the resource so much as the user's journey and the impact they want to make — it's possible this put too much pressure on the reader. What if they aren't ready to disciple or commit to "come alongside someone?" The original used these terms, but only in the sense of whether the readers themselves has ever had someone do this for them, not whether they want to do this for someone else.
Future experimentation should continue to test different approaches to framing the language and offer on the DDJ landing page to determine the most effective strategy for increasing sign-up rates. It is important to gather more data and achieve a higher statistical level of confidence in order to make valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How clarity around the problem and expanded value prop on main donate impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increase clarity around the problem and expanding value prop copy to specify how RTB is the solution for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because users will not only have better clarity around the problem RTB solves but also the impact a gift makes towards solving it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/26/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s main donation page, we believe there was an opportunity to leverage the value prop language we’d seen perform well on acquisition donation page and in campaigns. We hypothesized that adding more clarity around what the problem is and better illustrating how a gift to RTB helps solve it would improve conversion rate. A few specific callouts are the more action-oriented headline versus “Support Reasons to Believe. The inclusion of details about what types of questions RTB answers, whose writing the resources, and finally what’s really at stake when these questions go unanswered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing clarity around the problem and expanding the value proposition copy to specify how Reasons to Believe addresses the users' needs can significantly impact donor conversion rates and revenue generated. While donor conversion rate remained similar between control and treatment, the latter did show a substantial 103.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.5%, below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing variations of the value proposition copy and messaging to determine the most effective way to communicate the impact of donations to Reasons to Believe. Additionally, conducting experiments with larger sample sizes or running the test for a longer period may help increase the statistical confidence level of the results. Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that focusing on clearly articulating the problem and how RTB provides a solution can have a positive impact on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How reminding members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card did not have the intended impact on the click rate for the Members Card campaign. While the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 22.3%, which is not sufficient to draw a valid conclusion.

Note: The treatment email also had a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the wording and placement of value proposition texts in emails. It is possible that the added text in the treatment group did not effectively communicate the value of the Members Card or may have distracted recipients from the main call to action.

To improve the effectiveness of future experiments, it may be helpful to test different variations of value propositions, incorporate feedback from focus groups or surveys, and ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other factors such as the timing of the email, the design elements, and the target audience can also impact the success of the campaign.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition of a sticky bar impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition for the course sticky bar will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases the clarity of what someone will receive when they sign up for the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2024 - 02/13/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary regularly runs sticky bars on their voice.dts.edu website to advertise new courses, surveys, and more. They’ve previously tested into language that acknowledges them as a friend of DTS and use this language regularly.
For their new course, we wanted to test different language angles to see if we could get more people clicking into the course landing page.
For the first treatment, we wanted to test language about starting off the new year with a new course as it launched in January.
For the second treatment, we focused on the felt need that the course addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results showed that the treatment version resulted in a 3.1% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.7%, falling below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, while the treatment won, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

The key learning from this experiment is that changing the value proposition in the sticky bar can impact email conversions. However, to draw more concrete conclusions, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be necessary. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence to make reliable decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to navigation giving buttons impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons for the site navigation will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it increases the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 02/23/2024
Care Net tested into adding a “Give Monthly” button in their main navigation next to their main donate button. While this increased clarity, we wondered if we could increase the appeal of giving while adding even more clarity by adding additional copy below the main button text.
For the Donate button, we added the text, “Save a baby today.” For the Give Monthly button, we added the text, “Save babies 365 days a year.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons in the site navigation can potentially increase recurring donations by making the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis more prominent. The treatment, which added the text "Save a baby today" below the Donate button and "Save babies 365 days a year" below the Give Monthly button, resulted in a 67.0% increase in donations for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the treatment showed a promising outcome, it cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding additional copy below the main donation buttons to confirm if it consistently leads to increased donations. It is important to ensure that any findings have a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes based on the results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the copy or placement of the text could help optimize the impact on donation rates.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on a donation page impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the value propostion for Education CYE main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2023 - 12/11/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test if shortening the value proposition on the Education Donation Page to focus on one area instead of being very broad, could increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: By shortening the value proposition to only be positioned for year end, we where able to increase the donor conversion rate by 46.2% at a 75% level of confidence. While the result is not valid, it gives us a good baseline to expand on this test.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a sticky bar impacted donation conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the sticky bar will achieve a higher conversion rate because the appeal of the copy will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding value prop to the sticky bar could increase motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition to the sticky bar can increase motivation to give and result in a higher conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on acquisition donation pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for newly acquired names will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donation ask is enforced with year end motivations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2023 - 12/28/2023
Belmont Abbey College is a Catholic university located in North Carolina.
During calendar year-end, we decided to test the presence of a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages encouraging potential donors to make a year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a persuasive message on acquisition offer donation pages can potentially increase the conversion rate. The treatment version, which included the sticky bar with a specific call-to-action and a motivational message, resulted in a 31.5% increase in transactions for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be important to gather a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. A higher level of confidence is essential to ensure that any changes made based on the experiment results are valid and reliable.
Additionally, it would be worth considering testing variations of the sticky bar message, design, or placement to find the most effective combination. Exploring different strategies and optimizing the implementation of sticky bars can potentially lead to improved conversion rates and better engagement with potential donors.
Overall, this experiment provides a valuable insight into the potential impact of adding a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages. However, further experimentation is needed to validate and refine the results before implementing any changes on a larger scale.

experiments.title: Does additional value prop drive results for a transactional type email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value prop and missional language for email audience will achieve a higher conversion rate because the reason for the purchase is more thoroughly fleshed out. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/11/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In November and December PTV has seen great response to two premiums, the 2024 Pathway to Victory Daily Devotional and You Can Pray Big Things.
In an effort to drive a bit more response, we decided to send an email encouraging people to make their last minute Christmas purchases by December 7th so that they will arrive in time to homes.
For this email, we decided to test a very short copy, straight to the point versus a longer copy with more missional and value prop language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the shorter, straight-to-the-point copy performed better than the longer copy with more missional and value prop language. Although the results are not statistically significant, there is a trend indicating that the shorter copy may be more effective in driving donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the effectiveness of shorter, concise messaging in driving conversions. This could involve testing different variations of the short copy, or comparing it against other types of messaging, such as emotional appeals or storytelling.

Additionally, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence in the results is sufficient before drawing any conclusions. Conducting tests with a larger sample size or running the experiment for a longer duration may help achieve a higher level of confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is that concise messaging may have a positive impact on conversion rates, and this should be further explored in future experimentation.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the value proposition of a donation with an impact statement on the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving will increase donations by helping to overcome hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the impact of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving can potentially increase donations and help overcome hesitancy at the point of completing a transaction. Although the treatment showed a 19.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence of 72.3% is below the threshold of 85% needed for the results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving. The results of this experiment provide promising insights that can be further explored and tested with larger sample sizes to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, it is important to evaluate different variations of the statement to identify the most effective messaging to drive donations.

experiments.title: How updating value proposition on acquisition donation pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net’s mission will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
In the last few months, there have been some major social movements in the pro-life space, including the overturning of Roe v. Wade and the federal government announcing that they will be developing a Federal Reproductive Health Care Hotline. The hotline in particular is direct competition to one of Care Net’s ministries: the Pregnancy Decision Line. We wanted to see how updating the language to center around this particular issue would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net's mission resulted in a decrease in donor conversion and revenue. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on these learnings, it is important for Care Net to conduct further experimentation to validate the impact of updating the value proposition. The organization should explore other strategies and hypotheses to understand the factors contributing to the decrease in donor conversion. This can involve testing different messaging, design elements, or targeting strategies.

Future experimentation should be conducted for a longer duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical significance.

experiments.title: How adding goal language to a sticky bar impacts donors conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more specificity in a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more urgency is communicated by the goal language .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
RTB was running a fiscal year end campaign and implemented a site-wide sticky bar to generate more gifts. In order to increase donor conversion, we added more specificity and urgency to the sticky bar by adding the goal amount and a time to the deadline. The hypothesis is that the urgency language and specific dollar amount will drive more motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more specificity and urgency to a sticky bar did not achieve the desired result of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations. We saw a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results are not considered valid.
However, based on the data collected, it may be worth exploring other factors that could have influenced the decrease in donations. It is possible that the addition of the specific dollar amount and deadline in the treatment version may not capture new donors who need more context for the campaign. In fact, the control brought in 6 new donors while the treatment only brought in 2 new donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of the sticky bar, focusing on other value propositions for the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials in the donation page value proposition impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials through the value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the issue will feel closer to home for donation page visitors and they’ll be more inspired to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis instant donation page is producing a a low conversion rate. We wondered if enhancing the value proposition messaging through evidentials would better engage donation page visitors, as the messaging would feel closer to home and they’d therefore want to make a donation to help improve the border crisis situation.
The control uses more general value proposition while the treatment highlights specific threats to Texas citizens and examples of the harm the border crisis is causing to these citizens.
Let’s see which approach appeals more to our audience in terms of urging them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, the treatment resulted in a 45.2% decrease in donations with a 78.6% level of confidence. Potential donors did not respond to the treatment evidential-infused value proposition copy like we expected them to. Perhaps they already feel they have a strong grasp of the problem and therefore were not moved by the evidentials. In this case, we would want to hone in on the solution side of the formula more so than the problem side.
The treatment also produced a 29.5% decrease in revenue with a 73.6% level of confidence.
Returning visitors produced an 88% decrease in donations to the treatment with a valid 97.6% level of confidence - 1 donation to the treatment compared to 7 to the control. This provides further proof that our returning visitors already have a strong grasp of the problem.
Donors may care more about how their donation would make a difference in resolving the issues caused by the border crisis. Especially on this donation page where they're being asked to make a monthly donation, it's likely even more important to stress the reasons to give.
It would be interesting to run a test where we isolate the reasons to give between the control variant and the treatment variant to see how that impacts giving. In this instance, the treatment reasons to give focus more on stopping the bad guys (cartels) while the control reasons to focus more on implementing better policy. It would be interesting to isolate these to see which motivates our audience more to make donations.

experiments.title: How urgency-focused value proposition copy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that urgency value proposition language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because visitors will feel a sense of urgency to give and make an impact that they otherwise wouldn’t.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/23/2023
DTS is amid a high-urgency fiscal year-end campaign. As our org is pushing a large number of users to our fiscal year-end donation pages, we want to test into urgency-focused donation page copy.
As part of our FYE campaign, we launched a free content offer where users can sign up to receive the eBook, and once they do, they’re redirected an instant donation page. Our existing donation page deploys standard value proposition messaging through problem/solution copy and reasons to give; however, it does not include important FYE messaging including urgency language, the campaign deadline, campaign goal, or matching gift challenge associated with the campaign. The only urgent element it includes is a countdown clock, but it does not explain what the clock is counting down to.
We want to test urgency-focused messaging that does include these elements and mentions, and that grabs the user’s attention immediately, alerting them of this urgent opportunity to make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The urgency-focused language did not produce the results that we expected. After running for 17 days, the treatment resulted in a 9.6% decrease in donations with a 40.4% level of confidence. It also produced a 4% decrease in revenue with a 56.7% level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that urgency-focused language does not always lead to more donations. It can provide the urgency, the flash, and the untraditional that could inspire someone to give. Though, in this case, I wonder if the supporting value proposition copy was not as strong as the control. The control includes strong problem/solution copy and highlights specifically how a donor's gift will be used, while the treatment includes more general value proposition copy that never really explains the problem(s) that a donor's gift might help solve and how the gift would be used to solve them.
For a future iteration, it would be wise to blend the value proposition copy from the control with the urgency elements used in the treatment. This could be the formula for increase donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for course enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 05/07/2023
This theological seminary client offers a few online courses as a biblical resource. On the Psalms Course resource, we wanted to test specific language in the copy of the donation page to see if it would produce a higher conversion rate.
The control donation page copy includes a donation ask that shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the next published biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed language to fund their next biblical resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with a 29.9% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include specific details about what the donor will read, ie. chapter topics, so the donor will have a better expectation of what they will be receiving if they donate.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline + congruent value prop messaging impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a continuation headline and more congruent reasons to give consistent with the theme of the funnel will achieve an increase in donations among donation page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/04/2023
ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to similar offers. Click through rate at the ad level and petition sign up rate are very healthy, but we’re struggling to get donors to convert once they reach the instant donation page.
Reviewing the current donation page, two things stood out:
1) The headline comes off as conclusive and does not imply that there is another action the petition signer can take.
2) The reasons to give in the last paragraph are not on theme with the petition subject and therefore could be creating a disconnect that turns the page visitor away.
We developed a treatment that implements a continuation headline and closing value proposition copy (reasons to give) that align with the action they just took (signing a petition to stop judicial weaponization).
Let’s see how the treatment performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results were not what we anticipated. The treatment resulted in a 6.6% decrease in donations with a 20.1% level of confidence. Revenue to the treatment resulted in an 18% decrease with a 67.6% level of confidence. Ultimately, neither variant moved the needle.
Interestingly, there was a notable difference between mobile and desktop visitors:
Mobile: Treatment produced a 95.1% increase in donations with an 85.7% LoC
Desktop: Treatment produced a 42.5% decrease in donations with an 88.2% LoC
These outcomes came close to validating, however, it's difficult to put a finger on what led to these vastly different user responses given that the copy length stayed roughly the same and there was little difference in the actual visibility of the page.
We'll keep these results in mind and see if we can test this similar approach for other offers. If we do, we'll monitor to see if results are consistent or different to the ones we've recorded here.

experiments.title: How adding appeal and exclusivity to course sign up value proposition copy impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity for landing page visitors will increase sign up rate because visitors will gain an improved understanding of what they’ll get out of the course and how the course will improve their relationship with Christ.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/29/2023
In an effort to increase sign up rate to DTS’ Intro to Prophets online course, we built a sign up page treatment focusing on enhancing the appeal and exclusivity with the value proposition copy. We feel these elements are lacking in the existing copy.
For exclusivity, we added language including “Don’t miss this rare opportunity”, “This one-of-a-kind course”, and “become part of an exclusive community of like-minded learners.”
For appeal, we transitioned the copy to focus more on the potential course participant and the course itself instead of the organization and the organization’s mission, and added more action sentences to hopefully invoke the action of signing up.
Let’s see how the adjusted value prop performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity can potentially increase the sign-up rate for a course. However, the statistical level of confidence of 32.6% is well below the threshold of 85% and the results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment produced a minimal 0.98% lift in course sign ups.
Downstream metrics were minimal as well, with the treatment producing a 5% increase in donations with just a 22.5% level of confidence, and revenue decreasing by 2.7% with a 55.7% level of confidence.
The added appeal and exclusivity did not enhance user motivation and increase course sign up like we hypothesized it would. We will need to revisit the value proposition copy to brainstorm alternative motives to increase sign ups. Or consider testing opportunities elsewhere on the sign up page.
Further experiments need to be conducted with a larger sample size and more significant changes to the treatment. Future experiments should focus on creating a strong value proposition that directly addresses the needs of the potential course participants and highlights the unique features of the course. Additionally, the experiment should explore other factors that could potentially impact the sign-up rate, such as the design of the landing page and the placement of the call-to-action button. Overall, the experiment provides a starting point for further exploration of how to increase sign-up rates for online courses, but a larger sample size and more significant changes are needed to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription opportunity impacted email opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will gain clarity in what they’ll get from signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2023 - 05/21/2023
In an effort to get more people to sign up for IPI’s newsletter, we built a treatment that added value proposition copy above the newsletter sign up form. We highlighted what type of news coverage subscribers could expect, added more call-to-action language, and expresses the importance of being an engaged citizen.
The control version lacks each of these elements, so we’re interested to see if the treatment will result in an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, the experiment resulted in only one email opt-in per variant. Thus, the experiment had no winner and the lack of email conversions prevented any valid result. Value proposition does not see to be the issue. We should test different elements of the page to isolate the problem. Perhaps there was too much friction for visitors given the various check boxes they're asked to consider. A new test could be around simplifying the page to reduce decision friction.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Do different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase downstream conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
Although there was no difference in clickthrough rate, treatment 3 (with the emotional appeal) produced a 67% increase in revenue through a combination of an increase in conversion rate and a simultaneous increase in average gift. This shows that while the initial metric was not affected, those who chose to click on the third treatment had a much higher motivation to give, which resulted in more gifts at larger sizes.
Treatment 3 offered something that none of the other treatments did—a way to show love and support for the journal author. While the other three treatments showed ways that the user could support the site and connected the site’s impact with the author, the third treatment gave them a direct connection to the person they care for, and proposed a donation as the way to show love and support. This produced an opportunity to give that no other treatment could match.
This experiment prompts more testing around this language throughout the site, as well as testing around how to increase clickthrough rate on the donate box through accentuated placement and visual UI.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.

The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They tested three different treatments to see if they could produce a lift in clickthrough rate:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
 	The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
 	The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
none of these experiments produced a significant lift in clickthrough rate

experiments.title: How simplifying the value proposition in a call to action impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying and focusing the value proposition language for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will add clarity to the call to action and reduce friction .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 04/11/2023
CaringBridge has a tribute widget on authors’ journal pages. This is an opportunity to make a donation as a tribute to keep the author’s site running. When looking at the original copy used in this tribute widget, we suspected that the value proposition used in the call to action might have competing motivations. It asked the person to be one X people who have already given and were given the suggested dollar amount to power the person’s site for an entire month. Plus, it asks if you can keep the site on for you and the author. Our hypothesis was that this call to action was actually adding friction to the decision making process. We wanted to simplify the call to action. Additionally we wanted to test if the removal of “CaringBridge” in the CTA would make the ask less organizational centric and clearer. The second treatment also focuses on just making the gift in honor of the person.
Treatment 1: A $30 donation to CaringBridge powers a site like Jan’s for one month. Will you join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of Laura with your donation today?
Treatment 2: X donors have made a donation in honor of Laura. Will you join them with your donation today?
The first treatment simplifies the ask so that it does not ask the person to keep the site online for them and for you. The second treatment simplifies the CTA even further by removing mention of CaringBridge and how much is needed to power a site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in no conclusive "winner" when looking at donor conversion rate. Treatment 1 resulted in a 2.8% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 31% while treatment 3 resulted in a 3.4% increase in donations with a 38.4% statistical level of confidence.
These treatments resulted in no change in conversion rate but there does seem to be a valid increase in revenue. Treatment 1 produced a 33% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 98%. Treatment 2 produced a 19.5% lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 92%.
This implies that while the language doesn't inspire more people to give, it does motivate people to give even larger gifts. It's likely that the first treatment provided a larger lift in revenue than treatment 2 because it kept the specific $30 ask in the copy.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition language on a content offer landing page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they know what they will be helping achieve by donating..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. As part of our efforts to drive new name and new donor acquisition through content offers, we wanted to run a test on landing page copy to see whether being more concise with what a donation would get would impact conversion rates. This test ran across all acquisition offers on all channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to potential donors may not necessarily result in an increase in conversions. The treatment resulted in a 6.2% decrease in donations with a 40.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better. However, since the statistical level of confidence falls below 85%, we cannot consider these results valid.
For future experiments, we can continue to test which specific points of clarity will help increase donor conversions. Since Hoover is a think tank, the immediate connection between a donation and a tangible impact is a harder case to make; continued language testing will help clarify that.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations – attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will have an improved understanding of the impact their gift would have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 03/31/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
It’s important to note that we ran a previous expanded value proposition test on this main donation page roughly a month ago and it produced a 28% drop in donation conversion rate with a 85.9% level of confidence. The focus of that test was to bring attention to the “reasons why” someone should give. We laid out five reasons via bullet points but the approach was deemed unsuccessful per our audience’s response. You can view the experiment write up at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/pRdg
Our hypothesis stemming from our previous experiment’s results was that that because someone who clicks a “make a donation” button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to “stay out of their way” by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
For this experiment, we eliminated the “reasons why” someone should give – due to the high motivation someone has when clicking “make a donation,” we also hypothesized that they already have at least one reason to give in mind and therefore do not need more reasons presented to them. Here we cut down the copy from our previous test while still expanding the value proposition copy on the current control variant, this time focusing on a broader message of how their gift would help our nation today.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 30 days, this experiment produced flat results. The treatment resulted in a 0.27% decrease in donations with a less than 1% level of confidence. The drop was not as extensive as our previous donation page value proposition experiment, which further supports our hypothesis that the previous experiment's extensive copy was not well-perceived by our potential donors. However, the less extensive, but still enhanced value proposition we used for the treatment in this experiment still did not produce positive results.
Returning visitors saw a 73% increase in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence, while new visitors saw a 34.5% decrease in donations with an 83.3% level of confidence. It's difficult to explain the negative results of new visitors as that's the segment we would expect would benefit from expanded value prop and learning more about our cause.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page may not always be effective in increasing donations. It is important to consider that visitors who click on the "make a donation" button are already highly motivated to give, and adding more copy may create unnecessary friction.
The control variant continues to stand. For a next main donation page test, we may want to consider taking the existing language from our control and expanding specifically on it. The control mentions "America's founding principles" both in the headline and the main copy. Perhaps that phrase has great importance, clarity, and appeal to our audience, and instead of focusing on copy on something else like core pillars, we should use America's founding principles to expand on within our messaging.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to an online education program sign up page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a program sign up page for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will have a better understanding of the program and the value it provides.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 03/17/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute has a program called Little Patriots that offers lessons, activities, videos, cartoons, workbooks and more tailored toward children to teach them the story of America. Parents and teachers can sign up on behalf of their children to gain access to this content, and they would do so through the Little Patriots sign up page.
Currently, this page contains just a standard sign up form – it contains no value proposition copy and the headline simply says “REGISTRATION”. We wondered if the implementation of value proposition copy coupled with a descriptive call to action headline would lead to an increase in Little Patriots program sign ups. We’ve run countless tests that support this approach. Audiences typically respond well to value proposition and descriptive call to actions because the elements present value/benefit to the reader/prospect and gives them a clear path to the intended action. Let’s see how the approach works in this scenario…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 1.7% lift in email conversion rate with just a 22.6% level of confidence. The results do not dictate that the treatment should be rolled out or applied to the page moving forward. As the vast majority of page visitors come from the Little Patriots home page, there is a good chance they already have a strong understanding of the program and its benefits and do not need the help of value proposition copy on the registration page to convince them to register. In other words, the value is already clear in their minds and registering is just a formality.
The evidence is in the results. The treatment produced a 2.1% decrease in sign ups among returning visitors while new visitors produced a 1.9% lift. Still, the 1.9% lift only had a 22.5% level of confidence.
Paid social traffic produced a 9.6% decrease in email sign ups from the treatment, which could be explained by value being communicated to the user at both the Facebook ad level and the Little Patriots home page before they reach the sign up page.
Ultimately, value proposition may not be the most urgent focus for improving sing up rate. There are other approaches/elements we can try along the lines of social proof or credibility.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for study guide downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2023 - 02/27/2023
Luther Seminary was getting great responses for their free study guide on the book of Revelation in terms of email signups. However, they wanted to see if they could increase their instant donor conversion rate.
In reviewing the value proposition on their donation page, they noticed that the reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. They hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, producing more biblical resources like what the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, they drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, the treatment page saw a 93.2% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.9% level of confidence.

This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the biblical resources it promotes.

*However, it must be noted that this experiment was limited in its promotion due to budget, and thus only 10 total donations were included. While the gap in conversion rates was significant, it did not reach NextAfter's desired threshold of 50 donations.

experiments.title: How revamping the value proposition presented in the headline and body copy with specific, solution-focused language affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by more effectively communicating the impact of a potential donor’s gift and how it will be achieved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 02/06/2023
In an effort to improve conversion rates on an instant donation page for a public policy client, we wanted to know how stregthening the value proposition by adding a headline and solution-focused body copy would impact potential donors’ motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 14.3% increase in donations (30.7% level of confidence) and a 13.2% increase in overall revenue (62% level of confidence), however, we did not receive a large enough sample size of donations to reach statistically valid conclusions about our results. That being said, because the treatment did not have a negative effect on donations or revenue, we have recommended rolling out the treatment to all donation page traffic.

It is significant to note that among visitors obtained through paid social media (the primary acquisition source for this offer), we observed a 121.5% increase in donations with a 91.0% level of confidence.

This leads us to believe that the conclusion of a value prop-driven headline and body copy that is solution-focused and specific about why a donation is needed, what it will accomplish, and how increases the motivation for potential donors to give. Based on these results we will look for other opportunities to improve the value proposition on donation pages in an effort to increase donation rates.

experiments.title: How detailing more value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition  for the Scouting survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2022 - 11/12/2022
The Boy Scouts of America were running a survey to all alumni and people associated with Scouting to gauge their thoughts on Scouting and to try and acquire more names of fellow Scouts.
Following the survey, we had an instant donation page that had minimal value propositions for why they should make a gift to BSA. Because the ICR was low on this page, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donation to copy that detailed their value proposition more and explained better what a gift to BSA would do for Scouting.
The copy we used in the treatment was copy similar to the copy we used on a high-urgency appeal the previous month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 41.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 62.2% level of confidence. We had to shut this test off before reaching proper validation because we turned off spend for this acquisition offer.
Because this did not reach validity and only had 11 total gifts, we would like to test this again on another acquisition offer's instant donation page.

experiments.title: How emphasizing copy and expanding the value proposition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing key sentences within the copy and offering real-life application through the copy for page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 10/28/2022
TGC’s Secular Creed eBook funnel was lacking in instant donor conversion rate. Despite having a high sign-up rate for the offer, those who signed up were not converting at the end of the funnel by not donating through the instant donation page. We wondered if the subpar results were due to a lack of motivation. Therefore, we developed a copy treatment that expanded the value proposition, adding real-life application by talking about how resources like this one will help you resolve conflict and have impactful conversions with friends, neighbors, coworkers, and family members. We also bolded key sentences to get the message across in a stronger fashion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were inconclusive. Both the control and treatment led to 5 donations, and the treatment ultimately had just a 0.70% lift and only a 0.88% level of confidence. Potential donors gave little indication as to which donation page copy most resonated with them.

It's also difficult to know which components of the treatment helped or hurt donor conversion rate, as there were too many differences between the control and the treatment. The treatment had a longer headline, inconsistent bolding, and different copy overall. We may want to isolate these elements in future tests on this donation page.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 09/18/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to lifts in revenue for this acquisition offer. We saw a 39.1% increase in average gift with a lift of 75.4% level of confidence. This experiment had been running for over a month and had not been validated therefore we moved traffic to the treatment page with confidence that the value prop copy will increase the giving amount on this page.
While we saw a lift in average gift, the results for donor conversion rate were flat at 4.3% lift with a 7.34% level of confidence.
We believe that the value proposition copied inspired potential donors more to give a higher gift, but was not enough to convince someone to give if they already decided against it. Although it did not negatively affect the decision to make a gift.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: The impact of Fiscal Year End Language on the donation page for the fiscal year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding fiscal year end language will increase donor conversion on the main donation page during the Fiscal Year-End campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 08/27/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were using a premium offer, Find Your People and a donor goal. They wanted to see if adding value proposition on the donation page surrounding Fiscal Year-End and the importance of ending the year “strong” would increase donor conversion on the donation page. No other elements included this language, just the main donation page. The control page would discuss the donor goal and the premium, the treatment would include language about the fiscal year ending and why that matters to the work FamilyLife does to strengthen families worldwide.
We split traffic 50/50 and ran it for just over two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 257 donations, there wasn't a clear improvement on donor conversion or revenue from the treatment. We did see an 80% Level Of Confidence for an improvement on revenue, but when digging into the segments, there were conflicting results between leading sources of traffic to their site and large gifts may have skewed these results. Due to the importance of ending the campaign strong and the desire to launch another test in the last days of the campaign (a sitewide popup that would prompt visitors to complete their donation) we ended this test.
We will see if we can test this approach again next year, but add the Fiscal Year End language to the emails as well as to the donation page to see if this holistic approach increases conversion by keeping that language consistent from email to page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a premium landing page impacted donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop langauge for premium-resource driven donors will achieve a change in conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Their featured offer in May was Invincible: Conquering the Mountains That Separate You from the Blessed Life.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this specific offer, adding in value proposition language to the landing page for the book led to a decrease in both conversion rates and revenue per donor of 14.2% and 8.3% respectively. Despite an increase in average gift, the overall result was a decline in revenue for the experiment variant page.
Our hypothesis is that the majority of traffic to these book offer pages is driven directly from TV and radio programs promoting the book; those that reach that page want to purchase the book and don't have that much of an interest in value proposition.
However, it will be interesting to keep track of the 'value prop' donors long term, as our hypothesis is that by buying into the value prop of WHY they should donate to PTV instead of another ministry, they will become better long-term donors, and not just those looking to buy one book and that is the extent of their support.

experiments.title: How adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy affect conversions for new users
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy of a landing page will yield better conversion metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2022 - 08/27/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if adding more value proposition into the headline and body copy of the email acquisition landing page for a popular content offer would result in a greater number of email conversions. While the control was very clear, the treatment alluded that the resource would provide a “blueprint for success”…thus potentially eliminating complexity. This came from comments from new readers that prayer life can sometimes be complicated, so we wanted to see how the treatment affected new visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had a very slight lift of just 3% with a 40% level of confidence. The results were inconclusive as the experiment did not have enough samples or LoC to validate. Since we did not see a directional lift, we cannot say if this experiment had any positive impact. As the experiment did have an adverse effect on returning visitors, future iterations of this experiment might be limited to just new visitors.

experiments.title: How adding more value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition statements into the body copy of a donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2021 - 01/22/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives.
