For this experiment, they wanted to test if changing the body copy of the donation page to infuse more value proposition statements that align with the beliefs and values of the org would result in a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had a very small lift of 8% with about a 9% LoC. The results from this experiment were inconclusive and not valid due to a low LoC and small sample size. While we have plenty of experiments that validate for increased value proposition where we connect the "why", this [particular experiment probably had too many variables within the copy to have a bigger impact. Future versions of this experiment might be to shorten the body copy and to limit the amount of value proposition statements to focus attention on one thing.

experiments.title: How adding value prop before a petition impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating cognitive momentum with additional value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2020 - 12/09/2020
We were running a petition and were looking for ways to improve email conversion rate on the landing page. Although the Facebook ad gave supporting reasons to sign the petition, we noticed the control landing page went straight into the petition statement without giving any additional context for why someone should add their name to the petition. We wondered if adding clarity and urgency for visitors would help continue the cognitive momentum from one step to the next and developed a treatment that carried over value proposition into a brief paragraph and an additional subheader before leading into the beginning of the petition statement. We split traffic to both versions and ran the test for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the test for 3 weeks, we saw a 1.4% increase in conversion rate with 45.6% level of confidence so the experiment results are not valid. We needed a greater sample size for further indication of whether or not the treatment version was working. Upon review, the new intro paragraph in the treatment used organization-centric language that could have led to flat results. In future tests on this page, we will optimize the copy to be more donor-centric while testing the same concept. We could also implement some design features on the page that more clearly separate the petition language from the introductory value proposition to reduce confusion and friction in the mind of the page visitor.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps increases donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the steps to donate for ebook downloaders will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 06/27/2022
Since changing giving platforms, the Navigators giving experience is now a 3 step process after requesting an ebook on a landing page. After a site visitor fills out the form to get an offer, they’re taken to a landing page with the value prop language and button on the bottom to give. When people click on the button they’re taken to a generic giving page with a gift array. They are then given the option to “add to basket” and are taken to a page to fill out their credit card information. Our hypothesis is that if we can reduce this experience down to 2 steps, site visitors will experience less friction and we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
To test this concept we ran this experiment through a specific funnel/offer: “Fight on my knees” ebook. Traffic to this funnel is mainly driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 65% lift in donor conversion rate but we only saw a level of confidence of 53.9% which means this experiment did not validate. Even after running for over a month, this funnel did not get enough traffic in order to acquire a large enough sample size. It is recommended that this either retested in a different funnel that has more traffic or we test using a 1 page donation process if at all possible.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value of a premium offer impact donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: How longer, value prop-based language impacts donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that longer, value-prop focused language on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/25/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for their Missionary Care fund. In order to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted to test longer, value prop-focused language on the donation page. Our hypothesis is that the longer copy will drive more donations because it is giving the prospective donor a fuller reason to give towards Missionary Care. Traffic to this donation page came from campaign emails and sticky bar traffic from their website.
experiments.keyLearnings: While initially the results seemed to indicate that the longer copy outperformed the shorter, direct copy, the experiment ultimately resulted in a 17% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 57%. The treatment made no substantial difference to the end results. This concept can be retested by moving the gift array to the bottom oof the donation page so that the site visitor needs to read through the copy before getting to the intended call to action. It's likely that because the gift array was at the top, some site visitors may not have scrolled down to read the intended copy.

experiments.title: How removing an image and adding a reader-centric headline affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image and providing a reader-centric headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2022 - 06/23/2022
The Navigators were running acquisition ads to promote their 7 Tips to Help Others Grow in Christ offer. As they tried to optimize ad creative, they also looked at the landing page to see if there was anything that might be hindering conversion.
They hypothesized that the size of the image might be pushing the form and value proposition too far down the page (especially on mobile), and that removing the image might increase conversion. At the same time, they also noticed that the headline simply declared the title of the offer, and didn’t offer any value to the reader. They decided to test this as well in a “C” variant, which compounded on top of the image removal.
They split the traffic three ways to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither experiment had a material impact on conversion, regardless of device type. This showed them that the image was not hindering conversion (and removal of it didn't help), and that the new headline did not increase motivation or clarity to accept the offer.
They wanted to try a radical redesign next that dramatically altered the layout of the page to see if that would move the needle.

experiments.title: The impact of reinforcing the value of a premium offer on donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer and value proposition language impacts donor conversion in a higher urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity through value proposition language and motivation through a giving thermometer for potential monthly donors will achieve increased monthly donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2021 - 10/01/2021
RTB was running their monthly partner campaign in which their goal was to grow their number of monthly donors. We ran a test on RTB’s monthly partner donation page where two variables were tested.
We added value proposition language to each monthly giving button/option. The thought was that we could increase donor conversion rate by providing clarity on what their gift is going towards.
We also added a thermometer that indicated new monthly donors compared to RTB’s monthly partner campaign goal. We thought that adding clarity through value prop to what different levels of monthly giving can provide in addition to a visible goal that visitors can contribute to would lead to more monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in fewer conversions than the control; however, the difference was marginal.  This experiment resulted in a 68% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 70%. Therefore the results of this experiment did not validate. The run time of the experiment was only a week and could have validated given more time. However, there were two vastly different concepts/variables tested in this experiment. It is recommended that this is retested by isolating a single variable. From this experiment, we don't know if the fewer conversions were a result of the thermometer, added value prop, or both.

experiments.title: How reiterating the value proposition by the “submit” button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language by the submit button for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2021 - 01/30/2022
TFAS was running an offer (Democratic Socialism eBook) that had a high sign-up rate and a high gross cost per donor. In an effort to inspire more donations on the instant donation page we added a sentence directly underneath the “submit” button on the donation form. This sentence was value proposition-focused and would serve as a reminder of what their gift is going towards.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment did not validate. There was no statistical difference between those who saw the value prop language underneath the button vs those who did not. Our hypothesis is that this may have been too small of a difference with the given traffic to produce a valid result. The next step is to test this among multiple instant donation pages in order to produce valid learning.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 75.1% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Adding Additional Value Proposition on a Donation Page Can Increase Revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional value proposition for prayer pledge signers will achieve an increase in the revenue or the number of donors for this offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/28/2022
e3 Partners Ministry created a Prayer Pledge as a content offer and has seen significant positive results in terms of name acquisition ($0.17 gross cost per email). However, the instant donor conversion rate is incredibly low (0.2%).
For this test, e3 Partners implemented added value proposition to their donation page to see if it would increase donors acquired or overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test involved adding a single line of copy with an additional value proposition at the end of the copy, just above the donation form. Otherwise, the copy was the same. It would be worthwhile to do a more radical test on the donation page, further aligning with the sentiment of that last sentence, as well as incorporating ongoing initiatives to increase urgency.
 	This test was run prior to the optimization of their acquisition efforts within Facebook, including more effective targeting, resulting in an even lower cost per click and lower cost per name. It would be interesting to see how instant donor conversion would be impacted if more clear value propositions were incorporated with the influx of new people coming through the funnel.

experiments.title: How user focused messaging impacts donor conversion from the donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a user focused message increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2021 - 08/30/2021
After FamilyLife launched their new brand, they added some of their new brand messaging to the primary navigation header next to the donate button. The messaging read, “Because Family Matters Most”. We hypothesized that a message that was more user focused might help increase conversion from the donation button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for six weeks, we weren't able to validate the overall results. That said, on desktop devices there was a 13.7% decrease in donor conversion with a 89% level of confidence, but on mobile devices there was a 22% increase in donor conversion with a 94% level of confidence. This difference in conversion results is why the experiment didn't validate overall.
These findings are interesting in that mobile traffic is much more motivated by user centric messaging. We hypothesize that the placement and presence of this copy is also so much more visible on mobile devices which resulted in mobile visitors who were maybe on the fence about donating, made the decision to donate after seeing the messaging that had a clearer, user focused call-to-action.

experiments.title: How changing the value prop to focus more on the speaker affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy around Ted Cruz for those that visit the page will achieve a increase in conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2021 - 05/01/2021
This experiment was run on the registration page of their WWCB that was promoted on both Facebook and in email prospecting. The control copy focused more on the WWCB event itself with special guest Ted Cruz. With the Treatment copy, we focused more on Ted Cruz and specifically what he was going to be talking about on the call.
The thought behind this is people would be more interested and it would have more pull if we talked about what Ted Cruz would be discussing during the WWCB rather than talking about the event itself which happens every week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this did not validate we did see a 15% increase in conversion rate. The control saw a 3.1% conversion rate while the treatment saw a 3.5% instant conversion rate.

By focusing on Ted Cruz specifically and adding a bullet point list of what he was going to cover and talk about during the WWCB event we saw it lead to an increase in conversion rate and revenue as a whole. We will test this value prop again for future registrations to see if we continue to see similar results and if this will validate.

 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Identity Language affects donor conversion on a fiscal year end donation page
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Bringing clarity to who the donation helps will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2021 - 07/09/2021
During the Oblates Fiscal Year End campaign, we wanted to test identity language on different elements of the campaign. We tested this language on the donation page during the last two days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there was a distinct increase in giving from the email test, the donation page did not show as clear an impact. Our hypothesis is that the motivation was higher for all donation page visitors than for the people who opened the email. Further testing needs to be done for how we can use this strategy in the future.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of the wiki banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can increasing the clarity of the value proposition on the wiki banner increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2021 - 07/15/2021
Focus on the Family uses a banner at the top of their website during high urgency campaigns to capture site traffic and convert them into donors. The messaging for this banner is always in alignment with the campaign messaging. It usually includes a headline and a direct call to action. We hypothesized that we could increase donor conversion by increasing the clarity of the value proposition in the banner. To do this, we developed a treatment that communicated the problem and presented a call-to-action as the solution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For all traffic, there was no positive or negative impact on donor conversion. That said, this neutral result is due to the significant differences in impact with opposing segments. Specifically, when looking at new visitors to the site, there was a 4% decrease in conversion; while with returning visitors, there was a 5% increase in donor conversion. What we can learn from this experiment is that the motivation of users on the site are wildly different and the additional value proposition is needed for some site visitors, but not all. This can be dependent upon other messaging they have seen in other channels, and even people's familiarity with the organization. One specific trend we saw in the data is that when people were coming from an outside channel where they may have read or seen messaging for the campaign, the increased value proposition helped to increase donor conversion when people got to the page because the message they had previously read was reinforced through the wiki banner on the top of the website.
Other observations of the impact on visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 14.7% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 16.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 47.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 74.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 36.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 4.1% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 4.7% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a monthly donation ask email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for a monthly donor ask will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/11/2021
In this monthly donor ask email, Live Action tested an expanded value proposition to become a monthly donor over a shortened version that left out a lot of the benefits of becoming a Life Defender.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight lift in clicks from the expanded value proposition treatment, but ultimately this experiment proved invalid.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift, increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.
When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.
When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.
It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.
Because of this, and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How positioning what happens if a donor doesn’t give rather than what happens if they do impact donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the “fear of loss” (eg what won’t happen if the donor doesn’t give right now) will impact donor conversion rate when soliciting donors within the context of a campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/10/2021
As a part of The Fund for American Studies’ recent Founder’s Day campaign, they were asking high capacity donors to contribute to building a matching gift fund that would be used to challenge other donors to join the movement to rally right now and do more to help more students and teachers in 2021.
We wanted to experiment with what would motivate donors to take action now: either (a) the hope of what could be achieved through their gift right now, or (b) what won’t happen without their gift right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We achieved neither the minimum number of samples we needed nor did we observe a wide enough variance between the two treatments to reliably validate that one variant over the other.
We knew that validating this experiment might be too difficult, considering we were running it against such a small universe of names (of very few high capacity donors on the email file).
For the sake of thoroughness, we decided to check the statistics related to a revenue lift study based upon these results — and we only observed a +6.5% increase in revenue for the treatment, but with only a level of confidence (LoC) of only 56%. Finally, the number of samples achieved on a revenue lift study was nowhere near where we'd need it to be, and it only achieved a "coin flip" likelihood to improve results by only a few percentage points.
All of this is to say: Further experimentation should be considered moving forward but on a much wider universe of available prospects by which we can potentially validate a lift for one variant over another.

experiments.title: How do campaign emails focused on specific outreaches compare to general campaign emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on specific outreaches individually will increase the donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/29/2021
In preparation for KCBI’s Spring Campaign, we were curious if listing all of the outreaches the an organization is fundraising for clouds the impact and value proposition for each individual outreach. In other words, does talking about keeping radio on the air, saving lives with ultrasounds, and bringing Bibles to prisoners all in one email lessen the value prop and so the donor response? Would it be better to talk about each individual outreach in an email bring more depth the the value of the individual outreaches and so improve donor response. So we tested this theory by splitting their housefile into two groups. One group got 4 emails that talked through all of the outreaches in all four emails. The other group got 4 emails that focused on each outreach individually (i.e. one email was focused on ultrasounds, one email was focused on prison ministries).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcome of this test was inconclusive, but we have a few hypotheses as to why.

First, it is important to note that there was no significant increase in the click rate on the different emails. There was a 13% increase at a 66% Level of Confidence, so nothing concrete or indicative of higher motivation either way.

Our hypothesis is that there was not enough distinction in the messages. Many of the test emails replace a paragraph with outreach specific message. If we were to retest this, we would make the emails more focused on the outreaches and make them more central to the message.

However, as we looked in Google Analytics, we found the level of traffic to the donation page was the same for the test and the control. Meaning the same number of unique people went to the donation page from both segments of the test. But, the conversion rate for the people who came from the control is higher. The donation page mentioned all of the outreaches, similar to the control emails. So people coming from the control would experience continuity of message. Whereas people coming from the test emails would then be presented with a donation page that lists all of the outreaches. Our hypothesis is that this created cognitive dissonance in the people in the test segment.

So if we test further, we would make donation pages specific to the outreach itself to take the test through the entire funnel.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift on a child’s future and emotional health will impact sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: That showing what the child’s life is and what life will be after the donors gifts, with all the emotions and impact of that gift, will increase child sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their child specific page, the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor this particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the increased traffic to these pages, there was no discernible difference in sponsorship. A 52% level of confidence is a veritable coin flip either way.
Our hypothesis is that copy alone is not enough to impact conversion one way or the other. Our next step is to test tactical page design test to keep folks on the child sponsorship page and make this the complete decision point. With the current design, users are presented the option to leave the page at several different points. In fact, twenty percent of the gifts that were made on the control were people who left this page to give a donation to the main PFI donation page.
Revenue did experience a 27.9% decrease with a 93% level of confidence. This is because more people were likely to give a one-time gift on the control.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift through feelings and outcomes impact conversion on a sponsorship page
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on the feeling and affects of the problem and showing the change that a donor’s gift will make will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their main sponsorship page, where the audience has the opportunity to see the different children who need a sponsor. It is important to note that this is not the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor a particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings: The outcome of this test showed that this communication tactic does not affect sponsorship conversion by itself. Our hypothesis is this is not a page where people make the decision to sponsor, but to see the different children that they could sponsor. Since this page is not a decision point, a treatment on this page will not have enough conversion to validate with this level of traffic. The next step is to focus on the child specific pages.

experiments.title: How do campaign emails focused on specific outreaches compare to general campaign emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on specific outreaches individually will increase the donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/29/2021
In preparation for KCBI’s Spring Campaign, we were curious if listing all of the outreaches the an organization is fundraising for clouds the impact and value proposition for each individual outreach. In other words, does talking about keeping radio on the air, saving lives with ultrasounds, and bringing Bibles to prisoners all in one email lessen the value prop and so the donor response? Would it be better to talk about each individual outreach in an email bring more depth the the value of the individual outreaches and so improve donor response. So we tested this theory by splitting their housefile into two groups. One group got 4 emails that talked through all of the outreaches in all four emails. The other group got 4 emails that focused on each outreach individually (i.e. one email was focused on ultrasounds, one email was focused on prison ministries).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcome of this test was inconclusive, but we have a few hypotheses as to why.

First, it is important to note that there was no significant increase in the click rate on the different emails. There was a 13% increase at a 66% Level of Confidence, so nothing concrete or indicative of higher motivation either way.

Our hypothesis is that there was not enough distinction in the messages. Many of the test emails replace a paragraph with outreach specific message. If we were to retest this, we would make the emails more focused on the outreaches and make them more central to the message.

However, as we looked in Google Analytics, we found the level of traffic to the donation page was the same for the test and the control. Meaning the same number of unique people went to the donation page from both segments of the test. But, the conversion rate for the people who came from the control is higher. The donation page mentioned all of the outreaches, similar to the control emails. So people coming from the control would experience continuity of message. Whereas people coming from the test emails would then be presented with a donation page that lists all of the outreaches. Our hypothesis is that this created cognitive dissonance in the people in the test segment.

So if we test further, we would make donation pages specific to the outreach itself to take the test through the entire funnel.

experiments.title: How a copy-heavy popup ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more value heavy ad for Top Nonprofits visitors will achieve more email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2020 - 12/20/2020
In this experiment, we were running a pop-up ad with a nonprofit marketing partner. For the sake of time, we whipped up version A with minimal copy and a very simple design.
But once the campaign launched, we wondered if running a very text-heavy ad would actually lead to emails since it communicated the value far more effectively.
We assumed we would get lower clicks, but we thought the clicks we would get would be more qualified – thus leading to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw the exact same amount of conversion from both ads. As predicted, version B had significant decrease in clicks (158 VS 67), but a much higher conversion rate for those that viewed the landing page (6.6% VS 15%).

In this case, the drop in clicks and increase in conversion rate meant that our overall acquisition was a wash.

This indicates to me that there may be a happy medium between these two ad variations. It would be worth testing a new ad treatment with some value prop copy to help qualify the click, but leave more to be desired to discover on the next page.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a premium offer impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2019 - 12/16/2019
For the month of December, a special premium was being offered with a membership purchase. When evaluating the landing page copy, we wanted to develop a treatment that would increase conversions to the membership page. The control jumped right into the details about the premium and membership without giving a clear motivation for considering a Club Membership. We expanded the value proposition with a paragraph to enhance the value-add before launching into the and details of the Club benefits and premium information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we only had a short window to direct traffic to the treatment landing page. That being said, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate fully, but we did find that mobile visitors had a 20.1% increase in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence. We'll continue to find opportunities to introduce similar language on other landing pages that have a longer life span and see if we can observe a valid lift on conversions in the future on other premium offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 60.6% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 23.6% decrease in outbound link click with an 90% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 131.4% increase in outbound link click with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How interrupting the donation process with a value proposition question affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a slide-out question shown on the donation form improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
Based upon the results of a previous experiment, we had learned that if we solicit a donor’s opinion while they are making their gift, it can actually improve conversion rate. Our previous test was visually similar to a gift designation question that is often seen on other organizations’ donation form but we framed in a donor-centric manner.
Our concern with this tactic is that by placing it inline with the rest of the donation form, it was adding an element of friction that may have adversely impacted a segment of the visitors that didn’t need the added value reinforcement. We decided to use the same question but move it into a window that slid out as the donor scrolled down the page. The hypothesis was that this would remove it from the donation form which would reduce the visual friction but would still have the same impact that was seen with the original test.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the form with the slide out question and our standard form that didn't ask the question at all. It would appear that the lift achieved with our previous test was, in part, due to how the question was positioned to the donor. By making it part of the donation form, it became a question that the donor had to answer before moving forward. This value that it reinforced the in donor's mind outweighed the added friction associated with the questions.

experiments.title: How adding a blog description above an article can lead to an increase in clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the article descriptions above the article image  for the highlight section in the newsletter will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2021
At the bottom of RTB’s weekly newsletter email, they would have a “highlights” section where they would link to other articles/blogs. Below the articles, they would have a paragraph that would go into detail about what each blog/article was. The hypothesis was if we move the detailed description of what each article was directly above the article it would then lead to more clicks.
This was set up as a 50/50 split and was sent to their entire newsletter subscription file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall this experiment was a wash. We saw a slight increase in clicks but this experiment was nowhere close to validating. While this experiment didn't validate we did see that adding the description above the articles didn't lead to a decrease in clicks so we are looking to test this again.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual those segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.

When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.

When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.

It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.

Because of this and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: How communal language on a campaign sticky bar affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on a sticky bar will lead to stronger donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/27/2021
During KCBI’s spring campaign, we wanted to test communal language on their campaign sticky bar. Their typical sticky bar language focused on the urgency of the need and the end of the campaign with a countdown sticky bar. We paired that urgency and countdown with language that focused on the common value proposition that brings the reader into the community of KCBI. This language matches much of the way they talk on air during their fundraisers and they talk to their constituents like family. So it was a natural fit for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language increased donations by 16% and revenue by 29%. And there were no demographics that experienced a valid decrease in conversion. Our hypothesis was correct that using communal language coupled with urgency would resonate with their audience. That is because the copy started by connecting the most important points of value for their readers, connecting those to the mission and the outcome of the campaign.

experiments.title: How does focusing on a specific ministry over the full suite of programs affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing in on one ministry that the donor will be supporting rather than all of the organizations outreach will allow for a deeper explanation of value prop as well as a simpler ask, and that will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2021 - 03/29/2021
Prison Fellowship International launched its first content offer, Where Love & Justice Meet. Since this was a new initiative for them and they didn’t have many learnings from the market yet, we wanted to test which of their ministries were more likely to convert a donor. They have a mix of programs—prison bible studies, sponsorships for children of prisoners, and restorative justice projects. So to find out what converts a donor better, we did a 50/50 redirect split toward two different donation pages—The control, a donation page that showed how the reader’s donation would support their many prison ministries and 2. The Treatment, a page solely focused on the ministry and impact of a their prison bible study called The Prisoners Journey. The goal was to see which inspired more gifts. Because the copy on the donation page for The Prisoner’s Journey bible study was geared toward a monthly recurring donation ask, we made that form a tabbed donation form to highlight the recurring giving option, which was another variation in the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a full month of testing, the results are inconclusive. Neither donation page outperformed the other in donor conversion at any level that was valid. Our hypothesis is that both pages had equally strong value prop and simply delving into one ministry is not enough to move a needle on an acquisition offer.
Another key metric we were aiming for was acquiring recurring donors. The TPJ page acquired three monthly donors in that time span and the general ministries donation page acquired 2, so no meaningful findings there either.
Our next step is to focus on the page specific to the Prisoners Journey and learn how we can use their Lamplighter's program for monthly donors to TPJ as a greater incentive to give a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How longer value proposition on an acquisition donation page increases revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Expanding value proposition on an acquisition donation page will increase revenue by increasing the perceived value of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights Institute had been running an offer for an eBook on Equal Rights for months. And the average gift was not as high as we believed it could be. So we decided to test including more copy on the donation page around the need for their gift and the impact it would make. The hypothesis was that this would lead them to give more.
This test ran for two months, longer than typically suggested, because we continued to see fluctuations in the results and wanted to give it a long time horizon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not result in valid findings. When looking at the different demographics. We found that it increased revenue with new visitors by 45% at a 87% LOC, and decreased the revenue from returning visitors by 24% at a LOC of 77%. The findings from these two segments of users negated the other when you view this test through all traffic.

Our hypothesis is that this test showed a directional lift in revenue for new visitors because it told them more about an organization that they are new to, and it taught them more about the value of the eBook they just received. But overall, this test showed that this copy alone was not enough to increase revenue significantly for a sustainable amount of time.

Despite the extra time, these findings were still not strong enough to roll out this test. We will be testing other avenues for increasing revenue.

experiments.title: How prioritizing personalization on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the first name of the connection for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2021 - 06/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute donation page is where the majority of their conversions happen. As people are reading journal pages, they are able to give to support the site of their friend.
This page had long had a “cause and effect” value proposition that had not been challenged in some time. In reviewing this copy, they noticed that the organization name came before the “familiar” name of the friend whose journal they were reading.
The team came up with two treatments—one that just reversed the order of the “familiar” and organization names, and then another that also made a more direct ask and included language about a “vital connecting point” to increase the strength of the case for support.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither treatment moved the needle. Both treatments had slight decreases in conversion rate that weren't going to validate, and slight increases in revenue (through larger average gifts) that weren't going to validate either.
With a 34-35% donor conversion rate, it might prove more fruitful to get more motivated traffic from upstream to funnel into this page.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition and removing elements of friction impact donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How the value proposition copy on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the value prop copy treatment that we validated on the Genesis course stand on its own in the Revelation course, or does it only work when coupled with the removal of the premium offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 04/06/2019
DTS validated a copy test on the Genesis donation page that increased donations by 248% (experiment id: 10582), but also included the removal of a high-dollar premium offer from the page. They wanted to test the separation of the copy from the premium removal, so they tested the same copy on the Revelation course donation page, while still including the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach statistical validity and only impacted conversion rate slightly. We are going to re-run this test with the removal of the premium to see if the same concept that worked on the Genesis course works here.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition on a specific gift handle can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the higher gift handle of $50 increase average gift or donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2020 - 08/13/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association wanted to find a way to increase the average gift for the donors that finished one of their pledges. It was hypothesized that one potential way to do that would be to increase the perceived impact of some of the higher dollar value gift amounts. To do that, they highlighted the $50 gift button which would have been a ~20% increase to average gift. This same gift handle was previously mentioned on the page prior to the ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month and a half, we did not observe a statistically significant change in average gift or conversion rate. One hypothesis behind this is that the value of the $50 was mentioned in the copy right before the form. As a result, this visual emphasis didn’t have the same level of impact it may have otherwise had.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 01/02/2017
Neither of the value propositions showed a statistically significant improvement over the other. With such a low instant donor conversion, is showing that the visitors were not highly motivated to give which makes finding the right language more difficult. The email offer is still receiving emails for a decent cost so we will try testing a new value proposition to improve donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: When launching the free eBook offer for The Jesus Agenda, we wanted to determine the right value proposition to use for the instant donor conversion opportunity. The initial thought was that the new subscribers may be motivated enough to make a gift to get the full version of the book. However, we also wanted to see if explaining the various aspects of Buckner’s ministry would increase the perceived value of the contribution enough to lift giving.

experiments.title: How an updated value proposition impacted conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on children instead of the whole family and that incorporates scripture increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2019 - 05/02/2019
The text used on Buckner’s main donation page was created over three years ago and used messaging and tactics that we had long ago tested out of in our acquisition efforts. In an effort to boost the conversion to the page, we decided to test out an updated value proposition that incorporated scripture and focused more on the tangible impact to the children that Buckner serves instead of the holistic care being provided to all individuals.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant change in donor conversion from the treatment version of the donation page. It is possible that we did not identify the right value proposition for this audience. However, it is our hypothesis that it is likely the form itself that is causing a donor conversion rate lower than we would prefer. The next step in testing this hypothesis is setting up a new experiment that reduces the friction and anxiety associated with the form.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Nonprofit News Sources]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting an aggregate audience including follower of the Nonprofit Times and Chronicle of Philanthropy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach validity based on conversion. The control ad was turned off due to a significantly higher cost-per-registration in the treatment.
Neither the control or treatment was appearing to approach validity, and so it appears that adding bullets into the copy had no significant effect. Although, a low statistical power implies that we may not have had a large enough sample size to detect an effect if there indeed was one to be detected.
In any case, it is possible that the audience by nature is more prone to reading with closer scrutiny given that they are followers of nonprofit industry news sources. If this is the case, they would have reached the same understanding of the value propositon, regardless of whether or not they saw the control or the treatment.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects revenue on a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page will increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/08/2016
In response to Hillary Clinton’s plans to repeal the Hyde Amendment, SBA List created a petition in support of the Hyde Amendment. Traffic was pushed to the petition page through email and Facebook advertising. Once someone signed the petition they were taken to a thank you page with a donation ask.
SBA List wanted to do a radical redesign of the donation page to try and increase revenue. The control was a page layout they had been using previously. For the treatment, they tested a one column approach with a new headline and body copy that reinforced and clarified the value proposition. They also presented fewer giving arrays and a new call-to-action on the button. They did an A/B split test to determine which page would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings: They were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the test. Though the initial data they were able to get did show the one column treatment performing better because of a higher average gift, it is not enough data to come to a conclusion with those results. A similar test will be done for a future campaign with a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How the addition of value proposition copy affected donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition copy increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2016 - 05/19/2016
Susan B. Anthony list wanted to try and increase donations on their main donation page. In efforts to do this, they developed a treatment of the existing page. The existing page had minimal value proposition copy on it. For the treatment, they rewrote the existing copy, added value proposition language and a new headline and call-to-action that reinforced that value proposition. They split the traffic coming to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren’t able to validate the results for this test, the initial results tell us that the addition of the value proposition copy to the donation page actually caused a decrease in donations by 12.2%. The value proposition copy may have caused friction for those people who were already motivated enough to give once they came to the donation page.
The addition of the value proposition copy caused a decrease in donations for this test, but actually improved the second gift ask test we did on this same donation page. You can see that test here.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions on a donation page affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would motivate more donors to instantly convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/19/2016
In efforts to acquire names and email addresses, Susan B. Anthony List offered a free eBook called “Shutting Down Planned Parenthood”. After someone would request their free eBook they were taken to a confirmation page that had an immediate donation ask on it. SBA List wanted to test if they could improve instant donor conversion by testing two different value propositions. The value proposition for the control focused on getting the eBook in to the hands of more people and the treatment focused on advancing the pro-life movement and shutting down Planned Parenthood
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this test, the initial results told us that more people were motivated to donate to help put this resource into the hands of more people than they were to help advance the pro-life movement and shut down Planned Parenthood. The treatments value proposition may have seemed too big of a task for one person to do and accomplish through their donation. This could be why more people were motivated to convert with the control because it was more of a tangible thing that they could help do.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition affects clickthrough

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christian in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel.  With having two different value propositions and versions of emails, they tested them to see which would get a higher click-through.
They split-tested their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate these results, but what the initial data told us is that more people clicked through from the Israel outreach CTA than the control. This could be attributed to people being more motivated to give to a tangible need than a general value proposition and how the value proposition for the treatment was reinforced in the CTA.  Example: “Your gift will go immediately to direct, effective and unapologetic evangelism that is designed to present the Gospel in a way that Jewish people can relate to.”
In another test we did for this email, we found that the Israel outreach event increased donations by 47.3%. This audience is more motivated to give to a tangible event than a relevant value proposition for the organization. You can see the full details of that test here.

experiments.title: How an extended value proposition on a main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an extended value proposition increase donor conversion on a main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 12/21/2017
Live Action wanted to re-test an extended value proposition on their main donation page. Earlier tests had revealed that extended value proposition decreased conversion, but due to technical restrictions and errors on the donation platform, these results proved to be faulty and not reliable. Because of this, they ran a new test on the main donation page, testing a new headline and body copy that had a longer explanation of the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, as there was not enough traffic during the two months the test ran to get statistically sound data on a winning treatment. We recommend testing this approach in the future during a high-urgency campaign that will guarantee more traffic to the page in order to secure a valid learning on this experiment.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How shorter value proposition on a donation page during year-end impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a shorter value proposition increase donor conversion for email traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/04/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the donation page for the people coming to it from email. To do this, we developed a shorter value proposition that didn’t remove the strength of the message, but just made the copy/content shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this experiment, we did start to see a lift in conversion. Our hypothesis is that this traffic has already read and understands (through their email) the value proposition of the ask – they don’t need as much convincing and motivation to convert. The longer copy of the control may have even acted as friction in the giving process for these people.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the donation page can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we increase donor conversion by expanding the value proposition and telling them the premium they would get with their gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/31/2018
On the Boys Town website, they have a “You can help” page that get significant traffic and tells people the different ways they can help the organization. One of the primary ways is to make a gift. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test this donation feature by linking people to two different pages. The control page was a donation page that didn’t have a lot of value proposition copy on it and featured a banner with the primary message. This approach is more traditional to what Boys Town has done on their primary donation page. The treatment page did have extensive value proposition copy on it and featured the premium gift as well. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to impact donor conversion with this experiment. We hypothesize that because of the time of year that this experiment ran – year-end – motivation of donors was already high. It is difficult to impact donor conversion (positively or negatively) when motivation to give to an organization is already high. We propose testing this again when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
CaringBridge’s donation process had always had a separate page for mobile devices because of some legacy technology. Instead of loading a responsive page, the CaringBridge site used redirects to send mobile users to a separate page entirely. Over time, this page had not kept up with all the optimizations that had been implemented on the desktop page. CaringBridge decided to test the two pages against each other to see the cost and benefit of rolling both pages into one responsive page, which would keep them from having to keep two pages updated and in sync.
