The first major difference in the two pages was the gift array. This was tested independently, and had no impact on conversion or revenue. The next major change was the value proposition and layout of the form. The mobile page had always had a shorter value proposition because it was assumed that they had less “real estate” on the page to hold such copy. The team decided to test a mobile version of the exact same donation page that desktop users saw to see the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no change—and even a slight directional increase with the new copy. This gave CaringBridge the confidence to revert to a single, mobile-optimized donation page for their tribute funnel. Going forward, they will monitor desktop and mobile results independently to see how future experiments affect conversion on each.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition to the offer influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the application of Construal Level Theory increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2018 - 12/21/2018
For the instant donor conversion page on Buckner’s Advent Devotional, we wanted to test out a theory in conversion optimization known as Construal Level Theory. The theory states that an individual would be more likely to take an action that went against their own personal self interests if they felt it benefited a small group that they were a part of. In this situation, we were asking them to give a donation because they were part of a select group of people that wanted to protect vulnerable children this Christmas season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, we simply ran out of time before we could observe any significant influence of the treatment. We did not have enough visitors to the two variants to be able to measure the impact. Since this offer was only applicable during the Advent season, we had to turn if off two weeks into the experiment.
This is a theory worth retesting in the future when we have an offer that will see significant traffic volume or one that is being sent out to the email file.

experiments.title: How personalization and value proposition affect recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does personalization (and the presence of value proposition copy in the first place) affect conversion for recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2018
CaringBridge had recently reintroduced the recurring gift option in their tribute page donation process. While a previous test had showed that a single checkbox with value proposition copy beat a binary (monthly/one-time) option, they wanted to test the impact of personalizing the copy to the subject of the journal. While they were doing that, they wanted to also test a version that had no additional value proposition copy to establish a baseline conversion rate, since their first test was a radical redesign. They designed two treatments—one with personalized copy and one with just “Make this a monthly recurring donation” and launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there weren't enough conversions to validate any of the treatments, the final treatment with no value proposition copy showed a directional 40% decrease in conversion, with a growing level of confidence. This implies (though not statistically valid) that any amount of value proposition copy is better than no value proposition copy. There was no difference at all in conversion by adding personalization (which goes against the grain of previous testing), but also shows that we must work harder at this ask to increase results, as it is a secondary value proposition—asking the donor to "lift" a little more (with a monthly donation) when they are already making a lift as it is (with a single donation).

experiments.title: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more donations for the non-donor audience?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with expanding the value proposition within the body of the email it was sending to raise funds for their Rapid Response Team to minister to victims of the disaster. This was the second email in a longer series and was intended to make a direct ask for support. Our hypothesis was that many of the non-donors may not yet understand the reason to make a gift to BGEA and would require more explanation to help them toward a donation. To test this hypothesis, a second email was developed that explained more about the history and purpose of the Rapid Response Team.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a ~60% level of confidence, we learned that an expanded value proposition centered around the history of the Rapid Response Team did not motivate more non-donors to make a gift. However, we also learned that the longer copy did not really negatively impact donor conversion given the low level of significance.
This would indicate the potential for further testing of the expanded value proposition concept but using different reasons to make a gift. There is potential to focus on the increasing need that comes from multiple natural disasters that are coming in the near future or how this ministry fits into the overall mission of BGEA.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a shortened value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 09/13/2018
In a previous test for the primary donation page, we found that an expanded value proposition didn’t convert as many people into donors as a version of the page that had an banner image at the top with no additional value proposition. Since we have seen in hundreds of other experiments that value proposition can increase donor conversion and average gift, we wanted to continue to test this approach. We hypothesized that by shortening the value proposition we might be able to increase donor conversion since a large majority of the traffic coming to this page is mobile.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. That said, we did see a directional decrease with the treatment. This tells us that the banner image is still the best performing version of the primary donation page. The additional value proposition copy might actually be creating friction in the donation process because the people coming to the page are on mobile and are already highly motivated to give. Additional testing around the value proposition should be done to gain a better understanding of the donor’s motivation.

experiments.title: How a more organizational-centric value proposition impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Equip more parents like you with this resource
Invest in the lives of forgotten kids

experiments.researchQuestion: How would a different value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2018 - 10/08/2018
Boys Town offers an eBook on integrity. They promote the eBook through paid Facebook ads. It has a high appeal and they have been able to acquire new names really well with it. With the significant amount of people getting this eBook, that also means that they get a lot of traffic to the instant donation page that follow the eBook offer. The instant donor conversion on this page was low and they hypothesized that testing a different value proposition might increase donor conversion. They developed a treatment that focused more on the mission of Boys Town. It validated the parent reading the copy that they are a good parent for wanting to walk alongside their child and help them with their character. But then it talked about the kids at Boys Town who have been forgotten and aren’t as fortunate to have parents or loved ones who care about them. We tested this more organization-centric value proposition against the control that asked for people to make a gift so that other parents could be equipped with the resource that they just downloaded.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. The motivation of donors on this page is extremely low and the kind of value proposition didn’t impact donor conversion. We will need to do a very radical redesign of this page to impact donor conversion.

experiments.title: How simplifying and streamlining the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and creating an ordered “next steps” list on the instant donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2018 - 08/23/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an email acquisition campaign around the upcoming Supreme Court nominations. After visitors signed an open letter to the Senate, they were presented with an instant donor conversion page. We had seen relatively low donor conversion so we wanted to test out a bit of a radical change to the donation page.
Based upon experiments with similar organizations, we had found success in creating an ordered “next steps” list that walked visitors through what to expect and why a donation to AFP was so important. We decided to implement this and then remove some of the details of the full “plan” to reduce the friction associated with reading the whole page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two pages. After further analysis of the two value propositions, one aspect of the pages that remained the same was the focus on the “product-level” value proposition of the efforts behind the Kavanaugh confirmation hearings. It may be worth testing a value proposition that is not so narrowly focused and, instead, emphasizes the overall efforts of the organization. This will be the next step in our efforts to optimize this campaign.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition on a pledge donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 03/15/2018
Focus on the Family has a separate donation page from their primary page specifically for recurring giving. When looking at the copy on this page, we hypothesized that the copy led with a more transactional nature and that the messaging could be strengthened to better communicate the impact a person could have by becoming a recurring donor. We developed a treatment that reinforced the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for a month, we weren’t able to validate the results. This tells us that the expanded value proposition had no impact on conversion. Conversion rate on this page is very low. We will need to do a more radical redesign and address multiple friction points on the page if we want to decrease anxiety and increase motivation of people on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition about a matching gift affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does additional value prop about a matching gift increase clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
Buckner International was running their first June solicitation email regarding a matching gift and the program it affected. They wanted to see if adding additional value prop about the matching gift would motivate more people to click the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically significant and did not produce a significant directional lift. If this test were to be repeated, more elements would need to be changed, or copy would need to be changed more drastically in order to see a true difference.

experiments.title: How a product-level value proposition performs on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a product-level value proposition, focused on the Open Letter, increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/15/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an open letter to the US Senate in regards to the upcoming confirmation hearings for Judge Brett Kavanaugh. After every signature, visitors were taken to a donation page for the option of making an instant donation. Given the emphasis put on this acquisition effort, we wanted to identify the optimal value proposition that would lead to the best overall donor conversion.
With this in mind, we wanted to test two different value propositions. The control focused more about the overall goal of the campaign and all of the effort that was going to go into getting the Senate to confirm Judge Kavanaugh. The treatment version focused more on the Open Letter. The hypothesis was that since the visitor had just taken part in the Open Letter, they may be more likely to support a value proposition that centered around it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, after running for over a week, we did not see a significant improvement in the donor conversion. Given the time-sensitive nature of this campaign, we decided to turn off this campaign and move on to another hypothesis.
While we did not determine, with statistical significance, whether the product-level value proposition would outperform something that was a bit broader, it was unlikely that the product-level value proposition would outperform the control. Based upon that, we decided to move to the next hypothesis.

experiments.title: How value proposition copy affects clicks on a donate button in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the additional value proposition copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/19/2018
Leadership Institute sent a daily email called CampusWire, updating their audience about developments through their campus network. At the bottom of the email, they had a simple button that asked the reader to support CampusReform. While this did produce donors, they wondered if adding value proposition to the button would increase clickthrough rate and thus increase donation page traffic. They crafted a treatment and sent it to half of the file for several weeks to see if it would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After several weeks, there was no statistical difference between the two treatments. Clickthrough rate overall was low, since this is at the bottom of an email designed to get people to click through to the site, but it showed that the additional value proposition was not significantly increasing motivation—which suggests that readers were already motivated to give just through the content.
This experiment deserves further review to see if donation page conversion improved. It also suggests that fundraising might increase if this value proposition was broken out into its own email, away from the context.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects conversion on an “instant” donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will optimizing the value proposition on the donation page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/22/2018
Leadership Institute offered a free online video course, Conservatism 101 that they promoted through Facebook, hoping to use it to acquire prospects and turn them into donors. After users signed up, they were presented with a thank you page that asked for a donation. While this page had a conversion rate above 1%, which was the low side of the benchmark, they believed they could improve it through testing and optimization. They created a second donation page that took a more emotional tone.
The initial page went from the thank you message directly into tactical copy about how the course was delivered and why it was created. They reduced this “tactical” copy on the page in order to move more quickly into “why” the course was created, and the context of how it is currently taught on college campuses. The new copy tried to cast a vision for the course, and made a direct ask (in the form of a question) to fund it, finishing with a quantified ask of how $50 will help. Then, it preemptively thanked the donor before they made their gift. They launched an A/B test to determine which page delivered a better conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The second page produced a 97.5% lift, though the results were not statistically valid because of reduced media spend and low sample size. However, since a 75% level of confidence was reached, they felt the risk was mitigated to roll this out as the winner, increase the media spend, and develop a new treatment on top of this learning.

experiments.title: How adding an impact-focused value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/30/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Despite our best efforts, this experiment didn’t move the needle enough to be statistically valid. Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we should continue to test people’s motivation for providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well.

experiments.title: How an impact-focused value proposition on a donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move people to give more generously?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 06/02/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we did see a directional lift of 85% in average gift amount, which shows us that people are motivated by providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well. We should continue testing this type of value proposition in future messaging to see if this continues to be the case.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the Plugged In donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an expanded and strengthened value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/22/2018
On Focus on the Family’s Plugged In donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on that page by adding additional value proposition for why a person should give and what their donation would help accomplish. We developed a treatment that not only strengthened the value proposition, but also connected Plugged In with the parent brand, Focus on the Family. We included Plugged In as an evidential for why a person should support Focus on the Family.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for three weeks, we saw no significant difference in conversion with the treatment. In looking at the data from this experiment, a clear insight from it is that the conversion rate on the page is extremely low. The low conversion rate points to low motivation of the end user. Not even strengthening the value proposition were we able to impact the motivation of the end user. The next approach in testing this page needs to be focused on a more radical treatment to better understand the motivation and kind of people visiting this page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing friction on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing friction and adding value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2018 - 04/13/2018
The Missionary Oblates wanted to increase donations on their Mother’s Day premium gift pages. They wanted to test if streamlining the page by removing additional ways to leave the page (a back button) as well as adding value proposition to the copy would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased donor conversion rate by 32%. The motivation of people coming to this page is relatively high and it seems that the added value prop copy actually added friction to the conversion process rather than motivate more people to convert. Although this experiment wasn’t statistically valid, it would be helpful to try testing this again with only one changed variable, so we can nail down the exact variable that decreased conversion.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the call-to-action impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the impact of the gift instead of the challenge aspect increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
For their March appeal, Alliance Defending Freedom was sending a follow up email explaining the outcome of their recent Supreme Court case. As part of the appeal, they were also announcing a matching grant and using that as one of the primary reasons to give a gift. Since it was a “challenge” gift, much of the language around the call-to-action was about “accepting the challenge.” We had the hypothesis that by focusing on the value of the gift (ie. to protect lives and freedom) instead of the challenge, we could get more visitors to the website and more gifts to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant impact on overall gifts or clicks between the two treatments. It would appear that the value proposition of the various calls-to-action were not significantly different enough to see a measurable impact.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition on the primary donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding value proposition to the primary donation page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2018 - 02/27/2018
In an effort to increase donor conversion on the Focus on the Family primary donation page, we hypothesized that adding value proposition to it would increase donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we developed a value proposition for the page that communicated why a person should give to Focus and what a person’s gift would go towards. We then tested this treatment page against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for four weeks, we turned it off because we were seeing minimal impact at a low level of confidence. Because the main donation page has high motivation of people, the additional value proposition isn’t impacting conversion.
When we looked at conversion based on specific devices, we noticed on mobile that the updated value proposition had a negative conversion. We would hypothesize this is due to the additional length that came with the copy on the treatment.
We propose continuing to test the value proposition on the primary donation page, but decreasing the length of the copy on the treatment. This would help to validate the hypothesis on copy length and the impact a strong value proposition can have.

experiments.title: How expanding a sub-brand’s value proposition to include the broader ministry brand impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase conversion by expanding the value proposition on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 02/20/2018
On the Plugged In donation page we hypothesized that expanding the value proposition might help increase conversion on the page. We proposed testing two versions of the value proposition on the donation page; the current Plugged In donation page focuses on that particular sub-brand and the impact the program has. As a treatment, we developed a message around the broader Focus on the Family brand and value proposition. We then included language about Plugged In in that treatment copy but only as evidentials for how Focus is accomplishing the broader mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we weren’t able reach a significant level of confidence with the results (20%). We had trouble validating this experiment, not due to the sample size, but because the conversion rate on the page is so low. This tell us that the people coming to this page are not coming to give or are ready to give. No amount of value proposition will help increase the conversion rate on this specific page when we are asking for a gift. Instead, we need to be testing something higher in the conversion funnel. We propose testing a content offer on this page that then leads into a soft donation ask.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/23/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How a declarative value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a declarative value proposition produce an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2017 - 05/03/2017
Hillsdale College’s popular “Constitution 101” course had a donation page that had been through many iterative tests to optimize conversion. The control version (at the time) proposed a question to the reader:
We are often asked: Why does Hillsdale offer this course for free?
The copy then answered this question through three bulleted blurbs. Hillsdale’s team wondered if the rhetorical question-and-answer method might not properly motivate visitors to read through the copy. They developed a treatment that declared why Hillsdale taught the course for free, rather than proposing the question and answering it.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a slight lift, but was very far from statistical validity. This showed that there wasn’t any discernible difference at all between the two versions, and prompted more radical testing. It also might indicate that the motivation of the donor was high enough that the different styles of copy didn’t affect their decision to give one way or the other.

experiments.title: How the primary donation value proposition is impacted by relevance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the greatest donor conversion for Daily Signal readers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2016 - 12/15/2016
Our last test on The Daily Signal found that a value proposition that talked about the quality of journalism produced by TDS was the highest performing with regards to donor conversion. To continue that line of testing, we wanted to incorporate the relevant news of Donald Trump’s election as president and Heritage’s role in that transition.
With that in mind, we rolled out the Journalism value proposition as the winner and created a new treatment around the Donald Trump transition plan.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not produce a significant lift for either treatment. Our hypothesis is that these results are less about the value proposition itself and more about the tactic used to deliver the language. Including this snippet of text at the bottom of each article results in it not getting seen by a large proportion of the audience and it may not be standing out. Subsequent tests will focus on the tactic used to deliver the message.

experiments.title: How an ad’s targeted value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a journalism focused value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2017 - 05/08/2017
In an effort to boost the performance of the existing Dear Reader treatment, we wanted to try a modification of the existing value proposition. The current Control focused on the independent journalism aspect of the organization but also brought in some of the other work being done politically. Our hypothesis was that by focusing solely on the journalism aspect of the work and making the call-to-action softer, we could make the ask more relevant given the delivery mechanism of the test (the news site.)
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, we did not see any statistically significant increase in clicks or donor conversion of either treatment. As a result, we decided to move on from this test.

experiments.title: How refining the value proposition affected the follow-on HAFA gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the best conversion rate for follow-on Heritage Action gifts?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
The follow-on ask for Heritage Action that occurred on Heritage forms had become one of the largest sources of gifts for HAFA. In an effort to continue to improve upon the success of the page, we wanted to see if we could refine the stated value proposition. We set up a test across all donation forms so that we could test it with all completed transactions in order to reach validation quicker.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with a refined value proposition was not able to significantly impact the conversion rate one way or another. With an average conversion rate of ~24%, it would appear that we are going to have to take a more radical approach in order to improve the results.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a headline on a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused headline on a banner increase donations more than a match-focused headline on a banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that led with value prop headline first instead of match language would increase donations more than a match-focused headline on banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, likely because there was not enough difference in the variables tested against the control version to statistically affect conversion rate. The timing of the test was also during the end of their year-end campaign, when many people were already highly motivated to give once they got to the website.

experiments.title: How value proposition conveyed through headlines affects signups
experiments.researchQuestion: Headline’s emphasizing what will be gained by giving their email address will have a higher conversion rate than framing it as a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/21/2015
During the Christmas season, DTS creates a devotional series that runs between December 1st until Christmas. As a way to broaden the audience that receives the devotionals and collect new email addresses, we created a landing page to be used for Facebook advertising. We then created a new treatment to find the message the resonated most with the Facebook audience
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the minor difference in acquisition rate, it would appear the two treatments were not distinct enough to elicit an increase in response.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition delivers a higher donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 10/26/2015
DTS launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was name and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask. DTS wanted to test messaging on the donation page to determine which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask centered around teaching biblical truth.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for several months, with no clear winner. We determined that for this resource, there was no clearly superior value proposition. This resulted in the launch of a new, more radical test.

experiments.title: How the length of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 06/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course studying Genesis. After a new user signed up, they were presented with an opportunity to make a donation supporting the course and receive a companion book.
The value proposition on the page laid out a clear reason to support the course, but the team hypothesized that two changes might increase conversion. They changed the headline from “Thank you” to “Your enrollment in Genesis is confirmed” to entice more people to read the copy. Then, they shortened the body copy to attempt to increase the force of the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no valid lift or decrease in the conversion rate for the treatment. This tells us that DTS donors did not prefer one treatment over another, and that we must launch more radical experiments to increase the force of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 9.5% decrease in donor conversion rate although this was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to nondonors. In fact, this shows that there is practically no difference between the two. These results show that DTS must either take a more radical approach or generate more traffic in order to validate a test like this.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign for a donor segment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 23% decrease in donor conversion rate although this, like the nondonor test, was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to donors. This test was closer to validity than the nondonor test, but even when combining the data from the two tests, there was not a clear winner or statistical validity.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does added value proposition copy on a donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2017 - 07/10/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, they experienced a 10% lift in donations from the added treatment copy. This could be due to a more readable format (a bulleted list) for people to make a decision to donate, or perceived added value of their donation to the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International released a new offer giving parents a series of prayers to pray over their children. They wanted to test if adding additional value proposition and a bulleted list of what Buckner does to the donation page would increase donations. The form and other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How more concise value proposition affects email conversion rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/25/2017
Buckner International was running a test on an acquisition offer that offered free scriptures to pray for your kids. They wanted to test if a shortened, more concise value proposition on the landing page would increase conversion rate. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, and we saw only a minor decrease in conversion rate from the shortened copy. This could be tested further with a more radical test to figure out if people are deterred by length of copy, or more encouraged to download with a more built-out value proposition for the offer.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 11/06/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects visitor motivation on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/08/2016
In a recent experiment, we discovered a new value proposition that offered a significant lift to the email acquisition rate on the blog. In prior experiments, we had learned that blog traffic had differing motivations than those coming to the home page. As a result, we decided to test this new value proposition on the home page to see if it would have the same impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to email acquisition with the new treatment. This experiment is another reinforcement that the visitors to the blog have very different motivations than those visiting the home page. In this case, the visitors already had a better understanding of what ADF does so the “updates” and “latest news” did not require any additional validation or explanation.

experiments.title: How case-specific value proposition copy impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an newsletter signup offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted a test with their exit intent offer, asking: Will a more case-specific offer increase email acquisition? The results of that test were inconclusive but prompted a similar test on their ‘Jack Phillips’ information and email acquisition page. They asked: Will a case-specific newsletter offer increase email acquisition? They created two versions and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Again, Alliance Defending Freedom found that the results were inconclusive, since the test did not reach a statistical significance of 95% and a statistical power of 80%. We would naturally believe that aligning the newsletter offer with the related information on the page would increase email acquisition. And perhaps, it can if given enough time and traffic. However, this test cannot confirm that hypothesis, and thus, requires further testing to prove.

experiments.title: How low visitor motivation affected value proposition arguments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2015 - 07/03/2015
Heritage was running an email acquisition campaign using a petition against the government’s Common Core initiative. As part of the process, signers to the petition would be sent to a donation page following their signature.  The standard practice for these types of campaigns was to create a landing page thanking them for their signature and asking them to make a gift to Heritage based upon the high-level value proposition of Heritage.
We had a hypothesis that if we could focus the language on the page on how Heritage is specifically impacting this topic (Common Core), it would lead to a better conversion rate.  As a result, we created an experiment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: By the time the campaign was ended, we did not have enough conversions to be able to reach a statistically significant conclusion.  In this situation, there was a low motivation to give a gift for visitors coming to the page. As a result, neither of the value propositions tested were able to significantly impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How Imprimis’s value proposition affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will inspire the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2015 - 08/17/2015
The standard Imprimis donation page does not receive enough traffic each month to validate any tests. However, with the Imprimis survey that was sent out in August of 2015, we were able to drive enough visitors to possibly validate an experiment.
We created an experiment that tested two unique value propositions. The first is the current control which talks about the mission of the college and how Imprimis is connected to that. The new treatment utilized the cost metrics of Imprimis and framed it in terms of what it costs to send to both the donor and other like-minded people.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither treatment created enough of a lift to validate the experiment. This proved to us that both had equal appeal in the mind of the donor. We will continue forward with the existing control.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition impacted member renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2016 - 04/26/2016
For the member renewal campaign, we wanted to see if we could add some relevance to the appeal by talking about the upcoming challenges. We tested this expanded value proposition against the original control that had worked in the previous campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant difference in donor conversion or average gift. This would seem to imply that the two different value propositions were not different enough to have much of an impact on a donor’s likelihood to give.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: The impact of the messaging approach for the same value proposition of Imprimis
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors, advancing the principles of liberty or helping to distribute Imprimis to more Americans?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2015 - 04/30/2015
In this first experiment for the newly created Imprimis donation page, we tried to find a which approach to the core value proposition (advancing the principles of liberty) would be most appealing to the donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant different between either of the treatments.  In the end, both were promoting the core value that Imprimis embodies which is promoting the knowledge of American liberties. Both treatments approached it from different angles but in the end, the value propositions were not different enough to have a significant difference between the two.
